topics.title: Root Finding Methods: Bisection Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Root Finding Methods: Bisection Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **root** (or zero) of a function $f(x)$ is a value $x = \alpha$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$. In numerical computation, finding an exact analytical root is often impossible for polynomials of degree $\geq 5$ or transcendental equations, so we rely on **iterative numerical root-finding methods**.

The **Bisection Method** is a bracketing-type, root-finding algorithm based directly on the **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)**. Given a continuous function $f(x)$ on an interval $[a, b]$ where $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, the method repeatedly bisects the interval, retaining the sub-interval that contains the root, until the interval width becomes smaller than a prescribed tolerance $\varepsilon$ (epsilon).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bisection Method is the foundational bracketing technique. It is the *most reliable* (guaranteed convergence) but *slowest* (linear convergence) method. It MUST be mastered before studying Secant, Newton-Raphson, or False Position methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Rate:** The Bisection Method exhibits **linear convergence** with the constant $C = \frac{1}{2}$. Every iteration reduces the error by a factor of 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Bookshelf Search)

Imagine you are searching for a specific book in a long, organized bookshelf. You do not know the exact shelf number.

- You know the book lies somewhere between **Shelf 1** and **Shelf 100**.
- You go to the **middle shelf (Shelf 50)**. You check a hint ŌĆö the hint says "the book is in the right half."
- You discard the left half, so the new search range is **Shelf 51 to Shelf 100**.
- You go to the **middle of the new range (Shelf 75)**. Check the hint again.
- Repeat until the search range is just one shelf ŌĆö that is your answer.

This is precisely the Bisection Method. The "hints" are the function values $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ that tell us on which side of the midpoint the root lies.

## 1.3 Preconditions for Applying the Bisection Method

The function $f(x)$ MUST satisfy **two critical conditions** on $[a, b]$:

1. **Continuity:** $f(x)$ must be continuous on $[a, b]$.
2. **Sign Change:** $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (function values at endpoints must have **opposite signs**).

If either condition fails, the algorithm cannot guarantee the existence of a root in the interval.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Algorithm

The Bisection Method follows a deterministic, repeatable procedure:

1. **Initialization:** Start with an initial bracket $[a_0, b_0]$ such that $f(a_0) \cdot f(b_0) < 0$.
2. **Midpoint Computation:** Compute the midpoint $c_n = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$.
3. **Function Evaluation:** Evaluate $f(c_n)$.
4. **Bracket Update (Decision Logic):**
   - **Case 1:** If $f(c_n) = 0$ (or $\vert f(c_n) \vert < \varepsilon$), then $c_n$ is the root ŌĆö **STOP**.
   - **Case 2:** If $f(a_n) \cdot f(c_n) < 0$, the root lies in the **left sub-interval**. Set $b_{n+1} = c_n$, keep $a_{n+1} = a_n$.
   - **Case 3:** If $f(c_n) \cdot f(b_n) < 0$, the root lies in the **right sub-interval**. Set $a_{n+1} = c_n$, keep $b_{n+1} = b_n$.
5. **Convergence Test:** Stop if $\vert b_n - a_n \vert < \varepsilon$ (interval tolerance) OR after $N$ maximum iterations.
6. **Repeat** steps 2ŌĆō5 until convergence.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Midpoint | $c_n = \dfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$ | Bisects the bracket at iteration $n$ |
| Stopping Criterion 1 | $\vert b_n - a_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | Interval-width tolerance |
| Stopping Criterion 2 | $\vert f(c_n) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Function-value tolerance |
| Bracket Sign Test | $f(a_n) \cdot f(b_n) < 0$ | Root existence condition (IVT) |
| Error Bound After $n$ Iterations | $E_n \leq \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$ | Worst-case error is halved each step |
| Minimum Iterations Required | $n \geq \log_2 \!\left( \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon} \right)$ | Pre-compute iterations needed |
| Convergence Rate | Linear, $C = 0.5$ | Error ratio $E_{n+1} \approx 0.5 \cdot E_n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The formula $n \geq \log_2 \left( \frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon} \right)$ is a **favourite 3-mark question**. Memorize it. The base is **2** (not $e$ or 10).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Structural Engineering:** Finding the load at which a column buckles (solving nonlinear equilibrium equations).
- **Fluid Mechanics:** Determining flow rates where pressure drop satisfies the HagenŌĆōPoiseuille equation.
- **Electrical Networks:** Solving for currents/voltages in nonlinear circuits (diodes, transistors).
- **Heat Transfer:** Finding the steady-state temperature satisfying transcendental radiation equations.
- **Finance:** Computing the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** where NPV = 0.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Error Bound Formula

**Claim:** After $n$ iterations of the Bisection Method, the maximum possible error is bounded by $\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$.

**Proof by Induction (Sketch for KTU 14-Mark Answers):**

- **Base Case ($n=1$):** After 1 iteration, the root lies in $[a_1, b_1]$ of length $\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2}$. The midpoint is at most $\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2}$ away from the true root. Ō£ō
- **Inductive Step:** Assume after $n$ iterations, the interval length is $\frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$. The next midpoint $c_{n+1}$ lies in this interval, so its distance from the true root is at most half the interval length:

$$
E_{n+1} = \vert c_{n+1} - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}
$$

This completes the induction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** Find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 2 = 0$ using the Bisection Method in the interval $[1, 2]$ with stopping criterion $\vert b - a \vert < 0.01$.

### Step 0 ŌĆö Verify Initial Bracket

Compute $f(1)$ and $f(2)$:

$$
f(1) = (1)^3 - (1) - 2 = 1 - 1 - 2 = -2
$$

$$
f(2) = (2)^3 - (2) - 2 = 8 - 2 - 2 = 4
$$

Since $f(1) \cdot f(2) = (-2)(4) = -8 < 0$, a root exists in $[1, 2]$. Ō£ō

### Step 1 ŌĆö Iteration 1

Midpoint:

$$
c_1 = \frac{a_0 + b_0}{2} = \frac{1 + 2}{2} = 1.5
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.5) = (1.5)^3 - 1.5 - 2 = 3.375 - 1.5 - 2 = -0.125
$$

Decision: $f(1) \cdot f(1.5) = (-2)(-0.125) = +0.25 > 0$ ŌåÆ root lies in $[1.5, 2]$.

**New bracket:** $a_1 = 1.5$, $b_1 = 2.0$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Iteration 2

Midpoint:

$$
c_2 = \frac{1.5 + 2.0}{2} = 1.75
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.75) = (1.75)^3 - 1.75 - 2 = 5.359375 - 1.75 - 2 = 1.609375
$$

Decision: $f(1.75) \cdot f(2.0) = (1.609375)(4) > 0$ ŌåÆ root lies in $[1.5, 1.75]$.

**New bracket:** $a_2 = 1.5$, $b_2 = 1.75$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Iteration 3

Midpoint:

$$
c_3 = \frac{1.5 + 1.75}{2} = 1.625
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.625) = (1.625)^3 - 1.625 - 2 = 4.291016 - 1.625 - 2 = 0.666016
$$

Decision: $f(1.5) \cdot f(1.625) = (-0.125)(0.666016) < 0$ ŌåÆ root lies in $[1.5, 1.625]$.

**New bracket:** $a_3 = 1.5$, $b_3 = 1.625$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Iteration 4

Midpoint:

$$
c_4 = \frac{1.5 + 1.625}{2} = 1.5625
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.5625) = (1.5625)^3 - 1.5625 - 2 = 3.814697 - 1.5625 - 2 = 0.252197
$$

Decision: $f(1.5) \cdot f(1.5625) < 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[1.5, 1.5625]$.

**New bracket:** $a_4 = 1.5$, $b_4 = 1.5625$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Iteration 5

Midpoint:

$$
c_5 = \frac{1.5 + 1.5625}{2} = 1.53125
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.53125) = (1.53125)^3 - 1.53125 - 2 = 3.591614 - 1.53125 - 2 = 0.060364
$$

Decision: $f(1.5) \cdot f(1.53125) < 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[1.5, 1.53125]$.

**New bracket:** $a_5 = 1.5$, $b_5 = 1.53125$.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Iteration 6

Midpoint:

$$
c_6 = \frac{1.5 + 1.53125}{2} = 1.515625
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.515625) = (1.515625)^3 - 1.515625 - 2 = 3.482514 - 1.515625 - 2 = -0.033111
$$

Decision: $f(1.515625) \cdot f(1.53125) = (-0.033111)(0.060364) < 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[1.515625, 1.53125]$.

**New bracket:** $a_6 = 1.515625$, $b_6 = 1.53125$.

**Check stopping criterion:** $\vert b_6 - a_6 \vert = 0.015625$. This is **NOT** less than $0.01$, so continue.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Iteration 7

Midpoint:

$$
c_7 = \frac{1.515625 + 1.53125}{2} = 1.5234375
$$

Function value:

$$
f(1.5234375) = (1.5234375)^3 - 1.5234375 - 2 = 3.536683 - 1.5234375 - 2 \approx 0.013246
$$

Decision: $f(1.515625) \cdot f(1.5234375) < 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[1.515625, 1.5234375]$.

**Check stopping criterion:** $\vert b_7 - a_7 \vert = 0.0078125 < 0.01$. **STOP!** Ō£ō

## 3.3 Complete Iteration Matrix (KTU Board Format)

| Iteration $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n)$ | New Interval |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000000 | 2.000000 | 1.500000 | $-0.125000$ | $[1.5, 2.0]$ |
| 2 | 1.500000 | 2.000000 | 1.750000 | $+1.609375$ | $[1.5, 1.75]$ |
| 3 | 1.500000 | 1.750000 | 1.625000 | $+0.666016$ | $[1.5, 1.625]$ |
| 4 | 1.500000 | 1.625000 | 1.562500 | $+0.252197$ | $[1.5, 1.5625]$ |
| 5 | 1.500000 | 1.562500 | 1.531250 | $+0.060364$ | $[1.5, 1.53125]$ |
| 6 | 1.500000 | 1.531250 | 1.515625 | $-0.033111$ | $[1.515625, 1.53125]$ |
| 7 | 1.515625 | 1.531250 | 1.523438 | $+0.013246$ | $[1.515625, 1.5234375]$ |

**Final Approximate Root:** $\alpha \approx 1.5234$ (using $c_7$ as the best estimate).

The actual root of $x^3 - x - 2 = 0$ is $\alpha \approx 1.5214$, so our 7-iteration answer is accurate to $\pm 0.002$, which is well within the bracket tolerance of $0.01$.

## 3.4 Pre-computing Required Iterations (Alternate KTU Question)

**Problem:** How many iterations are needed to find the root in $[1, 2]$ with $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$?

Using the formula:

$$
n \geq \log_2 \!\left( \frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon} \right) = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2 - 1}{10^{-4}} \right) = \log_2 (10^4)
$$

$$
n \geq 4 \cdot \log_2 (10) = 4 \times 3.3219 \approx 13.288
$$

Since $n$ must be an integer, we take the ceiling: **$n = 14$ iterations**.

## 3.5 Python Implementation (Reference ŌĆö KTU 2024 Course Specific)

> [!NOTE]
> This course (PEMRT524) is **Numerical Computation Using Python**, so Python implementation is part of the syllabus. The following is a reference implementation.

```python
import math

def bisection(f, a, b, tol=1e-4, max_iter=100):
    """Find a root of f(x) in [a, b] using the Bisection Method."""
    if f(a) * f(b) >= 0:
        raise ValueError("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs (IVT fails).")
    
    print(f"{'n':>3} {'a':>12} {'b':>12} {'c':>12} {'f(c)':>14}")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    for n in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        c = (a + b) / 2.0
        fc = f(c)
        print(f"{n:>3} {a:>12.7f} {b:>12.7f} {c:>12.7f} {fc:>14.7f}")
        
        if abs(fc) < tol or (b - a) / 2 < tol:
            return c
        
        if f(a) * fc < 0:
            b = c
        else:
            a = c
    
    return c

# Example usage:
root = bisection(lambda x: x**3 - x - 2, 1.0, 2.0, tol=0.01, max_iter=20)
print(f"\nApproximate root: {root:.6f}")
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Bisection Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Input f, a, b, tol, max_iter/]
    B --> C{f(a) * f(b) < 0 ?}
    C -- No --> D[/Error: IVT fails/]
    D --> Z([Stop])
    C -- Yes --> E[Set n = 1]
    E --> F[Compute c = a + b / 2]
    F --> G[Compute fc = f c]
    G --> H{abs fc < tol OR n > max_iter ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[/Output c as root/]
    I --> Z
    H -- No --> J{f a * fc < 0 ?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Set b = c]
    J -- No --> L[Set a = c]
    K --> M[n = n + 1]
    L --> M
    M --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[User Input: f x] --> VAL[Bracketing Validator]
    IN2[User Input: a, b, tol] --> VAL
    VAL -- Invalid --> ERR[Error Handler: IVT Fail]
    VAL -- Valid --> MIDP[Midpoint Generator]
    MIDP --> FE[Function Evaluator]
    FE --> DEC[Decision Module]
    DEC -- fc approx 0 --> OUT[Output Root]
    DEC -- Sign Change Left --> UP1[Update b = c]
    DEC -- Sign Change Right --> UP2[Update a = c]
    UP1 --> CHK[Convergence Checker]
    UP2 --> CHK
    CHK -- Converged --> OUT
    CHK -- Not Converged --> MIDP
    OUT --> END([End])
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ŌĆö Per-Iteration Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant M as Main Loop
    participant F as f(x) Evaluator
    participant D as Decision Logic
    
    U->>M: Initialize a, b, tol
    loop Iteration n = 1, 2, 3, ...
        M->>F: Compute c = (a + b) / 2
        F-->>M: Return f(c)
        M->>D: Check f(a) * f(c) sign
        alt f(a) * f(c) < 0
            D-->>M: b = c (root in left half)
        else
            D-->>M: a = c (root in right half)
        end
        M->>M: Check |b - a| < tol
        alt Converged
            M-->>U: Return c as root
        else
            M->>M: Continue to next iteration
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowchart in **4.1** is the master algorithm. The block diagram in **4.2** decomposes it into functional modules (Validator, Generator, Evaluator, Decision, Checker). The sequence diagram in **4.3** shows the message-passing order between modules per iteration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. State the Intermediate Value Theorem and explain its role in the Bisection Method.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT):** If $f(x)$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $k$ is any value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = k$.

**Role in Bisection Method:**

1. IVT is the **mathematical foundation** of the Bisection Method. It guarantees that if $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists **at least one root** $\alpha \in (a, b)$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.
2. After every iteration, when we replace either $a$ or $b$ with the midpoint $c$, the IVT still applies to the new sub-interval, **guaranteeing continued convergence**.
3. IVT thus ensures the Bisection Method **cannot diverge** ŌĆö a property unique among root-finding methods.

*(Award: 1 mark for statement of IVT, 1 mark for continuity + sign condition, 1 mark for the guarantee of root existence.)*

---

### **Q2. Why is the Bisection Method guaranteed to converge, unlike Newton-Raphson?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Bisection Method is guaranteed to converge because:

1. It is a **bracketing method** ŌĆö the root is always trapped within the current interval $[a_n, b_n]$.
2. The interval length **shrinks monotonically**: $b_n - a_n = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.
3. The method relies only on **continuity** and **sign change**, not on derivatives, initial guesses, or function shape.

In contrast, Newton-Raphson:
- Requires the derivative $f'(x)$ (may not exist or may be zero).
- Can **diverge** if the initial guess is poor.
- Can fail at **inflection points** or **local extrema** where $f'(x) = 0$.

Hence Bisection is **robust but slow**; Newton-Raphson is **fast but fragile**.

*(Award: 1 mark each for the three points above.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (Option I) ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the error bound formula for the Bisection Method. Show that after $n$ iterations, the error $E_n \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Use the Bisection Method to find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ in the interval $[2, 3]$ up to 4 iterations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Derivation of the Error Bound:**

**Step 1:** At iteration $n = 0$, the root $\alpha$ lies in $[a_0, b_0]$, so the initial error satisfies:

$$
E_0 = \vert c_0 - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** The midpoint $c_0 = \frac{a_0 + b_0}{2}$ is at most half the interval length away from any point in $[a_0, b_0]$.

**Step 3 (Inductive Hypothesis):** Assume after $n$ iterations, $\alpha \in [a_n, b_n]$ and:

$$
E_n = \vert c_n - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}
$$

**Step 4 (Inductive Step):** At iteration $n+1$, the new interval $[a_{n+1}, b_{n+1}]$ has length:

$$
b_{n+1} - a_{n+1} = \frac{b_n - a_n}{2} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 5:** The new midpoint $c_{n+1} = \frac{a_{n+1} + b_{n+1}}{2}$ satisfies:

$$
E_{n+1} = \vert c_{n+1} - \alpha \vert \leq \frac{b_{n+1} - a_{n+1}}{2} = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 6:** As $n \to \infty$, $E_n \to 0$. Convergence is **guaranteed and linear**. $\blacksquare$

*(Award: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for induction, 2 marks for conclusion.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 0 ŌĆö Verify bracket:** $f(2) = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$, $f(3) = 27 - 6 - 5 = 16$. Since $-1 \cdot 16 < 0$, root in $[2, 3]$. Ō£ō

**Iteration 1:** $c_1 = 2.5$, $f(2.5) = 15.625 - 5 - 5 = 5.625 > 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[2, 2.5]$.

**Iteration 2:** $c_2 = 2.25$, $f(2.25) = 11.3906 - 4.5 - 5 = 1.8906 > 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[2, 2.25]$.

**Iteration 3:** $c_3 = 2.125$, $f(2.125) = 9.5957 - 4.25 - 5 = 0.3457 > 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[2, 2.125]$.

**Iteration 4:** $c_4 = 2.0625$, $f(2.0625) = 8.7732 - 4.125 - 5 = -0.3518 < 0$ ŌåÆ root in $[2.0625, 2.125]$.

| $n$ | $a_n$ | $b_n$ | $c_n$ | $f(c_n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 3.0 | 2.5000 | $+5.6250$ |
| 2 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 2.2500 | $+1.8906$ |
| 3 | 2.0 | 2.25 | 2.1250 | $+0.3457$ |
| 4 | 2.0625 | 2.125 | 2.0625 | $-0.3518$ |

**Final Approximation:** $\alpha \approx 2.125$ (after 4 iterations). The true root is $\alpha \approx 2.0946$.

*(Award: 1 mark for verification, 4 marks for iteration table, 2 marks for conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (Option II) ŌĆö 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the algorithm of the Bisection Method with a flowchart. State the conditions for its convergence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the smallest interval $[a, b]$ such that the equation $x^3 - 4x + 1 = 0$ has a root, then determine the number of iterations required to compute the root to an accuracy of $10^{-5}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Define $f(x)$ and obtain initial values $a$, $b$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
2. Compute $c = \frac{a + b}{2}$.
3. If $f(c) = 0$ or $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$, output $c$ and stop.
4. If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$; else set $a = c$.
5. Repeat from Step 2.

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4.1 ŌĆö same structure as the Mermaid flowchart).

**Convergence Conditions:**

1. $f(x)$ must be **continuous** on $[a, b]$.
2. $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (sign change).
3. The number of iterations must satisfy:

$$
n \geq \log_2 \!\left( \frac{b - a}{\varepsilon} \right)
$$

The method always converges if conditions (1) and (2) hold.

*(Award: 3 marks for algorithm, 2 marks for flowchart description, 2 marks for convergence conditions.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Locate the smallest bracket:**

Evaluate $f(x) = x^3 - 4x + 1$ at integer points:

$$
f(-2) = -8 + 8 + 1 = 1 > 0
$$

$$
f(-3) = -27 + 12 + 1 = -14 < 0
$$

So a root exists in $[-3, -2]$.

Test $f(-1) = -1 + 4 + 1 = 4 > 0$. Since $f(-2) \cdot f(-1) > 0$, no sign change here. Test $f(0) = 1 > 0$, $f(1) = -2 < 0$. Root in $[0, 1]$. Test $f(1) = -2 < 0$, $f(2) = 8 - 8 + 1 = 1 > 0$. Root in $[1, 2]$. Test $f(2) = 1 > 0$, $f(3) = 27 - 12 + 1 = 16 > 0$. No sign change.

**Roots are located in three intervals: $[-3, -2]$, $[0, 1]$, $[1, 2]$.**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Number of iterations for $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$:**

For the smallest bracket (length 1, e.g., $[0, 1]$):

$$
n \geq \log_2 \!\left( \frac{1 - 0}{10^{-5}} \right) = \log_2 (10^5) = 5 \log_2 10
$$

$$
n \geq 5 \times 3.32193 \approx 16.6096
$$

Take ceiling: **$n = 17$ iterations**.

*(Award: 4 marks for finding intervals, 2 marks for formula application, 1 mark for final answer.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the initial bracket.** Many students start iterating without checking $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Sign error in deciding the new interval.** The rule is: if $f(c)$ has the **same sign** as $f(a)$, then $b = c$. Many students get this backward. **Double-check signs at every iteration.**
> 3. **Wrong stopping criterion.** Use $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ (interval-based) OR $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$ (function-based). Do NOT mix up or use the wrong tolerance.
> 4. **Forgetting to show the iteration table.** A well-formatted table is worth **2ŌĆō3 marks** in the board exam. Always present a clean table with $a_n, b_n, c_n, f(c_n)$.
> 5. **Rounding errors.** Carry **at least 4ŌĆō5 decimal places** during intermediate steps. Do not round to 2 decimal places ŌĆö error accumulates and your final answer deviates.
> 6. **Confusing the log base.** The iteration formula uses $\log_2$, not $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$. Many students write $\log$ and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- [x] Bisection Method is a **bracketing, guaranteed-convergent** method with **linear convergence**.
- [x] Preconditions: $f(x)$ continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
- [x] Midpoint: $c_n = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$.
- [x] Bracket update: if $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$; else set $a = c$.
- [x] Stopping criteria: $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ **or** $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$.
- [x] Error bound: $E_n \leq \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$.
- [x] Iterations needed: $n \geq \log_2 \left( \frac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon} \right)$ (round up to next integer).
- [x] **Advantages:** guaranteed convergence, simple, no derivatives required.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** slow (linear), requires initial bracket with sign change.
- [x] Convergence rate constant: $C = \frac{1}{2}$ (i.e., $E_{n+1} = 0.5 \cdot E_n$).
- [x] Cannot find **complex roots** or roots where $f(a) \cdot f(b) > 0$ (no sign change).
- [x] For the syllabus course (PEMRT524), implementation is expected in **Python** using `def`, `if-else`, and a `for` or `while` loop.

> **End of Module 1 Notes ŌĆö Bisection Method**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton-Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Newton-Raphson Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton-Raphson Method (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The Newton-Raphson Method is an **iterative open-method** of root-finding in numerical analysis, used to successively refine approximations to the real roots of a real-valued function $f(x) = 0$. It is a first-order convergent scheme derived from the first two terms of the **Taylor Series Expansion** of $f(x)$ about an initial guess $x_0$, utilizing the local tangent line as a linear approximation to determine the next, improved estimate of the root.

**Key characteristics in KTU terminology:**
- **Category:** Open Method (does not require bracketing)
- **Order of Convergence:** Quadratic ($\alpha = 2$)
- **Required Information:** Function value $f(x)$ and its first derivative $f'(x)$
- **Type:** Single-point iteration (uses one previous approximation $x_n$ to generate $x_{n+1}$)
- **Stopping Criterion:** $\vert f(x_n) \vert < \epsilon$ or $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \epsilon$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Mountain Climber's Descent"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are a hiker stuck on a steep mountain at night (representing your initial guess $x_0$). You cannot see the valley (the root) at the bottom. However, you have a flashlight that points **downhill** (this flashlight represents the derivative $f'(x_0)$ ŌĆö the slope).
>
> The Newton-Raphson Method tells you to: **(1)** shine your flashlight at the ground to determine the **steepest downhill direction**, **(2)** walk exactly along that line until the ground becomes level, and **(3)** stop. The point where you stop is your new, better position $x_1$. You repeat this process ŌĆö flash, walk, stop ŌĆö and with each step, you get dramatically closer to the valley because you always know the exact slope of the terrain beneath your feet.
>
> If the terrain is smooth and you are not stuck on a flat plateau or a saddle point, you converge to the valley **extremely fast** ŌĆö each step roughly **doubles the number of correct decimal digits** (this is the essence of **quadratic convergence**).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Tolerance (KTU Exam convention):** $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$
- **Maximum Iterations (safety bound):** $N_{max} = 50$ (in most textbooks)
- **Convergence Rate Constant:** $C = \frac{\vert f''(x^*) \vert}{2 \cdot \vert f'(x^*) \vert}$ (used in error analysis)
- **Taylor Series Truncation:** First-order Taylor polynomial (linearization)
- **Unit:** Dimensionless (the method is purely algebraic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Newton-Raphson is taught as the **"flagship" open method** in Module 1 because it is the fastest-converging single-variable root-finder and is the foundation for higher-dimensional methods like Newton-Raphson for systems of nonlinear equations (covered in higher semesters).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The method is built upon the idea of **local linearization** of a nonlinear function $f(x)$ near an initial approximation $x_0$.

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Start with an Initial Guess:** Choose $x_0$ close to the true root $x^*$. The closer $x_0$ is, the faster the method converges.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Function and Derivative at $x_n$:** Evaluate $f(x_n)$ (the height of the curve above the x-axis) and $f'(x_n)$ (the slope of the tangent line at that point).

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the Tangent Line:** Write the equation of the tangent to $y = f(x)$ at the point $(x_n, f(x_n))$:
$$y - f(x_n) = f'(x_n) \cdot (x - x_n)$$

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Find the x-intercept of the Tangent:** The next approximation $x_{n+1}$ is the value of $x$ where this tangent line crosses the x-axis (i.e., where $y = 0$):
$$0 - f(x_n) = f'(x_n) \cdot (x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $x_{n+1}$ (The Newton-Raphson Update Formula):**
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

- **Step 6 ŌĆö Check Convergence:** If $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \epsilon$ or $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \epsilon$, STOP. Otherwise, set $x_n \leftarrow x_{n+1}$ and return to Step 2.

## 2.2 Conditions for Convergence (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sufficient Conditions for Convergence of Newton-Raphson:**
> 1. $f(x)$ must be **continuously differentiable** in an interval $I$ containing the root $x^*$.
> 2. $f'(x) \neq 0$ for all $x \in I$ (no stationary points near the root).
> 3. $f''(x)$ must exist and be **bounded** in $I$ (so the function is not too "curved").
> 4. The initial guess $x_0$ must be **sufficiently close** to the true root $x^*$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Use / Remarks** |
|:-----:|:----------------------|:--------------------------:|:------------------------------|
| 1 | Newton-Raphson Update Rule | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Core iteration formula; memorize verbatim. |
| 2 | Taylor Series Derivation (1st-order) | $f(x) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$ | Local linear approximation around $x_n$. |
| 3 | Order of Convergence | $\alpha = 2$ (Quadratic) | $e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^2$ |
| 4 | Error Propagation Equation | $e_{n+1} = \dfrac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} \cdot e_n^2$ | True quadratic convergence: error squared. |
| 5 | Stopping Criterion (Function) | $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Used when function value is meaningful. |
| 6 | Stopping Criterion (Increment) | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Most commonly used in KTU exams. |
| 7 | Stopping Criterion (Relative Error) | $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} \leq \epsilon$ | Used when root magnitude is large. |
| 8 | Convergence Failure Condition | $f'(x_n) = 0$ | Tangent is horizontal; method blows up. |
| 9 | Failure at Inflection Point | $f'(x_n) = 0 \text{ and } f(x_n) \neq 0$ | No valid tangent intercept exists. |
| 10 | Geometric Interpretation | Tangent at $(x_n, f(x_n))$ meets x-axis | $x_{n+1}$ is the intercept. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value and is rendered using standard LaTeX; avoid raw pipes inside markdown table cells to prevent rendering issues.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Engineering:** Solving the *power flow equation* $P = V^2 / R$ for voltage $V$ during load studies.
- **Structural Analysis:** Finding critical buckling loads where the stiffness matrix becomes singular.
- **Fluid Mechanics:** Solving the Colebrook-White equation for friction factor in pipe flow (open method is essential since the equation is transcendental).
- **Heat Transfer:** Computing Biot number and Fourier number from transcendental equations during transient conduction.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Phase-equilibrium calculations using Antoine and Peng-Robinson equations.
- **Computational Finance:** Implied volatility extraction in option pricing (Black-Scholes inversion).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Newton-Raphson Formula

### Starting Point: The Taylor Series Expansion

Let $f(x)$ be a real-valued function that is infinitely differentiable in a neighborhood of the point $x_n$. We expand $f(x)$ around $x = x_n$ using the **Taylor Series** (infinite series representation):

$$f(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) + \frac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x - x_n)^2 + \frac{f'''(x_n)}{3!}(x - x_n)^3 + \cdots$$

**Truncation to First Order:** Since Newton-Raphson is a **first-order** method, we discard all terms of order $(x - x_n)^2$ and higher. This gives a **local linear approximation**:

$$f(x) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

**Setting $f(x) = 0$ to find the root:** We are interested in the value of $x$ where the function crosses zero. Substituting $f(x) = 0$:

$$0 \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

**Solving for $x$:** This is the equation of a straight line. We want the x-intercept of this line, which is our improved estimate $x_{n+1}$:

$$0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

$$-f(x_n) = f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

$$x_{n+1} - x_n = -\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

$$\boxed{x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}}$$

This is the **Newton-Raphson update formula**, valid as long as $f'(x_n) \neq 0$.

### Derivation of the Quadratic Convergence Rate

Let $e_n = x_n - x^*$ denote the error at the $n$-th iteration. Applying Taylor's theorem to $f(x^*)$ around $x_n$:

$$f(x^*) = f(x_n - e_n) = f(x_n) - f'(x_n) e_n + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2} e_n^2$$

where $\xi$ lies between $x_n$ and $x^*$. Since $x^*$ is a root, $f(x^*) = 0$, so:

$$0 = f(x_n) - f'(x_n) e_n + \frac{f''(\xi)}{2} e_n^2$$

Dividing throughout by $f'(x_n)$:

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = e_n - \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)} e_n^2$$

Now, from the Newton-Raphson update rule:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} \implies e_{n+1} = e_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

Substituting the expression above:

$$e_{n+1} = e_n - \left[ e_n - \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)} e_n^2 \right]$$

$$e_{n+1} = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)} e_n^2$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\xi \to x^*$, so we obtain the **asymptotic error relation**:

$$\boxed{e_{n+1} \approx \frac{f''(x^*)}{2 f'(x^*)} \cdot e_n^2}$$

This confirms **quadratic convergence** ($\alpha = 2$): the error is proportional to the *square* of the previous error, meaning the number of correct decimal digits roughly **doubles per iteration**.

## 3.2 Complete Worked Example (Classic KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Find a real root of the equation $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0$ using Newton-Raphson Method, starting with $x_0 = 2$. Perform iterations until $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < 10^{-5}$.

**Step 1: Compute the Derivative (Mandatory)**
$$f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5 \implies f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$$

**Step 2: Set Up the Iteration Formula**
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{x_n^3 - 2x_n - 5}{3x_n^2 - 2}$$

**Step 3: Tabulate the Iterations**

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:--------------------------:|
| 0 | 2.000000 | $8 - 4 - 5 = -1.000000$ | $12 - 2 = 10.000000$ | $2.100000$ | $0.100000$ |
| 1 | 2.100000 | $9.2610 - 4.2000 - 5 = 0.061000$ | $13.2300 - 2 = 11.230000$ | $2.094569$ | $0.005431$ |
| 2 | 2.094569 | $9.1885 - 4.1891 - 5 = -0.000620$ | $13.1605 - 2 = 11.160494$ | $2.094551$ | $0.000018$ |
| 3 | 2.094551 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | $< 10^{-5}$ Ō£ō |

**Step 4: Detailed Arithmetic for Iteration 1**
- $f(2.1) = (2.1)^3 - 2(2.1) - 5 = 9.2610 - 4.2000 - 5.0000 = 0.0610$
- $f'(2.1) = 3(2.1)^2 - 2 = 3(4.41) - 2 = 13.2300 - 2.0000 = 11.2300$
- $x_2 = 2.1 - \dfrac{0.0610}{11.2300} = 2.1 - 0.0054309 \approx 2.0945691$

**Step 5: Conclude the Result**
The root is $x \approx 2.09455$ (correct to 5 decimal places). Notice that the number of correct digits jumped from **1 ŌåÆ 2 ŌåÆ 5** in just three iterations, demonstrating the **quadratic convergence** property.

## 3.3 Python Implementation (as per PEMRT524 syllabus)

```python
# Newton-Raphson Method ŌĆö PEMRT524 Module 1
import math

def newton_raphson(f, df, x0, tol=1e-6, max_iter=50):
    """
    Find a root of f(x) = 0 using Newton-Raphson.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    f   : callable  -> function f(x)
    df  : callable  -> derivative f'(x)
    x0  : float     -> initial guess
    tol : float     -> convergence tolerance
    max_iter: int   -> maximum iterations
    
    Returns
    -------
    root : float    -> approximated root
    iters: int      -> iterations used
    """
    print(f"{'n':>3} | {'x_n':>15} | {'f(x_n)':>15} | {'|x_{n+1}-x_n|':>15}")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    xn = x0
    for n in range(max_iter):
        fxn = f(xn)
        dfxn = df(xn)
        
        if dfxn == 0:
            raise ZeroDivisionError(f"Derivative zero at x = {xn}. Method fails.")
        
        xn1 = xn - fxn / dfxn
        err = abs(xn1 - xn)
        print(f"{n:>3} | {xn:>15.8f} | {fxn:>15.8f} | {err:>15.8f}")
        
        if err < tol:
            return xn1, n + 1
        xn = xn1
    
    return xn, max_iter

# ----- Example: x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0 -----
f  = lambda x: x**3 - 2*x - 5
df = lambda x: 3*x**2 - 2

root, iterations = newton_raphson(f, df, x0=2.0, tol=1e-6)
print(f"\nRoot = {root:.8f}  (found in {iterations} iterations)")
```

**Expected Output (truncated):**
```
  n |             x_n |          f(x_n) |   |x_{n+1}-x_n|
------------------------------------------------------------
  0 |       2.00000000 |     -1.00000000 |       0.10000000
  1 |       2.10000000 |      0.06100000 |       0.00543099
  2 |       2.09456901 |     -0.00061996 |       0.00001821
  3 |       2.09455079 |     -0.00000008 |       0.00000000

Root = 2.09455081  (found in 3 iterations)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Flow of the Newton-Raphson Iteration

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the geometric flow: starting from an initial guess $x_0$, the tangent line is constructed, its x-intercept becomes the next approximation, and the cycle repeats.

```mermaid

    B --> C{"Is f prime xn equal to 0?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["STOP: Method Fails (Horizontal Tangent)"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Compute x n+1 = xn minus f xn divided by f prime xn"]
    E --> F{"Is absolute value of x n+1 minus xn less than epsilon?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["STOP: Root Found x n+1"]
    F -- "No" --> H{"Iteration Count exceeds Nmax?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["STOP: Maximum Iterations Reached"]
    H -- "No" --> J["Update: xn = x n+1"]
    J --> B
```
