Use it to estimate the number of iterations required to reduce the initial error from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ if $k = \vert g'(p) \vert = 0.6$. **Model Solution:** **(a) Solution [7 marks]:** We use $g(x) = \cos(x) \cdot e^{-x}$ with $x_0 = 0.5$. Compute $g'(x) = -e^{-x}(\sin x + \cos x)$. At the root $p \approx 0.3$: $\vert g'(p) \vert \approx 0.5$ ŌåÆ convergent. | $n$ | $x_n$ | $x_{n+1} = \cos(x_n) \cdot e^{-x_n}$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.500000 | $\cos(0.5) \cdot e^{-0.5} = 0.877583 \cdot 0.606531 = 0.532281$ | 0.032281 | | 1 | 0.532281 | $\cos(0.532281) \cdot e^{-0.532281} = 0.862460 \cdot 0.587178 = 0.506391$ | 0.025890 | | 2 | 0.506391 | $\cos(0.506391) \cdot e^{-0.506391} = 0.873849 \cdot 0.602715 = 0.526680$ | 0.020289 | | 3 | 0.526680 | $\cos(0.526680) \cdot e^{-0.526680} = 0.864835 \cdot 0.590498 = 0.510683$ | 0.015997 | | 4 | 0.510683 | $\cos(0.510683) \cdot e^{-0.510683} = 0.872070 \cdot 0.600183 = 0.523411$ | 0.012728 | | 5 | 0.523411 | $\cos(0.523411) \cdot e^{-0.523411} = 0.866206 \cdot 0.592672 = 0.513305$ | 0.010106 | After 5 iterations: $x_5 \approx 0.5133$. The true root is $p \approx 0.5177$, so error $\approx 0.0044$. **(b) Solution [7 marks]:** Starting from $\vert x_{n+1} - p \vert \leq k \cdot \vert x_n - p \vert$ and applying recursively: $$ \vert x_n - p \vert \leq k^n \cdot \vert x_0 - p \vert $$ We want $k^n \cdot \vert x_0 - p \vert \leq \varepsilon$, so: $$ k^n \leq \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert x_0 - p \vert} $$ Taking logarithms: $$ n \ln k \leq \ln\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\vert x_0 - p \vert}\right) $$ Since $\ln k < 0$ for $0 < k < 1$, we flip the inequality: $$ n \geq \frac{\ln(\varepsilon / \vert x_0 - p \vert)}{\ln k} $$ Substituting $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$, $\vert x_0 - p \vert = 10^{-1}$, $k = 0.6$: $$ n \geq \frac{\ln(10^{-5} / 10^{-1})}{\ln(0.6)} = \frac{\ln(10^{-4})}{\ln(0.6)} = \frac{-4 \ln(10)}{\ln(0.6)} $$ Computing: $\ln(10) \approx 2.302585$, $\ln(0.6) \approx -0.510826$. $$ n \geq \frac{-4 \times 2.302585}{-0.510826} = \frac{-9.210340}{-0.510826} \approx 18.03 $$ Therefore $n \geq 19$ iterations are required. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Find a root of $x^3 - 6x + 4 = 0$ between $0$ and $1$ using Fixed-Point Iteration with $g(x) = \frac{x^3 + 4}{6}$. Start with $x_0 = 0.5$ and iterate until $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < 10^{-3}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain geometrically with a diagram why $|g'(p)| < 1$ ensures convergence and $|g'(p)| > 1$ causes divergence. Identify the type of convergence (linear/quadratic) for each case. **Model Solution:** **(a) Solution [7 marks]:** Check: $g'(x) = x^2/2$. At the root $p \approx 0.7321$: $g'(p) = 0.268 < 1$ ŌåÆ convergent. | $n$ | $x_n$ | $x_{n+1} = (x_n^3 + 4)/6$ | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.5000 | $(0.125 + 4)/6 = 0.6875$ | 0.1875 | | 1 | 0.6875 | $(0.3249 + 4)/6 = 0.7208$ | 0.0333 | | 2 | 0.7208 | $(0.3745 + 4)/6 = 0.7291$ | 0.0083 | | 3 | 0.7291 | $(0.3875 + 4)/6 = 0.7312$ | 0.0021 | | 4 | 0.7312 | $(0.3909 + 4)/6 = 0.7318$ | 0.0006 Ō£ō | Root: $p \approx 0.7318$ (true value $0.7321$, error $\approx 3 \times 10^{-4}$). **(b) Solution [7 marks]:** **Geometric Interpretation:** Plot $y = g(x)$ and $y = x$ on the same axes. The fixed point $p$ is their intersection. - **Case $|g'(p)| < 1$:** The slope of $y = g(x)$ at $p$ is *less steep* than $y = x$. The cobweb staircase spirals *inward* toward $p$. **Linear convergence** with rate $k = |g'(p)|$. - **Case $|g'(p)| > 1$:** The slope of $y = g(x)$ at $p$ is *steeper* than $y = x$. The cobweb staircase *spirals outward*, moving away from $p$. **Divergence**. - **Special case $g'(p) = 0$:** The curve $y = g(x)$ is tangent to the horizontal at $p$. The number of correct digits **doubles** each iteration. **Quadratic convergence** (as in Newton-Raphson, where $g(x) = x - f(x)/f'(x)$). **Convergence Type Summary:** | Condition on $g'(p)$ | Behavior | Order | |---|---|---| | $\vert g'(p) \vert = 0$ | Quadratic convergence | 2 | | $0 < \vert g'(p) \vert < 1$ | Linear convergence | 1 | | $\vert g'(p) \vert = 1$ | Marginal / no guaranteed convergence | ŌĆö | | $\vert g'(p) \vert > 1$ | Divergence | ŌĆö | --- ### Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize for KTU Board Exam ŌĆö Module 1, Fixed-Point Iteration** - **Definition:** A point $p$ is a fixed point of $g$ if $g(p) = p$. - **Iteration:** $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ is called *one-point iteration* or *successive substitution*. - **Convergence criterion:** $|g'(p)| < 1$ (strictly less than 1). - **Divergence criterion:** $|g'(p)| > 1$. - **Quadratic convergence:** Achieved when $g'(p) = 0$ (Newton-Raphson is the canonical example: $g(x) = x - f(x)/f'(x)$). - **Error bound (a priori):** $\vert p - x_n \vert \leq \dfrac{\vert g'(\xi) \vert}{1 - \vert g'(\xi) \vert} \vert x_n - x_{n-1} \vert$. - **Stopping criteria:** Either $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ (successive error) OR $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ (residual). - **Iteration count formula:** $n \geq \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon / E_0)}{\ln k}$ where $k = |g'(p)|$. - **Aitken's $\Delta^2$ acceleration:** $\hat{x}_n = x_n - \dfrac{(\Delta x_n)^2}{\Delta^2 x_n}$ boosts linear convergence dramatically. - **Choice of $g(x)$ is NOT unique** ŌĆö only the convergence condition matters. - **Trivial (but useless) form:** $g(x) = x - f(x)$ gives $g'(p) = 1$ ŌåÆ never converges. - **Banach Contraction Principle:** Guarantees uniqueness and global convergence under Lipschitz constant $k < 1$. - **Python implementation tip:** Wrap iteration in `for n in range(max_iter):` with `error = abs(x_next - x_prev)` and `if error < tol: break`. > **Final Mantra for KTU Valuation:** *State the theorem ŌåÆ verify $g'(p)$ ŌåÆ tabulate 4 columns ŌåÆ cross-check with Python ŌåÆ state the root with tolerance.*

topics.title: Convergence and Error Analysis of Root-Finding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convergence and Error Analysis of Root-Finding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Convergence in Root-Finding?

**Convergence** is the property of an iterative numerical method to produce a sequence of approximations $\{x_n\}$ that approach the true root $\alpha$ of the equation $f(x) = 0$ as the number of iterations $n \to \infty$. Formally, an iterative method **converges** if:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x_n = \alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the exact root such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.

## 1.2 What is Error Analysis?

**Error Analysis** is the systematic study of how the error between the computed approximation $x_n$ and the exact root $\alpha$ behaves as the iteration progresses. It quantifies **how fast** a method approaches the true answer and provides bounds on the residual $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ after $n$ iterations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The order of convergence ($p$) and the asymptotic error constant ($\mu$) are the two most frequently tested parameters in board examinations. Mastery of these directly contributes to scoring full marks in Module 1 derivations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Lighthouse and the Boat"

Imagine a boat in fog trying to reach a lighthouse (the true root). The boat can only estimate the lighthouse's direction by checking the brightness gradient (the function $f(x)$ and its derivative $f'(x)$).

- **Slow, steady method (Linear Convergence)**: The boat takes many small, equally proportional steps. Like the **Bisection Method**, it halves the search interval each time ŌĆö reliable but never glamorous.
- **Fast, accelerating method (Quadratic Convergence)**: The boat uses both position and gradient to leap closer ŌĆö like **Newton-Raphson**. Once near the lighthouse, the number of correct digits *doubles* every step.
- **In-between (Superlinear)**: The **Secant Method** doesn't use the derivative but approximates it ŌĆö it sits between linear and quadratic, with a "golden ratio" order of about **1.618**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ŌĆö Order of Convergence ($p$):**
> A sequence $\{x_n\}$ converging to $\alpha$ has **order of convergence $p$** if there exists a constant $\mu > 0$ such that:
> $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = \mu$$
> where $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ is the error at step $n$, and $\mu$ is the **asymptotic error constant**.

## 1.4 Types of Convergence (At a Glance)

| Order $p$ | Name | Speed | Example Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| $p = 1, \; \mu < 1$ | Linear | Constant ratio reduction | Bisection, Fixed-Point |
| $p = 1, \; \mu = 1$ | Sub-linear | Very slow | Unaccelerated iterative schemes |
| $1 < p < 2$ | Superlinear | Faster than linear | Secant Method ($p \approx 1.618$) |
| $p = 2$ | Quadratic | Digits double per step | Newton-Raphson |
| $p = 3$ | Cubic | Digits triple per step | Halley's Method |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Fundamental Error Metrics

Before analyzing convergence, KTU examiners expect students to distinguish the three standard error measures. Let $x_n$ be the current approximation and $x_{n-1}$ the previous one.

### 2.1.1 Absolute Error
$$E_{\text{abs}} = \vert x_n - x_{n-1} \vert$$
Measures the **magnitude** of change between successive iterates. A small absolute error means consecutive approximations are very close.

### 2.1.2 Relative Error
$$E_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\vert x_n - x_{n-1} \vert}{\vert x_n \vert}, \quad x_n \neq 0$$
A **dimensionless** measure useful when $x_n$ varies over large scales (e.g., engineering units).

### 2.1.3 True (Actual) Error
$$E_{\text{true}} = \vert \alpha - x_n \vert$$
Used in theoretical analysis only, since the true root $\alpha$ is usually unknown.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stopping Criterion (KTU Standard):** In practice, iterations halt when $E_{\text{abs}} < \varepsilon$ **OR** $E_{\text{rel}} < \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is a user-defined tolerance (commonly $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-8}$).

## 2.2 Order of Convergence ŌĆö Deep Dive

The **order of convergence** is the single most important metric in numerical analysis because it tells us **how many correct digits** are gained per iteration.

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Compute the error at step $n+1$ and step $n$.
- **Step 2:** Form the ratio $\frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p}$.
- **Step 3:** Take the limit as $n \to \infty$. If the limit exists and is a finite, non-zero constant $\mu$, then $p$ is the order.
- **Step 4:** The larger the $p$, the faster the method. A method with $p = 2$ is dramatically faster than $p = 1$ once $e_n$ is small.

### 2.2.2 Asymptotic Error Constant $\mu$

The constant $\mu$ is **not** a measure of speed on its own ŌĆö it is the *multiplier* in the asymptotic relation. For **Newton-Raphson**, $\mu = \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$, which is a local property of $f$ near the root.

## 2.3 Convergence Properties of Standard Methods

| Method | Order $p$ | Asymptotic Constant $\mu$ | Convergence Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bisection** | $p = 1$ | $\mu = 0.5$ (effective) | Linear |
| **Fixed-Point Iteration** | $p = 1$ | $\mu = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert$ | Linear (if $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$) |
| **False Position (Regula Falsi)** | $p = 1$ | $\mu = 1 - \tfrac{f(b)}{f(\alpha)}$ | Linear (often stalled on one side) |
| **Secant Method** | $p = \tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ | $\mu = \left(\tfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2\vert f'(\alpha) \vert}\right)^{(p-1)/p}$ | Superlinear (Golden Ratio Order) |
| **Newton-Raphson** | $p = 2$ | $\mu = \tfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ | Quadratic |
| **Halley's Method** | $p = 3$ | $\mu = \tfrac{\vert f'''(\alpha) \vert}{6 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$ | Cubic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In structural engineering, $f(x) = 0$ might represent the equilibrium condition for a load-bearing beam. A **quadratically convergent** method like Newton-Raphson is preferred for **real-time finite element solvers** because it converges in only 3ŌĆō5 iterations to 12-digit accuracy ŌĆö saving millions of floating-point operations in large-scale simulations.

## 2.4 Bisection Convergence Theorem (Rigorous Bound)

For the Bisection Method on $[a, b]$ with $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, the error after $n$ iterations satisfies:

$$\vert e_n \vert = \vert \alpha - x_n \vert \leq \frac{b - a}{2^{n+1}}$$

This is an **a priori** bound ŌĆö it guarantees accuracy *before* running the algorithm. The number of iterations required to achieve tolerance $\varepsilon$ is:

$$n \geq \frac{\log\!\left(\frac{b - a}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2} - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bisection is "Reliable but Slow":** The bound above guarantees *convergence* but the error decreases only by a factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ each step. To get 10 decimal places, we need roughly $n \approx 33$ iterations, regardless of the function's complexity.

## 2.5 Fixed-Point Convergence Theorem

Given $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$, with $g$ continuous and differentiable near $\alpha$, the iteration converges if:

$$\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$$

and the error follows the linear relation:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} = \vert g'(\alpha) \vert = \mu$$

This is the foundation for understanding **why** Newton-Raphson works: it is a fixed-point iteration with $g(x) = x - \frac{f(x)}{f'(x)}$, giving $g'(\alpha) = 0$ and hence *superior* convergence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Order of Convergence of Newton-Raphson

The Newton-Raphson iteration formula is:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the error recurrence.**
Let $e_n = \alpha - x_n$, so $x_n = \alpha - e_n$. Substitute into the iteration:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Taylor expand $f(x_n)$ around $\alpha$.**
By Taylor's theorem, since $f(\alpha) = 0$:

$$f(x_n) = f(\alpha - e_n) = f(\alpha) - e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) - \frac{e_n^3}{6} f'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

Simplifying (since $f(\alpha) = 0$):

$$f(x_n) = -e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) - \frac{e_n^3}{6} f'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Taylor expand $f'(x_n)$ around $\alpha$.**

$$f'(x_n) = f'(\alpha) - e_n f''(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f'''(\alpha) - \cdots$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the ratio $\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.**
Divide the series term-by-term. Factoring $f'(\alpha)$ from the denominator:

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \frac{-e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) - \cdots}{f'(\alpha)\left[1 - e_n \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + \cdots\right]}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the geometric series expansion** $\frac{1}{1 - u} = 1 + u + u^2 + \cdots$ with $u = e_n \tfrac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)}$:

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \left[-e_n + \frac{e_n^2}{2}\frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} - \cdots\right] \cdot \left[1 + e_n \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + \cdots\right]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Multiply and collect terms up to $e_n^2$.**

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = -e_n + \frac{e_n^2}{2}\frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + e_n^2 \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

Combining the $e_n^2$ coefficients:

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = -e_n + e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute back into the iteration formula.**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \left[-e_n + e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)\right]$$

Since $x_n = \alpha - e_n$:

$$x_{n+1} = \alpha - e_n + e_n - e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

The $-e_n + e_n$ cancels:

$$x_{n+1} = \alpha - e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Write the error equation.** Since $e_{n+1} = \alpha - x_{n+1}$:

$$e_{n+1} = \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n^2 + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Identify the order of convergence.**
Taking the limit:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^2} = \left\vert \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \right\vert = \mu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Newton-Raphson has **order of convergence $p = 2$ (quadratic)** with asymptotic error constant $\mu = \tfrac{3 \vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$. This means once $e_n$ is small (say $10^{-2}$), the next error is proportional to $10^{-4}$, and the one after to $10^{-8}$ ŌĆö digits *square* with each iteration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Order of Convergence of Secant Method

The Secant iteration is:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rewrite as a fixed-point-like form.** Let $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ and $e_{n-1} = \alpha - x_{n-1}$, so $x_n - x_{n-1} = e_{n-1} - e_n$.

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(e_{n-1} - e_n)}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Taylor-expand $f(x_{n-1})$ around $x_n$.**
Treating $x_{n-1} = x_n - (x_n - x_{n-1}) = x_n - h$ where $h = x_n - x_{n-1}$:

$$f(x_{n-1}) = f(x_n) - h f'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x_n) - \cdots$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the denominator.**

$$f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1}) = h f'(x_n) - \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x_n) + O(h^3)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})} = \frac{f(x_n)}{h f'(x_n)\left[1 - \frac{h f''(x_n)}{2 f'(x_n)} + \cdots\right]}$$

Using the geometric expansion:

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})} = \frac{f(x_n)}{h f'(x_n)} \left[1 + \frac{h f''(x_n)}{2 f'(x_n)} + \cdots\right]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $h = e_{n-1} - e_n$ and $x_n = \alpha - e_n$, then Taylor-expand $f$ around $\alpha$.**

$$f(x_n) = -e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) + O(e_n^3)$$

$$f'(x_n) = f'(\alpha) - e_n f''(\alpha) + O(e_n^2)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Assemble the error term.** After careful algebra (left as an exam exercise, but the structure is):

$$e_{n+1} \approx -\frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n \, e_{n-1} + O(\text{higher order})$$

This shows $e_{n+1} \propto e_n \cdot e_{n-1}$ ŌĆö a *two-term* recurrence, unlike Newton-Raphson's single-term $e_n^2$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Solve the recurrence using a power-law ansatz $e_n \approx C \cdot \lambda^{p^n}$.**
Plugging into $e_{n+1} = K e_n e_{n-1}$:

$$C \lambda^{p^{n+1}} = K C^2 \lambda^{p^n + p^{n-1}}$$

This gives $p^{n+1} = p^n + p^{n-1}$, i.e., dividing by $p^{n-1}$:

$$p^2 = p + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Ratio Appears!** The order of convergence of the Secant Method is the **Golden Ratio** $\phi = \tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. This is one of the most beautiful results in numerical analysis and a favorite KTU examiner question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bisection Error Bound

Starting with interval $[a, b]$ and midpoint $x_n = \tfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$, the next interval is half the previous:

$$b_{n+1} - a_{n+1} = \frac{b_n - a_n}{2}$$

By induction, after $n$ iterations:

$$b_n - a_n = \frac{b - a}{2^n}$$

Since $\alpha \in [a_n, b_n]$ and $x_n = \tfrac{a_n + b_n}{2}$, the maximum distance from $x_n$ to $\alpha$ is half the interval:

$$\vert e_n \vert = \vert \alpha - x_n \vert \leq \frac{b_n - a_n}{2} = \frac{b - a}{2^{n+1}}$$

Solving for $n$ given tolerance $\varepsilon$:

$$n \geq \log_2\!\left(\frac{b - a}{\varepsilon}\right) - 1$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write the bound as $\tfrac{b-a}{2^n}$ instead of $\tfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$. Remember: **the interval halves $n$ times, but the error bound is *half the interval***. Off-by-one errors here cost 1ŌĆō2 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Generic Iterative Root-Finding Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define f x and initial guess x0"] --> B["Initialize iteration counter n = 0"]
    B --> C["Choose stopping tolerance epsilon and max iterations Nmax"]
    C --> D{"n less than Nmax"}
    D -- No --> Z["Stop: Method did not converge"]
    D -- Yes --> E["Compute next approximation xn+1 using iteration formula"]
    E --> F["Compute absolute error Eabs = xn+1 - xn"]
    F --> G{"Eabs less than epsilon"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Accept xn+1 as root and report success"]
    G -- No --> I["Increment counter n = n + 1"]
    I --> J["Update xn = xn+1"]
    J --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Convergence Order Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bisection Method"] --> B["Order p = 1 Linear"]
    C["Fixed Point Iteration"] --> B
    D["False Position Method"] --> B
    E["Secant Method"] --> F["Order p = 1.618 Superlinear Golden Ratio"]
    G["Newton Raphson Method"] --> H["Order p = 2 Quadratic Digits Double"]
    I["Halley Method"] --> J["Order p = 3 Cubic Digits Triple"]
    B --> K["Slowest Convergent Family"]
    F --> L["Intermediate Convergent Family"]
    H --> M["Fast Convergent Family"]
    J --> N["Very Fast Convergent Family"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Convergence Order Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Obtain sequence of approximations xn-1, xn, xn+1"] --> B["Compute errors en-1, en, en+1 using known root alpha"]
    B --> C["Compute ratio r1 = en divided by en-1"]
    C --> D["Compute ratio r2 = en+1 divided by en squared"]
    D --> E{"r1 approaches constant mu and r2 diverges"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Conclusion: Linear convergence with p = 1"]
    E -- No --> G{"r2 approaches constant mu"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Conclusion: Quadratic convergence with p = 2"]
    G -- No --> I["Test r3 = en+1 divided by en cubed"]
    I --> J{"r3 approaches constant mu"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Conclusion: Cubic convergence with p = 3"]
    J -- No --> L["Test superlinear ratio using log log regression"]
    L --> M["Estimate p as limit of log en+1 divided by log en"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Error Decay Visualization (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Iteration n = 1: error 1e-1"] --> B["Iteration n = 2: error 5e-2 Linear"]
    A1["Iteration n = 1: error 1e-1"] --> B1["Iteration n = 2: error 1e-2 Secant"]
    A2["Iteration n = 1: error 1e-1"] --> B2["Iteration n = 2: error 1e-4 Newton"]
    B --> C["Iteration n = 3: error 2.5e-2"]
    B1 --> C1["Iteration n = 3: error 6.3e-3"]
    B2 --> C1
    C --> D["Iteration n = 10: error approx 1e-3"]
    B1 --> D1["Iteration n = 10: error approx 1e-8"]
    B2 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Starting from the same error $\vert e_1 \vert = 10^{-1}$:
> - **Linear** (Bisection): reaches $10^{-3}$ in ~10 steps.
> - **Superlinear** (Secant, $p = 1.618$): reaches $10^{-8}$ in ~10 steps.
> - **Quadratic** (Newton-Raphson, $p = 2$): also reaches $10^{-8}$ in ~5 steps.
>
> This visualizes *why* higher-order methods dominate in high-precision engineering solvers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set B, Q1.a]`
**Define the order of convergence of an iterative method. State the order of convergence of the Newton-Raphson method.**

**Model Answer:**

The order of convergence $p$ of a sequence $\{x_n\}$ converging to the root $\alpha$ is defined as the largest positive integer for which there exists a finite, non-zero asymptotic error constant $\mu$ satisfying:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} = \mu, \quad \mu > 0$$

where $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ is the error at the $n$-th iteration.

The **Newton-Raphson method** has **quadratic convergence**, i.e., $p = 2$, with asymptotic error constant $\mu = \tfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$. This is established by Taylor-expanding $f(x_n)$ and $f'(x_n)$ about $\alpha$, leading to the error recurrence $e_{n+1} \approx \tfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary, Q1.b]`
**Distinguish between linear and quadratic convergence. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Linear Convergence | Quadratic Convergence |
|---|---|---|
| Order $p$ | $p = 1$ | $p = 2$ |
| Error relation | $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx \mu \vert e_n \vert$ | $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx \mu \vert e_n \vert^2$ |
| Asymptotic constant | $0 < \mu < 1$ | $\mu$ is a local property of $f$ |
| Digits gained per step | Constant fraction | Number of correct digits *doubles* |
| Example | **Bisection Method** ($\mu = 0.5$ effective) | **Newton-Raphson Method** |

**Key Insight:** In linear convergence, error reduction is *proportional* to the current error. In quadratic convergence, the new error is *proportional to the square* of the old error ŌĆö so once $\vert e_n \vert < 1$, the method accelerates dramatically.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state assumptions** (e.g., "$f$ is twice continuously differentiable near $\alpha$") before applying Taylor expansions. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing hypotheses.
> 2. **Show ALL intermediate steps** in Taylor expansions. Skipping the geometric series step $\tfrac{1}{1-u} = 1 + u + u^2 + \cdots$ is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Use absolute value bars** (or $\vert \cdot \vert$) for $\mu$ ŌĆö never write a negative $\mu$ in the final answer.
> 4. **Mention the iteration formula** at the start of any convergence proof; this sets context and earns a "methodology" mark.
> 5. In **stopping criterion** questions, mention BOTH the absolute and relative error checks, and the maximum iteration safeguard.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main QP, Q2.a-b]`

**(a)** Derive the order of convergence of the **Newton-Raphson method** starting from the iteration formula. Show all steps of the Taylor expansion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Newton-Raphson to find the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$ near $x_0 = 2$ and demonstrate the quadratic convergence numerically by computing the ratio $\tfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^2}$ for two successive iterations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the iteration formula and assumptions.**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

Assume $f$ is twice continuously differentiable in a neighbourhood of the root $\alpha$ and $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the error.**

Let $e_n = \alpha - x_n$, so $x_n = \alpha - e_n$. The new error is $e_{n+1} = \alpha - x_{n+1}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Taylor expand $f(x_n)$ about $\alpha$.**

$$f(x_n) = f(\alpha) - e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) - \frac{e_n^3}{6} f'''(\alpha) + \cdots$$

Since $f(\alpha) = 0$:

$$f(x_n) = -e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Taylor expand $f'(x_n)$ about $\alpha$.**

$$f'(x_n) = f'(\alpha) - e_n f''(\alpha) + O(e_n^2)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the ratio $\tfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.**

$$\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \frac{-e_n f'(\alpha) + \frac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\alpha) + O(e_n^3)}{f'(\alpha)\left[1 - e_n \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^2)\right]}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the binomial series.**

$$= \left[-e_n + \frac{e_n^2}{2} \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)\right] \cdot \left[1 + e_n \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^2)\right]$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Multiply and collect up to $O(e_n^2)$.**

$$= -e_n + e_n^2 \left[\frac{1}{2}\frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)} + \frac{f''(\alpha)}{f'(\alpha)}\right] + O(e_n^3)$$

$$= -e_n + e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute back into the iteration.**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \left[-e_n + e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)\right]$$

$$= (\alpha - e_n) + e_n - e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

$$= \alpha - e_n^2 \cdot \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Identify the error recurrence.**

$$e_{n+1} = \alpha - x_{n+1} = \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n^2 + O(e_n^3)$$

**Step 10 ŌĆö State the conclusion.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^2} = \left\vert \frac{3 f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \right\vert = \mu$$

Hence the Newton-Raphson method has **quadratic convergence** with $p = 2$ and asymptotic constant $\mu = \tfrac{3 \vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

Given $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$, we have $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 2$.

**Iteration 1: $x_0 = 2$**
- $f(2) = 8 - 4 - 5 = -1$
- $f'(2) = 12 - 2 = 10$
- $x_1 = 2 - \tfrac{-1}{10} = 2.1$

**Iteration 2: $x_1 = 2.1$**
- $f(2.1) = 9.261 - 4.2 - 5 = 0.061$
- $f'(2.1) = 13.23 - 2 = 11.23$
- $x_2 = 2.1 - \tfrac{0.061}{11.23} \approx 2.09456812$

**Iteration 3: $x_2 = 2.09456812$**
- $f(2.09456812) \approx 9.1877 - 4.1891 - 5 = -0.0014$
- $f'(2.09456812) \approx 13.1612 - 2 = 11.1612$
- $x_3 \approx 2.09456812 - \tfrac{-0.0014}{11.1612} \approx 2.09455148$

**Numerical Verification of Quadratic Convergence:**

The exact root (from Cardano's formula) is $\alpha \approx 2.09455148$.

| $n$ | $x_n$ | $\vert e_n \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.00000000 | $9.4551 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 1 | 2.10000000 | $5.4485 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | 2.09456812 | $1.6630 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 3 | 2.09455148 | $\approx 10^{-10}$ |

**Compute the ratio $\tfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^2}$:**

For $n = 1$: $\dfrac{\vert e_2 \vert}{\vert e_1 \vert^2} = \dfrac{1.6630 \times 10^{-5}}{(5.4485 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \dfrac{1.6630 \times 10^{-5}}{2.9686 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.560$

For $n = 2$: $\dfrac{\vert e_3 \vert}{\vert e_2 \vert^2} = \dfrac{10^{-10}}{(1.6630 \times 10^{-5})^2} = \dfrac{10^{-10}}{2.7656 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 0.362$

**Theoretical $\mu$ check:**
$f''(\alpha) = 6\alpha \approx 12.567$, $f'(\alpha) = 3\alpha^2 - 2 \approx 11.161$.
$\mu_{\text{theory}} = \tfrac{3 \cdot 12.567}{2 \cdot 11.161} \approx 1.69$. (The ratio approaches this as $n \to \infty$; finite-$n$ values are smaller because higher-order terms still contribute.)

**Conclusion:** The ratio stabilizes to a non-zero constant $\mu$ when dividing by $\vert e_n \vert^2$, confirming $p = 2$ (quadratic convergence). $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary, Q3.a-b]`

**(a)** Prove that the **Bisection Method** converges linearly with order $p = 1$, and derive the *a priori* error bound after $n$ iterations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the **Secant Method** has superlinear convergence with order $p = \tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$. Outline the key steps of the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup.** Let $[a, b]$ be the initial bracketing interval with $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ and $f$ continuous on $[a, b]$. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, a root $\alpha$ exists in $(a, b)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the iteration.** At step $n$, the midpoint is:

$$x_n = \frac{a_n + b_n}{2}$$

If $f(a_n) \cdot f(x_n) \leq 0$, set $a_{n+1} = a_n$, $b_{n+1} = x_n$. Otherwise, $a_{n+1} = x_n$, $b_{n+1} = b_n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Bound the interval length.** At each step, the interval is halved:

$$b_{n+1} - a_{n+1} = \frac{b_n - a_n}{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Inductive formula.** By induction on $n$:

$$b_n - a_n = \frac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n} = \frac{b - a}{2^n}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Bound the error.** Since $\alpha \in [a_n, b_n]$ and $x_n$ is the midpoint, the maximum distance from $x_n$ to any point in $[a_n, b_n]$ is half the interval length:

$$\vert e_n \vert = \vert \alpha - x_n \vert \leq \frac{b_n - a_n}{2} = \frac{b - a}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convergence proof.** As $n \to \infty$, the RHS tends to zero, so $x_n \to \alpha$. Hence the method converges.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Order of convergence.** The error at step $n+1$ satisfies:

$$\vert e_{n+1} \vert \leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^{n+1}} = \frac{1}{2} \vert e_n \vert$$

So $\tfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert} \leq \tfrac{1}{2}$, meaning $\mu = 0.5$ and $p = 1$. **Linear convergence** is established. $\blacksquare$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Iterations for tolerance $\varepsilon$.** Given $\tfrac{b - a}{2^{n+1}} \leq \varepsilon$:

$$n \geq \log_2\!\left(\frac{b - a}{\varepsilon}\right) - 1$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Iteration formula.**

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Two-term error recurrence.** Using Taylor expansions of $f$ at $\alpha$ and rearranging (as derived in ┬¦3.2):

$$e_{n+1} \approx -\frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \, e_n \, e_{n-1} = K \, e_n \, e_{n-1}$$

where $K = -\tfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)}$ is constant near $\alpha$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Power-law ansatz.** Assume $e_n \approx C \lambda^{p^n}$ for some constants $C, \lambda$ with $\lambda \in (0, 1)$ and order $p > 1$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the recurrence.**

$$C \lambda^{p^{n+1}} = K \cdot C \lambda^{p^n} \cdot C \lambda^{p^{n-1}} = K C^2 \lambda^{p^n + p^{n-1}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Equate exponents.**

$$p^{n+1} = p^n + p^{n-1}$$

Dividing by $p^{n-1}$ (valid since $p > 0$):

$$p^2 = p + 1$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve the characteristic equation.**

$$p^2 - p - 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

Since order must be positive, we take the positive root:

$$p = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.61803\ldots$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Conclusion.** The Secant Method has **superlinear convergence** with order equal to the **Golden Ratio** $\phi \approx 1.618$. The asymptotic error constant is:

$$\mu = \left|\frac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)}\right|^{(p-1)/p}$$

This places the Secant Method *between* linear ($p = 1$) and quadratic ($p = 2$) in terms of speed. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Print This Before the Exam!**

### Core Concepts
- **Convergence**: An iterative method converges if $x_n \to \alpha$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Order $p$**: Largest integer such that $\tfrac{\vert e_{n+1} \vert}{\vert e_n \vert^p} \to \mu > 0$.
- **Asymptotic constant $\mu$**: Local property of $f$ at $\alpha$.

### Method-Speed Hierarchy (Memorize!)
1. **Bisection / Fixed-Point / Regula Falsi** ŌåÆ $p = 1$ (Linear)
2. **Secant Method** ŌåÆ $p = 1.618$ (Superlinear, Golden Ratio)
3. **Newton-Raphson** ŌåÆ $p = 2$ (Quadratic)
4. **Halley's Method** ŌåÆ $p = 3$ (Cubic)

### Critical Formulas (Must-Memorize)
- **Newton-Raphson error:** $e_{n+1} = \tfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} e_n^2$, so $\mu = \tfrac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert}$.
- **Bisection bound:** $\vert e_n \vert \leq \tfrac{b - a}{2^{n+1}}$.
- **Secant order:** $p^2 = p + 1 \Rightarrow p = \phi = \tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$.
- **Fixed-point:** converges iff $\vert g'(\alpha) \vert < 1$.

### Stopping Criteria (Always Mention Both!)
- $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ (absolute)
- $\tfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ (relative)
- Iteration count $\geq N_{\max}$ (safeguard)

### Common Pitfalls
- ŌØī Forgetting the absolute value in $\mu$ definition.
- ŌØī Writing $\tfrac{b-a}{2^n}$ instead of $\tfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ for Bisection error.
- ŌØī Confusing order of convergence with rate of convergence.
- ŌØī Skipping the Taylor expansion hypotheses ($f \in C^2$, $f'(\alpha) \neq 0$).
- ŌØī Not mentioning the **Golden Ratio** explicitly when discussing Secant ŌĆö a favourite 1-mark bonus.

### Quick Numerical Trick
For a quadratic method, if $\vert e_3 \vert \approx \vert e_2 \vert^2 / \vert e_1 \vert$ holds numerically, you've empirically confirmed $p = 2$ ŌĆö useful for verifying Newton-Raphson implementations in labs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic *always* pair a derivation (7 marks) with a numerical demonstration (7 marks). Practice both ŌĆö the derivation fetches methodology marks; the numerical part fetches the answer-evaluation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Nonlinear Equations Using the Above Methods in Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Nonlinear Equations Using the Above Methods in Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonlinear Equation (KTU 2024 Definition):** A nonlinear equation is an equation of the form $f(x) = 0$ where $f(x)$ is a non-linear function of $x$ (i.e., contains powers of $x$ other than 1, trigonometric, exponential, or logarithmic terms). The values of $x$ that satisfy this equation are called the **roots** or **zeros** of the equation.

For a nonlinear equation of the general form:

$$f(x) = 0$$

where $f(x)$ is continuous on an interval $[a, b]$, a **root** $\alpha$ satisfies $f(\alpha) = 0$. Unlike linear equations, nonlinear equations generally cannot be solved analytically (closed-form), so we rely on **iterative numerical methods** implemented in Python.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (Module 1):** The methods covered for solving nonlinear equations include:
> 1. **Bisection Method** (Bracket-based)
> 2. **Regula Falsi / False Position Method** (Bracket-based)
> 3. **Newton-Raphson Method** (Open method)
> 4. **Secant Method** (Open method)
> 5. **Fixed Point Iteration** (Open method)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the foot of a mountain and want to find where the **sea level** (height = 0) cuts through it on a contour map. You can either:

- **Walk along the slope** (climb up and down) and bracket the sea level between two points (Bisection / Regula Falsi).
- **Use a slope-measuring device** to project the next point directly (Newton-Raphson).
- **Estimate the slope using two previous points** without needing calculus (Secant).

Each strategy converges to the root at a different **speed** and **reliability**. In Python, we implement these strategies as algorithms and let the computer iterate hundreds of times in milliseconds.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Metrics:**
> - **Absolute Error:** $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert$
> - **Relative Error:** $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert}$
> - **Tolerance:** $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$ (typical KTU exam value)
> - **Maximum Iterations:** $N = 50$ (typical KTU exam value)
> - **Function Value Criterion:** $\vert f(x_n) \vert < \varepsilon$

### 1.4 Why Python for Numerical Computation?

Python (with libraries like **NumPy**, **SciPy**, and **SymPy**) is the KTU-recommended platform because:
- Built-in complex number support.
- Free, open-source, and platform-independent.
- Rich ecosystem of numerical functions.
- Easy plotting via **Matplotlib** for visualizing convergence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method 1 ŌĆö Bisection Method (Bracketing)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Verify $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (sign change).
2. Compute midpoint $c = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$.
3. If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$; else set $a = c$.
4. Repeat until $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$.

**Convergence:** Linear. Number of iterations needed: $n \geq \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{b-a}{\varepsilon}\right)$.

### 2.2 Method 2 ŌĆö Regula Falsi (False Position)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Verify $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
2. Compute $c = \dfrac{a \cdot f(b) - b \cdot f(a)}{f(b) - f(a)}$.
3. Replace the endpoint that has the **same sign** as $f(c)$.
4. Repeat.

**Convergence:** Linear (generally faster than bisection).

### 2.3 Method 3 ŌĆö Newton-Raphson Method (Open)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Start with an initial guess $x_0$.
2. Iterate: $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$.
3. Repeat until $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$.

**Convergence:** Quadratic (very fast), but **fails** if $f'(x_n) = 0$ or $x_0$ is too far from the root.

### 2.4 Method 4 ŌĆö Secant Method (Open, derivative-free)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Start with two initial guesses $x_0$ and $x_1$.
2. Iterate:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$
3. Repeat.

**Convergence:** Superlinear (order Ōēł 1.618 ŌĆö the **Golden Ratio**).

### 2.5 Method 5 ŌĆö Fixed Point Iteration (Open)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Rewrite $f(x) = 0$ as $x = g(x)$.
2. Iterate: $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$.
3. Converges if $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ near the root.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE ŌĆö MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| Method | Iteration Formula | Convergence Order | Initial Requirement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bisection | $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | Linear ($\mathcal{O}(1)$) | $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ |
| Regula Falsi | $c = \dfrac{a f(b) - b f(a)}{f(b) - f(a)}$ | Linear (faster) | $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ |
| Newton-Raphson | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | Quadratic ($\mathcal{O}(2)$) | $f'(x_0) \neq 0$ |
| Secant | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n)-f(x_{n-1})}$ | Superlinear ($\approx 1.618$) | Two initial guesses |
| Fixed Point | $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ | Linear (if $\vert g' \vert < 1$) | $\vert g'(x_0) \vert < 1$ |

| Stopping Criterion | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| Absolute Error | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ |
| Function Value | $\vert f(x_n) \vert < \varepsilon$ |
| Relative Error | $\dfrac{\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} < \varepsilon$ |
| Bracket Width (Bisection) | $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Applications of Nonlinear Root-Finding:**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Finding the depth of flow in open channels (Manning's equation).
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Solving for operating points of nonlinear circuits (diodes, transistors).
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Determining stress-strain intersections in plasticity problems.
> - **Chemical Engineering:** Computing equilibrium concentrations from cubic equations of state (van der Waals).
> - **Computer Science:** Optimization, machine learning (gradient root-finding), and graphics (ray-surface intersection).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bisection Convergence Bound

We want to find the **minimum number of iterations** $n$ such that the interval width is less than $\varepsilon$.

After $n$ iterations, the interval width is:

$$b_n - a_n = \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n}$$

For convergence, we require:

$$b_n - a_n < \varepsilon \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{2^n} < \varepsilon$$

Taking log base 2 on both sides:

$$\log_2(b_0 - a_0) - n < \log_2(\varepsilon)$$

Solving for $n$:

$$n > \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,n \geq \left\lceil \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{b_0 - a_0}{\varepsilon}\right) \right\rceil\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Newton-Raphson Iteration

We start from Taylor's expansion of $f(x)$ around $x_n$:

$$f(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x - x_n) + \dfrac{f''(x_n)}{2!}(x - x_n)^2 + \ldots$$

Setting $f(x) = 0$ and ignoring higher-order terms:

$$0 \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1} - x_n)$$

Solving for $x_{n+1}$:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$

This is the **Newton-Raphson iteration formula**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Secant Method from Newton-Raphson

Replace $f'(x_n)$ in Newton-Raphson with the **finite difference approximation**:

$$f'(x_n) \approx \dfrac{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}{x_n - x_{n-1}}$$

Substituting:

$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n) \cdot (x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

### 3.4 Convergence Order Derivation (Newton-Raphson)

Let $e_n = x_n - \alpha$. Taylor expansion of $f(\alpha)$ around $x_n$:

$$0 = f(\alpha) = f(x_n) - e_n f'(x_n) + \dfrac{e_n^2}{2} f''(\xi)$$

Using $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$:

$$e_{n+1} = e_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} = \dfrac{e_n^2 f''(\xi)}{2 f'(x_n)}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{n+1} \approx \dfrac{f''(\alpha)}{2 f'(\alpha)} \cdot e_n^2\,}$$

This confirms **quadratic convergence**.

### 3.5 Complete Python Implementations (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the course is **PEMRT524 ŌĆō Numerical Computation Using Python**, full Python code is **required** by the KTU 2024 syllabus and is included below.

#### 3.5.1 Bisection Method in Python

```python
import math

def bisection(f, a, b, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    """
    Find root of f(x) = 0 in [a, b] using Bisection Method.
    """
    if f(a) * f(b) >= 0:
        raise ValueError("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs.")

    print(f"{'Iter':<5}{'a':<12}{'b':<12}{'c':<12}{'f(c)':<12}")
    print("-" * 53)

    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        c = (a + b) / 2.0
        fc = f(c)

        print(f"{i:<5}{a:<12.6f}{b:<12.6f}{c:<12.6f}{fc:<12.6f}")

        if abs(fc) < tol or (b - a) / 2.0 < tol:
            return c

        if f(a) * fc < 0:
            b = c
        else:
            a = c

    return c

# Example: f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9  (root near x = 2.706)
f = lambda x: x**3 - 4*x - 9
root = bisection(f, 2, 3)
print(f"\nApproximate Root: {root:.6f}")
```

#### 3.5.2 Newton-Raphson Method in Python

```python
def newton_raphson(f, df, x0, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    """
    Find root of f(x) = 0 using Newton-Raphson Method.
    Requires derivative f'(x).
    """
    print(f"{'Iter':<5}{'x_n':<15}{'f(x_n)':<15}{'Error':<15}")
    print("-" * 50)

    xn = x0
    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        fxn = f(xn)
        dfxn = df(xn)

        if dfxn == 0:
            raise ZeroDivisionError("Derivative is zero. Method fails.")

        xn_new = xn - fxn / dfxn
        err = abs(xn_new - xn)

        print(f"{i:<5}{xn:<15.8f}{fxn:<15.8f}{err:<15.8f}")

        if err < tol:
            return xn_new

        xn = xn_new

    return xn

# Example: f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9, f'(x) = 3x^2 - 4
f  = lambda x: x**3 - 4*x - 9
df = lambda x: 3*x**2 - 4
root = newton_raphson(f, df, x0=2.5)
print(f"\nApproximate Root: {root:.6f}")
```

#### 3.5.3 Secant Method in Python

```python
def secant(f, x0, x1, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    """
    Find root of f(x) = 0 using Secant Method.
    Does NOT require derivative.
    """
    print(f"{'Iter':<5}{'x_n-1':<15}{'x_n':<15}{'x_n+1':<15}")
    print("-" * 50)

    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        f0, f1 = f(x0), f(x1)
        if f1 - f0 == 0:
            raise ZeroDivisionError("Division by zero in secant formula.")

        x2 = x1 - f1 * (x1 - x0) / (f1 - f0)

        print(f"{i:<5}{x0:<15.8f}{x1:<15.8f}{x2:<15.8f}")

        if abs(x2 - x1) < tol:
            return x2

        x0, x1 = x1, x2

    return x1

# Example
f = lambda x: x**3 - 4*x - 9
root = secant(f, 2, 3)
print(f"\nApproximate Root: {root:.6f}")
```

#### 3.5.4 Regula Falsi Method in Python

```python
def regula_falsi(f, a, b, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    if f(a) * f(b) >= 0:
        raise ValueError("f(a) and f(b) must have opposite signs.")

    print(f"{'Iter':<5}{'a':<12}{'b':<12}{'c':<12}{'f(c)':<12}")
    print("-" * 53)

    c_old = a
    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        fa, fb = f(a), f(b)
        c = (a * fb - b * fa) / (fb - fa)
        fc = f(c)

        print(f"{i:<5}{a:<12.6f}{b:<12.6f}{c:<12.6f}{fc:<12.6f}")

        if abs(fc) < tol or abs(c - c_old) < tol:
            return c

        if fa * fc < 0:
            b = c
        else:
            a = c
        c_old = c

    return c

# Example
f = lambda x: x**3 - 4*x - 9
root = regula_falsi(f, 2, 3)
print(f"\nApproximate Root: {root:.6f}")
```

#### 3.5.5 Fixed Point Iteration in Python

```python
def fixed_point(g, x0, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    """
    Find x such that x = g(x).
    """
    print(f"{'Iter':<5}{'x_n':<15}{'g(x_n)':<15}{'Error':<15}")
    print("-" * 50)

    xn = x0
    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        xn_new = g(xn)
        err = abs(xn_new - xn)

        print(f"{i:<5}{xn:<15.8f}{xn_new:<15.8f}{err:<15.8f}")

        if err < tol:
            return xn_new
        xn = xn_new

    return xn

# Example: Solve x^3 - 4x - 9 = 0  =>  x = (x^3 - 9)/4
g = lambda x: (x**3 - 9) / 4
root = fixed_point(g, x0=2.5)
print(f"\nApproximate Root: {root:.6f}")
```

### 3.6 Convergence Comparison (Same Problem)

| Method | Iterations to Converge | Final Root | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Bisection | ~16 | 2.70653 | Slow but guaranteed |
| Regula Falsi | ~8 | 2.70653 | Faster bracketing |
| Newton-Raphson | ~4 | 2.70653 | Fastest; needs $f'(x)$ |
| Secant | ~5 | 2.70653 | No derivative; slightly slower than NR |
| Fixed Point | ~25 | 2.70653 | Slowest open method |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Root-Finding Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: f(x) = 0"] --> B{"Is f(x) linear?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Direct Algebraic Solution"]
    B -- No --> D{"Do you have a bracketing interval?"}
    D -- Yes --> E{"Need derivative?"}
    D -- No --> F["Open Methods"]
    E -- No --> G["Bisection Method"]
    E -- Slow OK --> H["Regula Falsi Method"]
    F --> I{"Derivative available?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Newton-Raphson Method"]
    I -- No --> K["Secant Method"]
    F --> L["Fixed Point Iteration"]
    G --> M["Converged Root"]
    H --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**State the Bisection Method algorithm. Why is it called a bracketing method?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bisection Method Algorithm:**
> 1. Find $a$ and $b$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.
> 2. Compute the midpoint $c = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$.
> 3. If $f(c) = 0$ (or $\vert f(c) \vert < \varepsilon$), then $c$ is the root.
> 4. Else if $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, set $b = c$; else set $a = c$.
> 5. Repeat from step 2.

**Why bracketing:** The method always **encloses the root** within a shrinking interval $[a, b]$. Since $f$ is continuous and changes sign, by the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, a root must lie inside. The bracket is halved at each step, guaranteeing convergence.

*(3 marks: 1 mark algorithm steps, 2 marks explanation.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Compare Newton-Raphson and Secant methods in terms of convergence order and derivative requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Newton-Raphson | Secant |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Convergence Order | **2 (Quadratic)** | **Ōēł 1.618 (Superlinear)** |
| Derivative Required? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Initial Guess | **One ($x_0$)** | **Two ($x_0, x_1$)** |
| Speed | Faster (per iter) | Slightly slower (per iter) |
| Failure Cases | $f'(x_n) = 0$ | Denominator $\to 0$ |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for both aspects.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always** show the iteration table ŌĆö failing to do so costs **3ŌĆō4 marks**.
> - State the **stopping criterion explicitly** at the start of your answer.
> - In Python-based questions, **include the full code** with output. Partial code loses **50%** of the mark.
> - Specify **initial guess and tolerance** at the beginning.
> - Mention **convergence order** wherever applicable (a 1-mark KTU favorite).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9 = 0$ using the **Newton-Raphson Method** with $x_0 = 2.5$ and $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$. Perform **4 iterations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete **Python program** to solve the same equation using the **Bisection Method** in the interval $[2, 3]$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

Given: $f(x) = x^3 - 4x - 9$, $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 4$, $x_0 = 2.5$.

Iteration formula:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{x_n^3 - 4x_n - 9}{3x_n^2 - 4}$$

**Iteration 1:** $x_0 = 2.5$

$$f(2.5) = 15.625 - 10 - 9 = -3.375$$
$$f'(2.5) = 18.75 - 4 = 14.75$$
$$x_1 = 2.5 - \dfrac{-3.375}{14.75} = 2.5 + 0.2288 = 2.7288$$

**Iteration 2:** $x_1 = 2.7288$

$$f(2.7288) \approx 20.322 - 10.915 - 9 = 0.407$$
$$f'(2.7288) \approx 22.343 - 4 = 18.343$$
$$x_2 = 2.7288 - \dfrac{0.407}{18.343} = 2.7066$$

**Iteration 3:** $x_2 = 2.7066$

$$f(2.7066) \approx 19.829 - 10.826 - 9 = 0.003$$
$$f'(2.7066) \approx 21.974 - 4 = 17.974$$
$$x_3 = 2.7066 - \dfrac{0.003}{17.974} = 2.70653$$

**Iteration 4:** $x_3 = 2.70653$ (converged, error $\approx 10^{-5}$).

| Iter | $x_n$ | $f(x_n)$ | $f'(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 2.5000 | $-3.3750$ | 14.75 | 2.7288 |
| 2 | 2.7288 | $0.4070$ | 18.343 | 2.7066 |
| 3 | 2.7066 | $0.0030$ | 17.974 | 2.7065 |
| 4 | 2.7065 | $\approx 0$ | ŌĆö | 2.7065 |

**Root $\approx 2.7065$**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

```python
def f(x):
    return x**3 - 4*x - 9

def bisection(f, a, b, tol=1e-4, max_iter=50):
    if f(a) * f(b) >= 0:
        print("No bracket exists.")
        return None

    print(f"{'Iter':<6}{'a':<12}{'b':<12}{'c':<12}{'f(c)':<12}")
    print("-" * 54)

    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        c = (a + b) / 2
        fc = f(c)
        print(f"{i:<6}{a:<12.6f}{b:<12.6f}{c:<12.6f}{fc:<12.6f}")

        if abs(fc) < tol or (b - a) / 2 < tol:
            print(f"\nRoot found: x = {c:.6f}")
            return c

        if f(a) * fc < 0:
            b = c
        else:
            a = c

    return c

bisection(f, 2, 3)
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Iter  a            b            c            f(c)         
------------------------------------------------------
1     2.000000     3.000000     2.500000     -3.375000    
2     2.500000     3.000000     2.750000     0.796875     
3     2.500000     2.750000     2.625000     -1.412109    
...
14    2.706543     2.706543     2.706543     0.000124     

Root found: x = 2.706543
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** Using the **Secant Method**, find the root of $f(x) = \cos(x) - x \cdot e^x$ with $x_0 = 0$ and $x_1 = 1$. Take $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$ and perform **5 iterations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a **Python program** implementing the **Newton-Raphson Method** for the equation $f(x) = x^3 - 2x - 5$ with $x_0 = 2$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

Iteration formula:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)(x_n - x_{n-1})}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}$$

| $n$ | $x_{n-1}$ | $x_n$ | $f(x_{n-1})$ | $f(x_n)$ | $x_{n+1}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | $-1.3098$ | 0.4331 |
| 2 | 1.0000 | 0.4331 | $-1.3098$ | $0.1050$ | 0.4443 |
| 3 | 0.4331 | 0.4443 | $0.1050$ | $-0.0072$ | 0.4440 |
| 4 | 0.4443 | 0.4440 | $-0.0072$ | $0.0001$ | 0.4440 |
| 5 | 0.4440 | 0.4440 | $0.0001$ | $\approx 0$ | 0.4440 |

**Root $\approx 0.4440$**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

```python
def f(x):
    return x**3 - 2*x - 5

def df(x):
    return 3*x**2 - 2

def newton_raphson(f, df, x0, tol=1e-5, max_iter=50):
    print(f"{'Iter':<6}{'x_n':<15}{'f(x_n)':<15}{'x_{n+1}':<15}")
    print("-" * 55)

    xn = x0
    for i in range(1, max_iter + 1):
        fxn = f(xn)
        dfxn = df(xn)

        if dfxn == 0:
            print("Derivative zero. Method fails.")
            return None

        xn_new = xn - fxn / dfxn
        print(f"{i:<6}{xn:<15.8f}{fxn:<15.8f}{xn_new:<15.8f}")

        if abs(xn_new - xn) < tol:
            print(f"\nRoot: x = {xn_new:.6f}")
            return xn_new

        xn = xn_new

    return xn

newton_raphson(f, df, x0=2)
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Iter  x_n            f(x_n)         x_{n+1}        
-------------------------------------------------------
1     2.00000000     -1.00000000    2.10000000     
2     2.10000000     0.06100000     2.09456812     
3     2.09456812     0.00018543     2.09455148     
4     2.09455148     0.00000002     2.09455148     

Root: x = 2.094551
```

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö KTU 2024 Module 1**

- Ō£ģ A **nonlinear equation** $f(x) = 0$ has no closed-form solution in general; iterative numerical methods are used.
- Ō£ģ **Bisection Method** is **guaranteed to converge** but is slow (linear).
- Ō£ģ **Newton-Raphson** is the **fastest** (quadratic) but **needs the derivative** $f'(x)$ and a good initial guess.
- Ō£ģ **Secant Method** approximates $f'(x)$ using a finite difference ŌĆö **no derivative needed**.
- Ō£ģ **Regula Falsi** combines bracketing reliability with faster convergence than Bisection.
- Ō£ģ **Fixed Point Iteration** requires $\vert g'(x) \vert < 1$ for convergence.
- Ō£ģ Always **print the iteration table** in KTU exams ŌĆö this is worth **3ŌĆō4 marks** by itself.
- Ō£ģ **Stopping criteria** to remember: $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$, $\vert f(x_n) \vert < \varepsilon$, or iteration limit.
- Ō£ģ For Python implementations, **always define $f(x)$ as a lambda** and call the function in a driver script.
- Ō£ģ **Convergence order** is a frequently asked 1-mark question: Bisection = 1, Regula Falsi = 1, NR = 2, Secant = 1.618, Fixed Point = 1.
- Ō£ģ When the **derivative is unavailable**, prefer **Secant** over Newton-Raphson.
- Ō£ģ When the function has a **sign change in an interval**, prefer **bracketing methods** (Bisection, Regula Falsi) for guaranteed convergence.
- Ō£ģ For **engineering applications** (KTU theory part), always mention at least **one real-world example** (Manning's equation, diode circuits, van der Waals equation).

---

topics.title: Newton's Finite and Backward Difference Interpolation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Newton's Finite and Backward Difference Interpolation Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Newton's Forward Difference Interpolation Formula** is a finite difference based polynomial interpolation technique used to estimate intermediate values of a function $f(x)$ that lies **close to the top (beginning)** of a tabulated data set where arguments are equally spaced. It is constructed by expanding the function in a Taylor-like series using the **forward difference operator** $\Delta$.

**Newton's Backward Difference Interpolation Formula** is the dual counterpart of the forward method, used to estimate intermediate values of $f(x)$ that lies **close to the bottom (end)** of an equally spaced tabulated data set. It is constructed using the **backward difference operator** $\nabla$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Both formulas belong to the family of **NewtonŌĆōGregory Interpolation Polynomials** and form a compulsory sub-topic under Module 2 (Finite Differences and Interpolation). Examiners frequently test the construction of the difference table, identification of the correct operator, and direct substitution into the formula.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hiking along a trail, and your **GPS has readings only at every kilometre marker** (the tabulated data points). If you want to know your altitude at the **half-kilometre point near the trailhead**, Newton's *Forward* method gives the best estimate because it builds the path starting from the *first* marker and walks forward. If instead you want the altitude near the *summit* (end of the trail), the **Backward** method is preferred because it walks *backward* from the last marker ŌĆö using the local gradient and curvature information at the top of the table, which the forward method ignores.

### 1.3 Key Operators and Constants

- The **forward difference operator**: $\Delta f(x_i) = f(x_{i+1}) - f(x_i)$
- The **backward difference operator**: $\nabla f(x_i) = f(x_i) - f(x_{i-1})$
- **Spacing parameter** $h$ (assumed uniform throughout the table): **bold** $h = x_{i+1} - x_i$
- The **dimensionless parameter** $s = (x - x_0)/h$ for forward; $s = (x - x_n)/h$ for backward.

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 Scheme emphasises that the difference operators $\Delta$ and $\nabla$ are linear and obey standard algebraic identities (e.g., $\Delta \equiv E - 1$ and $\nabla \equiv 1 - E^{-1}$, where $E$ is the shift operator). However, for exam problems, the **constructive table-based approach** (Section 3) is sufficient and is the most rewarded.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify Equispacing.**  
Confirm that the argument values $x_0, x_1, \dots, x_n$ are equally spaced with constant $h$. The Newton finite-difference formulas **fail** for non-uniform data.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the Forward/Backward Difference Table.**  
Compute the successive differences:  
- **First order:** $\Delta f_i = f_{i+1} - f_i$ (or $\nabla f_i = f_i - f_{i-1}$).  
- **Higher orders:** Apply the operator recursively, $\Delta^k f_i = \Delta(\Delta^{k-1} f_i)$.  
- Stop when the differences become constant, zero, or unstable (rounding noise).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose the Correct Formula.**  
- Point is **near the top** of the table: use **Newton's Forward** formula.  
- Point is **near the bottom**: use **Newton's Backward** formula.  
- Point is **in the middle**: Stirling's or Bessel's formula (out of KTU Module 2 scope but conceptually relevant).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Reduced Variable $s$.**  
$s$ is *not* a value in the table; it is a pure number indicating fractional position.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute into the Polynomial and Simplify.**  
Evaluate the finite sum, truncating at the order where the difference becomes negligibly small.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why equispacing?** Finite-difference operators are defined using equal intervals; variable spacing breaks their algebraic recursion.
- **Why a difference table?** It isolates redundant information and makes polynomial coefficients visible.
- **Why pick forward or backward?** Each method gives a *unique* polynomial of degree $n$; choosing the operator matched to the locality of $x$ minimises round-off error from the truncated higher-order terms (the terms of order $\mathcal{O}(s^{n+1})$ explode as $|s|$ grows).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Forward Difference Formula | Backward Difference Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Reduced variable | $s = (x - x_0)/h$ | $s = (x - x_n)/h$ |
| Newton's formula | $y(x) = y_0 + s\,\Delta y_0 + \dfrac{s(s-1)}{2!}\,\Delta^2 y_0 + \dfrac{s(s-1)(s-2)}{3!}\,\Delta^3 y_0 + \dots$ | $y(x) = y_n + s\,\nabla y_n + \dfrac{s(s+1)}{2!}\,\nabla^2 y_n + \dfrac{s(s+1)(s+2)}{3!}\,\nabla^3 y_n + \dots$ |
| General $k$-th term | $\dfrac{s(s-1)(s-2)\dots(s-k+1)}{k!}\,\Delta^k y_0$ | $\dfrac{s(s+1)(s+2)\dots(s+k-1)}{k!}\,\nabla^k y_n$ |
| Sign of $s$ in useful range | $0 \le s \le 1$ (near top) | $-1 \le s \le 0$ (near bottom) |
| Operator identity | $\Delta \equiv E - 1$ | $\nabla \equiv 1 - E^{-1}$ |
| Required table entries | Top of each difference column | Bottom of each difference column |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** In the backward formula, $s$ is **negative** for points *inside* the table (e.g., for the last interval, $s = -1$ at $x_{n-1}$). Do **not** drop the sign.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forward formula:** Used in trajectory prediction at the *launch* of a rocket, where early telemetry points are clean and the state changes rapidly.
- **Backward formula:** Used in landing / descent trajectory reconstruction, where the last reliable samples dominate and historical drift is irrelevant.
- Both formulas are foundational in **finite-difference solvers for PDEs** (heat equation, wave equation), **digital signal processing** (interpolation filters), and **actuarial / financial table smoothing**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Forward Difference Formula (Logic Only)

The forward difference operator $\Delta$ satisfies $\Delta = E - 1$, where $E$ is the shift operator $E f(x) = f(x+h)$.

$$E = 1 + \Delta$$

$$E^s = (1 + \Delta)^s$$

Applying $E^s$ to $f(x_0)$ gives $f(x_0 + sh) = f(x)$:

$$f(x) = (1 + \Delta)^s f(x_0)$$

Expanding $(1+\Delta)^s$ using the generalised binomial theorem:

$$f(x) = \left[ 1 + s\Delta + \frac{s(s-1)}{2!}\Delta^2 + \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)}{3!}\Delta^3 + \dots \right] f(x_0)$$

$$f(x) = f(x_0) + s\,\Delta f(x_0) + \frac{s(s-1)}{2!}\,\Delta^2 f(x_0) + \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)}{3!}\,\Delta^3 f(x_0) + \dots$$

This is the **NewtonŌĆōGregory Forward Interpolation Polynomial**. The coefficients are read **down the leftmost column** of the difference table.

### 3.2 Derivation of Newton's Backward Difference Formula (Logic Only)

Using the backward operator identity $\nabla = 1 - E^{-1}$, we have $E^{-1} = 1 - \nabla$ and therefore:

$$E^{-s} = (1 - \nabla)^s$$

$$f(x) = E^s f(x_n) \cdot E^{-s} f(x_n) \quad \text{(re-indexed around } x_n \text{)}$$

Expanding $(1 - \nabla)^s$ binomially (note the alternating sign in standard form; using $s' = -s$ gives the canonical form):

$$f(x) = f(x_n) + s\,\nabla f(x_n) + \frac{s(s+1)}{2!}\,\nabla^2 f(x_n) + \frac{s(s+1)(s+2)}{3!}\,\nabla^3 f(x_n) + \dots$$

The coefficients are read **down the rightmost column** of the difference table.

### 3.3 Worked Example A ŌĆö Newton's Forward Difference (Full Computation)

**Problem.** Given the data:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
y = f(x) & 1 & 8 & 27 & 64 & 125 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Estimate $f(1.5)$ using Newton's forward difference formula.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Construct the difference table.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
x_i & y_i & \Delta y_i & \Delta^2 y_i & \Delta^3 y_i & \Delta^4 y_i \\
\hline
1 & 1 & 7 & 12 & 6 & 0 \\
2 & 8 & 19 & 18 & 6 & \\
3 & 27 & 37 & 24 & & \\
4 & 64 & 61 & & & \\
5 & 125 & & & & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Detailed computation of entries:  
- $\Delta y_0 = 8 - 1 = 7$  
- $\Delta y_1 = 27 - 8 = 19$  
- $\Delta y_2 = 64 - 27 = 37$  
- $\Delta y_3 = 125 - 64 = 61$  
- $\Delta^2 y_0 = 19 - 7 = 12$  
- $\Delta^2 y_1 = 37 - 19 = 18$  
- $\Delta^2 y_2 = 61 - 37 = 24$  
- $\Delta^3 y_0 = 18 - 12 = 6$  
- $\Delta^3 y_1 = 24 - 18 = 6$  
- $\Delta^4 y_0 = 6 - 6 = 0$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the reduced variable $s$.**

$$
s = \frac{x - x_0}{h} = \frac{1.5 - 1}{1} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the forward formula.**

$$
f(1.5) = 1 + (0.5)(7) + \frac{(0.5)(-0.5)}{2}(12) + \frac{(0.5)(-0.5)(-1.5)}{6}(6) + \frac{(0.5)(-0.5)(-1.5)(-2.5)}{24}(0)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate each term explicitly.**

- Term 0: $1$  
- Term 1: $(0.5)(7) = 3.5$  
- Term 2: $\dfrac{-0.25}{2} \times 12 = (-0.125)(12) = -1.5$  
- Term 3: $\dfrac{0.375}{6} \times 6 = (0.0625)(6) = 0.375$  
- Term 4: $0$ (since $\Delta^4 y_0 = 0$)

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sum the terms.**

$$
f(1.5) = 1 + 3.5 - 1.5 + 0.375 + 0 = 3.375
$$

**Verification.** The exact function is $f(x) = x^3$, so $f(1.5) = 3.375$. The interpolation is **exact** because the data is generated by a cubic and our formula reproduced it exactly (the fourth difference vanishes).

### 3.4 Worked Example B ŌĆö Newton's Backward Difference (Full Computation)

**Problem.** Using the same data table, estimate $f(4.5)$ using Newton's backward difference formula.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the bottom entry of each column.**

From the difference table in Example A:  
- $y_n = y_4 = 125$  
- $\nabla y_4 = \Delta y_3 = 61$  
- $\nabla^2 y_4 = \Delta^2 y_2 = 24$  
- $\nabla^3 y_4 = \Delta^3 y_1 = 6$  
- $\nabla^4 y_4 = \Delta^4 y_0 = 0$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the reduced variable $s$.**

$$
s = \frac{x - x_n}{h} = \frac{4.5 - 5}{1} = -0.5
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the backward formula.**

$$
f(4.5) = 125 + (-0.5)(61) + \frac{(-0.5)(0.5)}{2}(24) + \frac{(-0.5)(0.5)(1.5)}{6}(6) + \frac{(-0.5)(0.5)(1.5)(2.5)}{24}(0)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate each term explicitly.**

- Term 0: $125$  
- Term 1: $(-0.5)(61) = -30.5$  
- Term 2: $\dfrac{-0.25}{2} \times 24 = (-0.125)(24) = -3$  
- Term 3: $\dfrac{-0.375}{6} \times 6 = (-0.0625)(6) = -0.375$  
- Term 4: $0$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Sum the terms.**

$$
f(4.5) = 125 - 30.5 - 3 - 0.375 + 0 = 91.125
$$

**Verification.** $f(4.5) = (4.5)^3 = 91.125$. The result is **exact** (cubic data, vanishing 4th difference).

### 3.5 Truncation and Error Magnitudes

The **remainder term** for the forward formula (truncated at order $k$) is:

$$
R_k(x) = \frac{s(s-1)(s-2)\dots(s-k)}{(k+1)!}\,\Delta^{k+1} f(x_0) \cdot h^{k+1} \cdot \text{(correction factor)}
$$

A simple **engineering bound** sufficient for KTU problems is:

$$
\vert R_k \vert \le \frac{\vert s(s-1)\dots(s-k) \vert}{(k+1)!} \cdot \max \vert \Delta^{k+1} f \vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU's typical 14-mark problem, students are expected to compute *one* order beyond the apparent constant difference to justify the truncation ŌĆö this is the difference between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Interpolation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the algorithm ŌĆö from raw data acquisition to validated interpolated output ŌĆö as required by the KTU 2024 computational-numerics syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Tabulated Data x and y] --> B{Is Spacing Uniform?}
    B -- No --> C[STOP: Reject or Resample Data]
    B -- Yes --> D[Compute Step Size h]
    D --> E[Build Forward or Backward Difference Table]
    E --> F{Identify Locality of Target x}
    F -- Near Top --> G[Use Newton Forward Formula]
    F -- Near Bottom --> H[Use Newton Backward Formula]
    F -- Central --> I[Use Stirling or Bessel Method]
    G --> J[Compute Reduced Variable s]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Evaluate Polynomial Truncation Order]
    K --> L[Assemble and Sum Polynomial Terms]
    L --> M[Output Interpolated Value y star]
    M --> N[Validate via Remainder Estimate]
    N --> O[Report Final Answer with Bound]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Forward vs. Backward Operator Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[x sub 0] --> B[x sub 1] --> C[x sub 2] --> D[x sub 3] --> E[x sub n]
    A -.Forward Expansion.-> F[Delta k y sub 0]
    E -.Backward Expansion.-> G[nabla k y sub n]
    F --> H[Newton Forward Polynomial]
    G --> I[Newton Backward Polynomial]
    H --> J[Best near x sub 0]
    I --> K[Best near x sub n]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **functional flow schematics**, not physical drawings. They describe the *control flow* of the numerical method as implemented in any language, and they map cleanly onto Python implementations using `numpy.diff` for the table and direct term-by-term accumulation for the polynomial.

### 4.3 Computational Mapping Notes

- The **difference table** is constructed row by row, with each successive row having one fewer element.  
- For the **forward formula**, the algorithm reads the *leftmost* entry of each row.  
- For the **backward formula**, the algorithm reads the *rightmost* entry of each row.  
- A **stopping criterion** is the appearance of a constant (or near-constant) difference ŌĆö beyond this, additional terms add noise rather than accuracy.
