The **Runge-Kutta (RK) family** is a class of iterative numerical integrators that compute the next value $y_{n+1}$ by evaluating the slope function $f(x, y)$ at several cleverly chosen points inside the interval $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$ and forming a **weighted average** of these slopes. This mirrors the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus idea: integrate the slope to get the change in $y$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Runge-Kutta Method (KTU 2024 Formal Definition):** An explicit, single-step, multi-stage numerical integration procedure for first-order IVPs in which $y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,\Phi(x_n, y_n, h)$, where the increment function $\Phi$ is a linear combination of $s$ evaluations of $f(x, y)$ at strategically sampled points within the step.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Hill-Walker"

Imagine you are blindfolded on a hill and must reach a target $50$ m ahead. You cannot see the slope of the entire hill at once. Your strategy:

1. **Probe the slope at your feet** (this is Euler's method ŌĆö naive, first-order accurate).
2. **Take a half-step forward, re-probe the slope, then take a full step from your original position** (this is the **Midpoint RK2** method).
3. **Probe at four different points: start, midpoint using slope 1, midpoint using slope 2, and endpoint using slope 3** ŌĆö then take a *weighted average* of all four slopes (this is **Classical RK4**).

The Runge-Kutta methods essentially "interview" the slope function at multiple locations and use the gathered intelligence to take a more confident step.

> [!TIP]
> The genius of RK methods is that they achieve **high-order accuracy without requiring analytical derivatives of $f(x, y)$** ŌĆö a massive practical advantage over Taylor series methods, which demand computation of $f_x, f_y, f_{xx}, f_{xy}, \ldots$

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use Euler's Method?

Euler's method $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n, y_n)$ is a **first-order Runge-Kutta method** with local truncation error $O(h^2)$ and global error $O(h)$. To halve the global error, you must halve $h$, **doubling the computational workload**. Higher-order RK methods (RK4) achieve $O(h^4)$ global error, so a tiny reduction in $h$ yields a massive accuracy boost ŌĆö at the cost of 4 function evaluations per step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Form of an Explicit s-Stage Runge-Kutta Method

An explicit RK method with $s$ stages computes $y_{n+1}$ as:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h \sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i \, k_i
$$

where each stage slope $k_i$ is evaluated as:

$$
k_i = f\!\left( x_n + c_i h, \; y_n + h \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_{ij} \, k_j \right), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The summation upper limit is $i-1$ because the method is **explicit**: stage $i$ uses only the *previously computed* stages $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_{i-1}$. This makes RK methods straightforward to implement ŌĆö no implicit equation solving is required.

## 2.2 The Butcher Tableau ŌĆö A Compact Representation

All coefficients of an RK method are organized in a **Butcher tableau**:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 \\
c_2 & a_{21} \\
c_3 & a_{31} & a_{32} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \\
c_s & a_{s1} & a_{s2} & \cdots & a_{s,s-1} \\
\hline
& b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_s
\end{array}
$$

- The $a_{ij}$ entries (lower-triangular for explicit methods) dictate the *input arguments* of $f$ for each stage.
- The $b_i$ row gives the *weights* in the final update.
- The $c_i$ column gives the *time offsets* where each slope is sampled.

## 2.3 Butcher Tableaux of Common RK Methods

**Euler's Method (RK1):**
$$
\begin{array}{c|c}
0 & \\
\hline
& 1
\end{array}
$$

**Midpoint Method (RK2):**
$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & & \\
1/2 & 1/2 & \\
\hline
& 0 & 1
\end{array}
$$

**Heun's Method / Improved Euler (RK2):**
$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & & \\
1 & 1 & \\
\hline
& 1/2 & 1/2
\end{array}
$$

**Classical Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4):**
$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & & & & \\
1/2 & 1/2 & & & \\
1/2 & 0 & 1/2 & & \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & \\
\hline
& 1/6 & 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/6
\end{array}
$$

## 2.4 Order Conditions ŌĆö Why Specific Coefficients?

For a Runge-Kutta method to have **order $p$**, its coefficients must satisfy a set of nonlinear algebraic constraints derived by matching the Taylor series expansion of $y_{n+1}$ to the true solution up to $O(h^{p+1})$. For order 2, only two constraints are needed:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i c_i = \frac{1}{2}
$$

For order 4, eight order conditions must be satisfied simultaneously ŌĆö a feat accomplished by the classical RK4 tableau above.

## 2.5 Local vs. Global Truncation Error

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Local Truncation Error (LTE):** Error introduced in a *single* step, assuming the previous value was exact. For RK4, LTE $= O(h^5)$.
> - **Global Truncation Error (GTE):** Cumulative error after $n = (b-a)/h$ steps. For RK4, GTE $= O(h^4)$.

The leap from LTE to GTE reduces the exponent by 1 because errors accumulate roughly linearly across $n \approx 1/h$ steps.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of RK Methods |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Trajectory propagation of satellites and reentry vehicles |
| Circuit Simulation | SPICE solvers (e.g., trapezoidal/RK-based transient analysis) |
| Chemical Engineering | Solving stiff reactor kinetics ODEs |
| Robotics | Real-time path integration for SLAM and odometry |
| Computational Fluid Dynamics | Time integration of Navier-Stokes equations |
| Pharmacology | Pharmacokinetic / pharmacodynamic models |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Runge-Kutta Methods

| **Method** | **Stage Formulae** | **Order $p$** | **LTE** | **GTE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Euler (RK1) | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$; $\;y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_1$ | $1$ | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h)$ |
| Midpoint (RK2) | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$; $\;k_2 = f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + h k_1/2)$; $\;y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_2$ | $2$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| Heun's (RK2) | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$; $\;k_2 = f(x_n + h,\; y_n + h k_1)$; $\;y_{n+1} = y_n + (h/2)(k_1 + k_2)$ | $2$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| Ralston's (RK2) | $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$; $\;k_2 = f(x_n + 3h/4,\; y_n + 3h k_1/4)$; $\;y_{n+1} = y_n + h(k_1/3 + 2 k_2/3)$ | $2$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| Classical (RK4) | See Section 1.4 logic | $4$ | $O(h^5)$ | $O(h^4)$ |

**Key structural relations for order 2:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i = 1, \qquad \sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i c_i = \frac{1}{2}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Starting Point ŌĆö Taylor Series Expansion

The exact solution at $x_{n+1} = x_n + h$ has the Taylor expansion:

$$
y(x_{n+1}) = y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x_n) + \cdots
$$

Since $y' = f(x, y)$, the higher derivatives are:

$$
y'' = \frac{df}{dx} = f_x + f \, f_y
$$

$$
y''' = f_{xx} + 2f f_{xy} + f^2 f_{yy} + f_x f_y + f f_y^2
$$

Computing these by hand becomes impractical beyond order 2. Runge-Kutta methods **bypass the need for analytical derivatives** by evaluating $f$ at sampled points.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of RK2 from Taylor Series

Consider a generic 2-stage explicit RK method:

$$
k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)
$$

$$
k_2 = f(x_n + p h, \; y_n + q h k_1)
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left( a k_1 + b k_2 \right)
$$

where $a, b, p, q$ are constants to be determined.

**Step 1: Taylor expand $k_2$ about the point $(x_n, y_n)$.**

Using the multivariate Taylor expansion of $f(x + \Delta x, y + \Delta y)$:

$$
k_2 = f(x_n, y_n) + p h f_x(x_n, y_n) + q h k_1 f_y(x_n, y_n) + O(h^2)
$$

Since $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$:

$$
k_2 = f + p h f_x + q h f f_y + O(h^2)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the update formula for $y_{n+1}$.**

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ a k_1 + b \left( f + p h f_x + q h f f_y \right) + O(h^2) \right]
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h (a + b) f + h^2 b \left( p f_x + q f f_y \right) + O(h^3)
$$

**Step 3: Equate with the Taylor expansion of the true solution.**

$$
y(x_{n+1}) = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2} (f_x + f f_y) + O(h^3)
$$

Matching term-by-term:

$$
\boxed{a + b = 1} \quad \text{(coefficient of } h f \text{)}
$$

$$
\boxed{b p = \frac{1}{2}} \quad \text{(coefficient of } h^2 f_x \text{)}
$$

$$
\boxed{b q = \frac{1}{2}} \quad \text{(coefficient of } h^2 f f_y \text{)}
$$

**Step 4: Three equations, four unknowns ŌĆö infinitely many solutions.**

We are free to choose one parameter. Famous choices:

| Method | $a$ | $b$ | $p$ | $q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Midpoint | $0$ | $1$ | $1/2$ | $1/2$ |
| Heun's | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| Ralston's | $1/3$ | $2/3$ | $3/4$ | $3/4$ |

**Step 5: The Local Truncation Error for RK2.**

After dropping the $h^2$-matching terms, the next unmatched Taylor term gives:

$$
\text{LTE}_{RK2} = \frac{h^3}{6} y'''(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LTE} = O(h^3)
$$

This confirms RK2 is a **second-order method**.

## 3.3 Outline of Classical RK4 Derivation

For RK4, the ansatz involves 4 stages with 13 free parameters:

$$
k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)
$$

$$
k_2 = f(x_n + c_2 h, y_n + a_{21} h k_1)
$$

$$
k_3 = f(x_n + c_3 h, y_n + a_{31} h k_1 + a_{32} h k_2)
$$

$$
k_4 = f(x_n + c_4 h, y_n + a_{41} h k_1 + a_{42} h k_2 + a_{43} h k_3)
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h (b_1 k_1 + b_2 k_2 + b_3 k_3 + b_4 k_4)
$$

Expanding each $k_i$ in a multivariate Taylor series about $(x_n, y_n)$ through $O(h^3)$, substituting, and matching the result to the true Taylor series of $y_{n+1}$ through $O(h^4)$ yields **8 nonlinear order conditions** in the 13 unknowns. The classical solution is:

$$
c_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}, \; a_{21} = \tfrac{1}{2}, \; c_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}, \; a_{31} = 0, \; a_{32} = \tfrac{1}{2}, \; c_4 = 1, \; a_{41} = a_{42} = 0, \; a_{43} = 1
$$

$$
b_1 = \tfrac{1}{6}, \; b_2 = \tfrac{1}{3}, \; b_3 = \tfrac{1}{3}, \; b_4 = \tfrac{1}{6}
$$

This produces the canonical RK4 formula:

$$
k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)
$$

$$
k_2 = f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \tfrac{h k_1}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_3 = f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \tfrac{h k_2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_4 = f\!\left(x_n + h, \; y_n + h k_3\right)
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6} (k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4)
$$

The unmatched term in Taylor expansion is $O(h^5)$, giving LTE $= O(h^5)$ and GTE $= O(h^4)$.

## 3.4 Error Analysis ŌĆö Comparison Snapshot

Using $y' = y$, $y(0) = 1$ (true solution $y = e^x$) at $x = 1$ with $h = 0.1$:

| Method | Approximation at $x=1$ | Abs. Error | Cost (evaluations per step) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euler (RK1) | $2.5937$ | $0.1242$ | 1 |
| Midpoint (RK2) | $2.7140$ | $0.0039$ | 2 |
| Classical RK4 | $2.7183$ | $< 10^{-6}$ | 4 |

This empirical table illustrates the dramatic accuracy gains: **RK4 with $h=0.1$ is more accurate than Euler with $h=0.001$**, despite using fewer total function evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classical RK4 ŌĆö Algorithm Flow Architecture

The following flowchart captures the computational logic of one full RK4 step and its placement within a multi-step loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n0(["Start"]) --> n1[/"Input: x0, y0, h, n"/]
    n1 --> n2["Initialize: xi equals x0, yi equals y0, i equals 0"]
    n2 --> n3{"i less than n?"}
    n3 -- Yes --> n4["Stage 1: k1 equals f of xi and yi"]
    n4 --> n5["Stage 2: k2 equals f of xi plus h over 2 and yi plus h times k1 over 2"]
    n5 --> n6["Stage 3: k3 equals f of xi plus h over 2 and yi plus h times k2 over 2"]
    n6 --> n7["Stage 4: k4 equals f of xi plus h and yi plus h times k3"]
    n7 --> n8["yi plus 1 equals yi plus h over 6 times k1 plus 2 k2 plus 2 k3 plus k4"]
    n8 --> n9["xi plus 1 equals xi plus h, i equals i plus 1"]
    n9 --> n3
    n3 -- No --> n10[/"Output final value yn"/]
    n10 --> n11(["End"])
```

## 4.2 Order-Hierarchy Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["Explicit Euler\nOrder 1\n1 function eval per step"] --> nB["Midpoint Method\nOrder 2\n2 function evals per step"]
    nB --> nC["Heuns Method\nOrder 2\n2 function evals per step"]
    nC --> nD["Classical Runge Kutta RK4\nOrder 4\n4 function evals per step"]
    nD --> nE["Adaptive RKF45\nOrder 4 and 5 pair\n6 function evals per step"]
    nA --> nF["Accuracy rises with stage count"]
    nB --> nF
    nC --> nF
    nD --> nF
    nE --> nF
    nF --> nG["Cost also rises proportionally"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The right-hand branch of the second diagram shows a fundamental engineering trade-off: **higher-order RK methods buy accuracy at the cost of more slope evaluations per step**. The sweet spot for most non-stiff problems is RK4 ŌĆö RKF45 adds even more evaluations and is mainly justified when $f(x,y)$ is very expensive to compute.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Runge-Kutta method for solving the initial value problem $y' = f(x, y)$, $y(x_0) = y_0$. State the formula for the classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) method and mention its order of local truncation error.**

**Model Answer:**

The Runge-Kutta method is a single-step, multi-stage numerical procedure that advances the solution of an IVP from $y_n$ to $y_{n+1}$ by computing a *weighted average* of several evaluations of $f(x, y)$ at carefully sampled points inside $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$. Unlike Taylor series methods, it does not require analytical derivatives of $f$.

The classical RK4 formula is:

$$
k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)
$$

$$
k_2 = f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \tfrac{h k_1}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_3 = f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \tfrac{h k_2}{2}\right)
$$

$$
k_4 = f\!\left(x_n + h, \; y_n + h k_3\right)
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6} (k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4)
$$

The **local truncation error is $O(h^5)$** and the **global truncation error is $O(h^4)$**, making RK4 a *fourth-order method*.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the order conditions that a 2-stage explicit Runge-Kutta method must satisfy to be of order 2. Hence write the Butcher tableau for Heun's method.**

**Model Answer:**

For a 2-stage explicit RK method,
$y_{n+1} = y_n + h(b_1 k_1 + b_2 k_2)$ to have order 2, the two order conditions are:

$$
b_1 + b_2 = 1
$$

$$
b_2 c_2 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Heun's method is a specific 2-stage RK2 method with $c_2 = 1$, $a_{21} = 1$, $b_1 = 1/2$, $b_2 = 1/2$. Its Butcher tableau is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1/2 & 1/2
\end{array}
$$

The corresponding update is $y_{n+1} = y_n + (h/2)(k_1 + k_2)$ where $k_2$ uses Euler's predicted slope.

---

## ­¤ōĢ Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the second-order Runge-Kutta method by matching coefficients with the Taylor series expansion of $y(x_{n+1})$ up to $O(h^3)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method with step size $h = 0.2$, solve the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy + x^2$, $\;y(0) = 1$ to find $y(0.4)$. Also write a Python program that implements RK4 for this problem. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Solution to (a):**

We begin with the generic 2-stage explicit RK ansatz:
$k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$,
$k_2 = f(x_n + ph, y_n + qhk_1)$,
$y_{n+1} = y_n + h(a k_1 + b k_2)$.

**Multivariate Taylor expansion of $k_2$:**

$$
k_2 = f + ph f_x + qh k_1 f_y + O(h^2)
$$

Substituting:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h(a+b)f + h^2 b(p f_x + q f f_y) + O(h^3)
$$

Comparing with the Taylor expansion $y_{n+1} = y_n + hf + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_x + f f_y) + O(h^3)$:

- $a + b = 1$
- $bp = 1/2$
- $bq = 1/2$

These three equations in four unknowns admit infinitely many solutions ŌĆö three popular ones are Midpoint, Heun's, and Ralston's. Ō£ö

#### **Solution to (b):**

Given $f(x,y) = xy + x^2$, $\;x_0 = 0$, $\;y_0 = 1$, $\;h = 0.2$.

**Step 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.2$**

$$
k_1 = f(0, 1) = 0 \cdot 1 + 0^2 = 0
$$

$$
k_2 = f\!\left(0 + 0.1, \; 1 + \tfrac{0.2 \cdot 0}{2}\right) = f(0.1, 1) = 0.1 \cdot 1 + 0.01 = 0.11
$$

$$
k_3 = f\!\left(0.1, \; 1 + \tfrac{0.2 \cdot 0.11}{2}\right) = f(0.1, 1.011) = 0.1011 + 0.01 = 0.1111
$$

$$
k_4 = f(0.2, \; 1 + 0.2 \cdot 0.1111) = f(0.2, 1.02222) = 0.204444 + 0.04 = 0.244444
$$

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.2}{6}\left(0 + 2(0.11) + 2(0.1111) + 0.244444\right) = 1 + \frac{0.2}{6}(0.686644) = 1.022888
$$

**Step 2: From $x_1 = 0.2$ to $x_2 = 0.4$**

$$
k_1 = f(0.2, 1.022888) = 0.2 \cdot 1.022888 + 0.04 = 0.244578
$$

$$
k_2 = f(0.3, \; 1.022888 + 0.1 \cdot 0.244578) = f(0.3, 1.047346) = 0.314204 + 0.09 = 0.404204
$$

$$
k_3 = f(0.3, \; 1.022888 + 0.1 \cdot 0.404204) = f(0.3, 1.063308) = 0.318993 + 0.09 = 0.408993
$$

$$
k_4 = f(0.4, \; 1.022888 + 0.2 \cdot 0.408993) = f(0.4, 1.104687) = 0.441875 + 0.16 = 0.601875
$$

$$
y_2 = 1.022888 + \frac{0.2}{6}(0.244578 + 0.808408 + 0.817986 + 0.601875) = 1.022888 + 0.082428 = \mathbf{1.105316}
$$

**Python Implementation:**

```python
def rk4(f, x0, y0, h, n):
    """Classical RK4 integrator for dy/dx = f(x, y)."""
    x, y = x0, y0
    print(f"x = {x:.4f}, y = {y:.6f}")
    for i in range(n):
        k1 = f(x, y)
        k2 = f(x + h/2, y + h*k1/2)
        k3 = f(x + h/2, y + h*k2/2)
        k4 = f(x + h,   y + h*k3)
        y = y + (h/6)*(k1 + 2*k2 + 2*k3 + k4)
        x = x + h
        print(f"x = {x:.4f}, y = {y:.6f}")
    return y

f = lambda x, y: x*y + x**2
result = rk4(f, x0=0, y0=1, h=0.2, n=2)
print(f"\ny(0.4) Ōēł {result:.6f}")
```

**Final Answer:** $y(0.4) \approx \mathbf{1.1053}$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Butcher tableau. Write the Butcher tableau for: (i) Euler's method, (ii) Midpoint method, and (iii) Classical RK4. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Solve the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y^2$, $\;y(0) = 0$ using the RK4 method with $h = 0.2$ to find $y(0.4)$. Compare your answer with the Euler method result at the same point. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Solution to (a):**

The **Butcher tableau** is a compact tabular representation of any Runge-Kutta method. It encodes all coefficients $\{c_i, a_{ij}, b_i\}$ that govern how stages are formed and combined. The general layout is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 \\
c_2 & a_{21} \\
c_3 & a_{31} & a_{32} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \\
c_s & a_{s1} & a_{s2} & \cdots & a_{s,s-1} \\
\hline
& b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_s
\end{array}
$$

The first column holds the $c_i$ values (the time offsets), the lower-triangular block holds $a_{ij}$ coefficients, and the bottom row holds the weights $b_i$.

**Specific Tableaux:**

(i) **Euler (RK1):** $\;y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_1,\; k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$

$$
\begin{array}{c|c}
0 & \\
\hline
& 1
\end{array}
$$

(ii) **Midpoint (RK2):** $\;k_2 = f(x_n + h/2, y_n + h k_1/2)$

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & & \\
1/2 & 1/2 & \\
\hline
& 0 & 1
\end{array}
$$

(iii) **Classical RK4:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & & & & \\
1/2 & 1/2 & & & \\
1/2 & 0 & 1/2 & & \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & \\
\hline
& 1/6 & 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/6
\end{array}
$$

Ō£ö

#### **Solution to (b):**

Given $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$, $\;x_0 = 0$, $\;y_0 = 0$, $\;h = 0.2$.

**Step 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.2$**

$$
k_1 = 0^2 + 0^2 = 0
$$

$$
k_2 = (0.1)^2 + (0 + 0.1 \cdot 0)^2 = 0.01 + 0 = 0.01
$$

$$
k_3 = (0.1)^2 + (0 + 0.1 \cdot 0.01)^2 = 0.01 + 0.000001 = 0.010001
$$

$$
k_4 = (0.2)^2 + (0 + 0.2 \cdot 0.010001)^2 = 0.04 + 0.0000040008 = 0.0400040
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + \frac{0.2}{6}(0 + 0.02 + 0.020002 + 0.040004) = \frac{0.2}{6}(0.080006) = 0.002667
$$

**Step 2: From $x_1 = 0.2$ to $x_2 = 0.4$**

$$
k_1 = (0.2)^2 + (0.002667)^2 = 0.04 + 0.0000071 = 0.0400071
$$

$$
k_2 = (0.3)^2 + (0.002667 + 0.1 \cdot 0.0400071)^2 = 0.09 + (0.0066676)^2 = 0.09 + 0.0000445 = 0.0900445
$$

$$
k_3 = (0.3)^2 + (0.002667 + 0.1 \cdot 0.0900445)^2 = 0.09 + (0.0116713)^2 = 0.09 + 0.0001362 = 0.0901362
$$

$$
k_4 = (0.4)^2 + (0.002667 + 0.2 \cdot 0.0901362)^2 = 0.16 + (0.0206941)^2 = 0.16 + 0.000428 = 0.160428
$$

$$
y_2 = 0.002667 + \frac{0.2}{6}(0.0400071 + 0.180089 + 0.180272 + 0.160428) = 0.002667 + 0.018693 = \mathbf{0.021360}
$$

**Comparison with Euler's Method:**

Euler: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h(x_n^2 + y_n^2)$

- $y_1 = 0 + 0.2(0 + 0) = 0$
- $y_2 = 0 + 0.2(0.04 + 0) = 0.008$

| Method | $y(0.4)$ |
|---|---|
| Euler (RK1) | $0.008000$ |
| RK4 | $0.021360$ |

Since the true solution of $y' = x^2 + y^2$ grows faster than linear, the Euler method severely underestimates due to its first-order nature. RK4 captures the curvature far better. Ō£ö

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Misplaced arguments:** A very common error is writing $k_2 = f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + h/2)$ instead of $f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + h k_1/2)$. Always carry the **previous slope** into the $y$-argument. *(-1 mark per occurrence)*
> 2. **Incorrect weights in RK4:** Students often write $(h/8)(k_1 + k_2 + k_3 + k_4)$ (equal weights) ŌĆö but the correct weights are $(h/6)(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$. *(-1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting the index update:** Always advance $x_{n+1} = x_n + h$ *and* assign $y \leftarrow y_{n+1}$ before the next iteration. *(-0.5 mark)*
> 4. **Mixing local and global error terminology:** LTE is one order better than GTE for any RK method. Writing "RK4 has error $O(h^4)$" without specifying "global" is technically ambiguous and may cost partial credit.
> 5. **Arithmetic slip-ups:** Carry 4-5 significant figures in intermediate $k_i$ values to prevent the final answer from drifting by more than $10^{-4}$.

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Runge-Kutta methods are explicit, single-step, multi-stage ODE integrators of the form $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \sum b_i k_i$ with $k_i$ sampled intelligently inside the step.
- **Euler = RK1:** The simplest RK method, with GTE $= O(h)$.
- **RK2 family:** Midpoint, Heun's, and Ralston's all satisfy two order conditions: $\sum b_i = 1$ and $\sum b_i c_i = 1/2$. Their GTE is $O(h^2)$.
- **Classical RK4:** Four stage evaluations, weights $(1/6, 1/3, 1/3, 1/6)$, GTE $= O(h^4)$ ŌĆö the workhorse of scientific computing.
- **Butcher tableau:** The compact array $\{c_i, a_{ij}, b_i\}$ fully specifies an RK method.
- **Cost vs. accuracy:** Higher order buys accuracy at the cost of more $f$-evaluations per step. RK4 is the sweet spot for general-purpose non-stiff problems.
- **Stability:** RK4 has a finite region of absolute stability in the complex plane. For stiff ODEs, implicit methods (e.g., backward Euler, Radau) are preferred.
- **Why not Taylor?** RK methods achieve high-order accuracy **without** needing analytical derivatives of $f(x, y)$ ŌĆö a decisive advantage in real applications.
- **Step-size selection:** Use Richardson extrapolation or embedded methods (RKF45) to adapt $h$ automatically.
- **When to use:** RK4 is appropriate for smooth, non-stiff IVPs with moderate accuracy demands (e.g., $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$ tolerance). Avoid it for stiff systems or problems with discontinuities.
- **Examiner hot-spots:** Coefficient derivation from Taylor series, Butcher tableau construction, and the RK4 numerical application are the most frequently tested areas in KTU 2024 scheme end-semester exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multistep Methods: Adams-Bashforth and Adams-Moulton Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multistep Methods: Adams-Bashforth and Adams-Moulton Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Linear Multistep Method (LMM)** is a numerical technique for solving the Initial Value Problem (IVP) of an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE),

$$y'(t) = f(t, y(t)), \quad y(t_0) = y_0,$$

in which the new approximation $y_{n+1}$ is computed as a linear combination of **previously computed function/derivative values** $f_{n-k} = f(t_{n-k}, y_{n-k})$, rather than solely from the current value as in single-step RungeŌĆōKutta methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adams Family of Multistep Methods (KTU Module 4 Highlight):**
> - **AdamsŌĆōBashforth (AB) Methods** ŌĆö *Explicit* multistep methods (use only past values $f_n, f_{n-1}, \ldots$).
> - **AdamsŌĆōMoulton (AM) Methods** ŌĆö *Implicit* multistep methods (use $f_{n+1}$ along with past values).

A general $k$-step Adams linear multistep method is written in the canonical form:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{k} \alpha_j \, y_{n+j} = h \sum_{j=0}^{k} \beta_j \, f_{n+j}, \qquad \alpha_k = 1,$$

where the coefficients $\alpha_j$ and $\beta_j$ determine the order, stability, and explicitness/implicitness of the scheme.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "GPS Route Re-Routing with Memory"**
> Imagine you are driving from city A to city B using GPS navigation.
> - A **single-step method** (Euler, RK4) is like a GPS that *recalculates the entire route* at every turn, using only your current position and speed.
> - An **Adams multistep method** is like a smarter GPS that **remembers your last 3ŌĆō4 velocity/speed readings** and uses that *historical trend* to project your next position more accurately, with far less computational effort per step.
> - **AdamsŌĆōBashforth (Explicit)**: "From where I am now, and how fast I was going in the last few intervals, predict where I'll be next." ŌåÆ No re-routing needed.
> - **AdamsŌĆōMoulton (Implicit)**: "I need to know where I'll be next to compute the next step, but I can estimate it first and then correct it." ŌåÆ A *two-stage* prediction-correction.

### 1.3 Why We Need Multistep Methods

| Drawback of Single-Step (RK) Methods | How Multistep Methods Solve It |
|---|---|
| Re-evaluates $f(t,y)$ multiple times per step (e.g., 4 evaluations for RK4) | Uses **already-computed $f$ values**; only **one new $f_{n+1}$** evaluation per step |
| Lower accuracy per function evaluation | Higher order of accuracy with same number of evaluations |
| Wastes prior computational work | Fully **re-uses** past derivative information |

### 1.4 Physical / Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Orbital mechanics & spacecraft trajectory propagation** (Adams methods used in NASA's classical integration routines)
> - **Chemical reaction kinetics** (stiff systems)
> - **Control systems simulation** (real-time ODE solvers)
> - **Structural dynamics** (vibration analysis)
> - **Electrical circuit transient analysis** (SPICE-class simulators)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö Origin from the Integral Equation

Starting from the ODE $y'(t) = f(t, y(t))$, integrating from $t_n$ to $t_{n+1}$:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \int_{t_n}^{t_{n+1}} f(\tau, y(\tau)) \, d\tau.$$

Substituting $\tau = t_n + sh$, $d\tau = h\,ds$ (with $s \in [0,1]$):

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \int_0^1 f(t_n + sh, y(t_n + sh)) \, ds.$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The entire Adams family is obtained by approximating the integral using a polynomial interpolant through a chosen set of nodes.**

- **AdamsŌĆōBashforth (Explicit):** interpolate $f$ at the **$k$ past points** $t_n, t_{n-1}, \ldots, t_{n-k+1}$.
- **AdamsŌĆōMoulton (Implicit):** interpolate $f$ at the **current point $t_{n+1}$** along with $k-1$ past points.

### 2.2 Newton's Backward Difference Representation (Building Block)

The interpolating polynomial using Newton's backward difference formula anchored at $t_n$:

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx f_n + s \nabla f_n + \frac{s(s+1)}{2!} \nabla^2 f_n + \frac{s(s+1)(s+2)}{3!} \nabla^3 f_n + \cdots,$$

where the backward differences are:

$$\nabla^0 f_n = f_n, \quad \nabla f_n = f_n - f_{n-1}, \quad \nabla^2 f_n = f_n - 2f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}, \quad \nabla^3 f_n = f_n - 3f_{n-1} + 3f_{n-2} - f_{n-3}.$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2.3(a) ŌĆö AdamsŌĆōBashforth (Explicit) Methods

| Order $p$ | Steps $k$ | Formula | Local Truncation Error $\tau_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f_n$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ |
| 2 | 2 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{3}{2} f_n - \tfrac{1}{2} f_{n-1} \right]$ | $\tfrac{5}{12} h^3 y'''(\xi)$ |
| 3 | 3 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{23}{12} f_n - \tfrac{16}{12} f_{n-1} + \tfrac{5}{12} f_{n-2} \right]$ | $\tfrac{3}{8} h^4 y^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| 4 | 4 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{55}{24} f_n - \tfrac{59}{24} f_{n-1} + \tfrac{37}{24} f_{n-2} - \tfrac{9}{24} f_{n-3} \right]$ | $\tfrac{251}{720} h^5 y^{(5)}(\xi)$ |

#### Table 2.3(b) ŌĆö AdamsŌĆōMoulton (Implicit) Methods

| Order $p$ | Steps $k$ | Formula | Local Truncation Error $\tau_{n+1}$ |
|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | 1 (Backward Euler) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f_{n+1}$ | $-\tfrac{1}{2} h^2 y''(\xi)$ |
| 2 | 2 (Trapezoidal) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{1}{2} f_{n+1} + \tfrac{1}{2} f_n \right]$ | $-\tfrac{1}{12} h^3 y'''(\xi)$ |
| 3 | 3 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{5}{12} f_{n+1} + \tfrac{8}{12} f_n - \tfrac{1}{12} f_{n-1} \right]$ | $-\tfrac{1}{24} h^4 y^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| 4 | 4 | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \tfrac{9}{24} f_{n+1} + \tfrac{19}{24} f_n - \tfrac{5}{24} f_{n-1} + \tfrac{1}{24} f_{n-2} \right]$ | $-\tfrac{19}{720} h^5 y^{(5)}(\xi)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** AdamsŌĆōMoulton has roughly **half the error** of the corresponding AdamsŌĆōBashforth method of the same step-count, but it is **implicit** (requires solving for $y_{n+1}$). The standard fix is the **PredictorŌĆōCorrector** scheme.

### 2.4 PredictorŌĆōCorrector Algorithm (Hamming/Milne Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 4-step PredictorŌĆōCorrector Pair:**
>
> **Predictor** (AdamsŌĆōBashforth 4-step ŌĆö explicit):
> $$y_{n+1}^{(p)} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{24}\left[ 55 f_n - 59 f_{n-1} + 37 f_{n-2} - 9 f_{n-3} \right]$$
>
> **Evaluate** $f_{n+1}^{(p)} = f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}^{(p)})$.
>
> **Corrector** (AdamsŌĆōMoulton 4-step ŌĆö implicit):
> $$y_{n+1}^{(c)} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{24}\left[ 9 f_{n+1}^{(p)} + 19 f_n - 5 f_{n-1} + f_{n-2} \right]$$
>
> **Error estimate** (Milne's device):
> $$E_{n+1} \approx \tfrac{1}{15}\left[ y_{n+1}^{(c)} - y_{n+1}^{(p)} \right] = \mathcal{O}(h^5)$$

### 2.5 Stability & Convergence ŌĆö KTU Key Point

- An LMM is **zero-stable** iff its first characteristic polynomial $\rho(\zeta) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \alpha_j \zeta^j$ satisfies the **Root Condition**: every root $\zeta_i$ has $\vert \zeta_i \vert \le 1$, and roots with $\vert \zeta_i \vert = 1$ are simple.
- Both AdamsŌĆōBashforth and AdamsŌĆōMoulton families are **zero-stable** by construction.
- A method of order $p$ has **convergence** if it is consistent ($p \ge 1$) **and** zero-stable (Dahlquist's equivalence theorem).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AdamsŌĆōBashforth** is the workhorse in **non-stiff IVP solvers** (e.g., `ode113` in MATLAB).
- **AdamsŌĆōMoulton / BDF (Backward Differentiation Formulas)** dominate **stiff system solvers** (e.g., `ode15s`, `LSODA`).
- Used in **digital real-time simulation** of flight control, automotive ECUs, and power-electronics converters.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below follow the canonical KTU Module 4 derivation strategy: integrate the Newton backward-difference polynomial representation of $f(t,y)$ over the appropriate interval.

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2-Step AdamsŌĆōBashforth Method (Order 2)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the integral form.**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \int_0^1 f(t_n + sh, y(t_n + sh)) \, ds.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Interpolate $f$ through nodes $t_n$ and $t_{n-1}$ using the first-degree Newton backward polynomial.**

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx f_n + s \nabla f_n = f_n + s (f_n - f_{n-1}) = (1 + s) f_n - s \, f_{n-1}.$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the integral of each coefficient with respect to $s$ from $0$ to $1$.**

For coefficient of $f_n$:

$$\int_0^1 (1 + s) \, ds = \left[ s + \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = 1 + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{2}.$$

For coefficient of $f_{n-1}$:

$$\int_0^1 s \, ds = \left[ \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2}.$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Assemble the formula.**

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \frac{3}{2} f_n - \frac{1}{2} f_{n-1} \right] \,}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Truncation Error.**

The next omitted term in the integrand is $\dfrac{s(s+1)}{2} \nabla^2 f_n$. Its integral from $0$ to $1$ is $\dfrac{5}{12}$. Therefore:

$$\tau_{n+1} = \frac{5}{12} h^3 y'''(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Order} = 2.$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the 3-Step AdamsŌĆōBashforth Method (Order 3)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Use the second-degree Newton backward polynomial anchored at $t_n$ (nodes $t_n, t_{n-1}, t_{n-2}$).**

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx f_n + s \nabla f_n + \frac{s(s+1)}{2} \nabla^2 f_n.$$

Substituting $\nabla f_n = f_n - f_{n-1}$ and $\nabla^2 f_n = f_n - 2f_{n-1} + f_{n-2}$:

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx \left(1 + s + \frac{s^2 + s}{2}\right) f_n + \left(-s - s^2 - s\right) f_{n-1} + \frac{s^2 + s}{2} f_{n-2}.$$

Simplifying the coefficients:

$$= \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) f_n + \left(-2s - s^2\right) f_{n-1} + \frac{s(s+1)}{2} f_{n-2}.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Integrate each coefficient from $s=0$ to $s=1$.**

Coefficient of $f_n$:

$$\int_0^1 \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) ds = \left[ s + \frac{3s^2}{4} + \frac{s^3}{6} \right]_0^1 = 1 + \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{12 + 9 + 2}{12} = \frac{23}{12}.$$

Coefficient of $f_{n-1}$:

$$\int_0^1 (-2s - s^2) \, ds = \left[ -s^2 - \frac{s^3}{3} \right]_0^1 = -1 - \frac{1}{3} = -\frac{4}{3} = -\frac{16}{12}.$$

Coefficient of $f_{n-2}$:

$$\int_0^1 \frac{s(s+1)}{2} \, ds = \frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{s^3}{3} + \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{5}{6} = \frac{5}{12}.$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assemble the formula.**

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \frac{23}{12} f_n - \frac{16}{12} f_{n-1} + \frac{5}{12} f_{n-2} \right] \,}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Truncation Error.**

Omitted term: $\dfrac{s(s+1)(s+2)}{6} \nabla^3 f_n$ integrated from $0$ to $1$ gives $\dfrac{19}{24}$. Thus:

$$\tau_{n+1} = \frac{3}{8} h^4 y^{(4)}(\xi) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Order} = 3.$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the 2-Step AdamsŌĆōMoulton Method (Trapezoidal Rule, Order 2)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Interpolate $f$ at the two nodes $t_{n+1}$ and $t_n$ (linear Lagrange interpolation).**

Parametrize $t$ with $s = \dfrac{t - t_n}{h}$, so $s$ runs from $0$ to $1$ as $t$ moves from $t_n$ to $t_{n+1}$. The linear interpolant:

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx (1 - s) f_n + s \, f_{n+1}.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Integrate from $s=0$ to $s=1$.**

$$\int_0^1 (1 - s) f_n \, ds = \left[ s - \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_0^1 f_n = \frac{1}{2} f_n.$$

$$\int_0^1 s \, f_{n+1} \, ds = \left[ \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_0^1 f_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2} f_{n+1}.$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assemble the formula.**

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\left( f_n + f_{n+1} \right) \,}$$

This is precisely the **Trapezoidal Rule** for ODEs. Truncation error: $-\dfrac{1}{12} h^3 y'''(\xi)$ ŌåÆ **Order 2**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the 3-Step AdamsŌĆōMoulton Method (Order 3)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Anchor the Newton backward polynomial at $t_{n+1}$ with nodes $t_{n+1}, t_n, t_{n-1}$.**

Let $s = \dfrac{t - t_{n+1}}{h}$. Then $s = 0$ at $t_{n+1}$, $s = -1$ at $t_n$, $s = -2$ at $t_{n-1}$.

$$f(t_{n+1} + sh) \approx f_{n+1} + s \nabla f_{n+1} + \frac{s(s+1)}{2} \nabla^2 f_{n+1}.$$

Substituting $\nabla f_{n+1} = f_{n+1} - f_n$ and $\nabla^2 f_{n+1} = f_{n+1} - 2f_n + f_{n-1}$:

$$f \approx \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) f_{n+1} + (-2s - s^2) f_n + \frac{s(s+1)}{2} f_{n-1}.$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Integrate from $s = -1$ to $s = 0$.**

Coefficient of $f_{n+1}$:

$$\int_{-1}^{0} \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) ds = \left[ s + \frac{3s^2}{4} + \frac{s^3}{6} \right]_{-1}^{0} = 0 - \left(-1 + \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{6}\right) = \frac{12 - 9 + 2}{12} = \frac{5}{12}.$$

Coefficient of $f_n$:

$$\int_{-1}^{0} (-2s - s^2) \, ds = \left[ -s^2 - \frac{s^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{0} = 0 - \left(-1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{2}{3} = \frac{8}{12}.$$

Coefficient of $f_{n-1}$:

$$\int_{-1}^{0} \frac{s(s+1)}{2} \, ds = \frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{s^3}{3} + \frac{s^2}{2} \right]_{-1}^{0} = \frac{1}{2}\left[0 - \left(-\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{1}{12}.$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assemble the formula.**

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = y_n + h \left[ \frac{5}{12} f_{n+1} + \frac{8}{12} f_n - \frac{1}{12} f_{n-1} \right] \,}$$

Truncation error: $-\dfrac{1}{24} h^4 y^{(4)}(\xi)$ ŌåÆ **Order 3**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | AdamsŌĆōBashforth (AB) | AdamsŌĆōMoulton (AM) |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Explicit | Implicit |
| Computes $f_{n+1}$ directly? | No | Yes (requires solving) |
| Order vs. step-count | $p = k$ | $p = k+1$ |
| Stability region (real axis) | Smaller | Larger |
| Error constant (4-step) | $\tfrac{251}{720}$ | $\tfrac{19}{720}$ |
| Suitability for stiff systems | Poor | Good (with BDF extension) |
| Typical use | Predictor | Corrector |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Multistep Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Numerical ODE Solvers for IVP] --> B[Single-Step Methods]
    A --> C[Multistep Methods]
    B --> B1[Euler Method]
    B --> B2[Runge-Kutta RK2 RK4]
    C --> D[Linear Multistep Methods LMM]
    D --> E[Adams Family]
    D --> F[Backward Differentiation BDF]
    E --> G[Adams-Bashforth Explicit]
    E --> H[Adams-Moulton Implicit]
    G --> G1[2-Step AB Order 2]
    G --> G2[3-Step AB Order 3]
    G --> G3[4-Step AB Order 4]
    H --> H1[Trapezoidal 2-Step AM]
    H --> H2[3-Step AM Order 3]
    H --> H3[4-Step AM Order 4]
    G1 --> I[Predictor-Corrector Pair]
    H1 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Milne Device Error Estimate]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö PredictorŌĆōCorrector Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: y_n, f_n, f_{n-1}, f_{n-2}, f_{n-3}] --> B[Stage 1: PREDICTOR]
    B --> B1[Apply 4-Step Adams-Bashforth]
    B1 --> C[Compute y_{n+1 to the power p}]
    C --> D[Evaluate f_{n+1 to the power p} = f at predicted y]
    D --> E[Stage 2: CORRECTOR]
    E --> E1[Apply 4-Step Adams-Moulton with f_{n+1 to the power p}]
    E1 --> F[Compute corrected y_{n+1 to the power c}]
    F --> G[Stage 3: ERROR CHECK]
    G --> G1[Milne Device: E = y_c - y_p divided by 15]
    G1 --> H{Is Error within tolerance?}
    H -->|No| I[Reduce step size h and retry]
    H -->|Yes| J[Accept y_{n+1} and advance to n+1]
    J --> K[Update history buffer]
    K --> A
```

### 4.3 Comparison of Method Cost vs Accuracy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Euler: 1 eval/step, O(h)] --> A2[Heun RK2: 2 evals/step, O(h^2)]
    A2 --> A3[RK4: 4 evals/step, O(h^4)]
    A2 --> A4[AB2: 1 new eval/step, O(h^2)]
    A4 --> A5[AB3: 1 new eval/step, O(h^3)]
    A5 --> A6[AB4: 1 new eval/step, O(h^4)]
    A3 -.matches.-> A6
    A6 --> A7[AB4 predictor plus AM4 corrector: 2 new evals, O(h^5)]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** AdamsŌĆōBashforth 4-step achieves the same accuracy as RK4 (which costs 4 function evaluations per step) using only **1 new function evaluation per step** ŌĆö at the price of needing 4 *bootstrapping* values from a separate starter method (e.g., RK4 or exact Taylor series).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP]`
**Distinguish between single-step and multistep methods for solving an initial value problem. State two advantages of multistep methods over RungeŌĆōKutta methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Step (RK) | Multistep (Adams) |
|---|---|---|
| Information used | Only $y_n, f_n$ | $y_n, f_n, f_{n-1}, \ldots, f_{n-k+1}$ |
| New $f$ evaluations/step | $\ge 2$ (e.g., 4 for RK4) | **1** new evaluation |
| Memory required | Minimal | Must store $k$ past values |

**Two advantages:**
1. **Computational efficiency:** Once started, only **one new $f$ evaluation** is needed per step, drastically reducing function-call cost.
2. **Higher order per evaluation:** A 4-step AB4 method matches RK4 in accuracy with one-quarter the function evaluations.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model QP]`
**Why is AdamsŌĆōBashforth called an explicit method and AdamsŌĆōMoulton called an implicit method? What is a predictorŌĆōcorrector scheme?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **AdamsŌĆōBashforth (Explicit):** The update formula $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \sum \beta_j f_{n+1-j}$ contains $f$ only at *known* previous points $t_n, t_{n-1}, \ldots$. Hence $y_{n+1}$ is computed **directly** without solving an equation.
- **AdamsŌĆōMoulton (Implicit):** The update contains $f_{n+1} = f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1})$, i.e., the unknown $y_{n+1}$ appears on **both sides**. It must be solved (often by fixed-point or Newton's method) ŌĆö it is **implicit**.
- **PredictorŌĆōCorrector:** A two-stage hybrid: an **explicit** method (e.g., AB4) is first used to *predict* a provisional $y_{n+1}^{(p)}$, then an **implicit** method (e.g., AM4) uses that prediction to *correct* it. It combines the simplicity of explicit schemes with the higher accuracy of implicit ones, and yields a built-in error estimate (Milne's device).

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model QP ŌĆö Module 4 Choice]`

**(a)** *Derive the 3-step AdamsŌĆōBashforth formula. State its local truncation error. (7 marks)*

**Solution:**

We start with the integral form of the ODE:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \int_0^1 f(t_n + sh, y(t_n + sh)) \, ds.$$

Using the second-degree Newton backward difference polynomial anchored at $t_n$ (nodes $t_n, t_{n-1}, t_{n-2}$):

$$f(t_n + sh) \approx f_n + s\nabla f_n + \frac{s(s+1)}{2}\nabla^2 f_n.$$

Expanding in terms of $f_n, f_{n-1}, f_{n-2}$:

$$f \approx \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) f_n - (2s + s^2) f_{n-1} + \frac{s(s+1)}{2} f_{n-2}.$$

Integrating term by term from $0$ to $1$:

$$\int_0^1 \left(1 + \frac{3s}{2} + \frac{s^2}{2}\right) ds = \frac{23}{12}, \quad \int_0^1 (2s + s^2) ds = \frac{4}{3} = \frac{16}{12}, \quad \int_0^1 \frac{s(s+1)}{2} ds = \frac{5}{12}.$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{12}\left[23 f_n - 16 f_{n-1} + 5 f_{n-2}\right] \,}$$

**Local truncation error:** $\tau_{n+1} = \dfrac{3}{8} h^4 y^{(4)}(\xi)$ ŌåÆ method is of **order 3**.

---

**(b)** *Derive the 2-step AdamsŌĆōMoulton (trapezoidal) formula and the 3-step AdamsŌĆōMoulton formula. Compare their orders of accuracy. (7 marks)*

**Solution (2-step / Trapezoidal):** Linear interpolation of $f$ between $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$ gives $f(t_n + sh) \approx (1-s)f_n + s f_{n+1}$. Integrating:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h\int_0^1\left[(1-s)f_n + s f_{n+1}\right]ds = y_n + \frac{h}{2}(f_n + f_{n+1}).$$

**Solution (3-step):** Anchoring Newton backward at $t_{n+1}$ with nodes $t_{n+1}, t_n, t_{n-1}$ (so $s$ ranges over $[-1, 0]$) and integrating yields:

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h\left[\frac{5}{12} f_{n+1} + \frac{8}{12} f_n - \frac{1}{12} f_{n-1}\right].$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | Steps | Order $p$ | Error constant |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| 2-step AM (trapezoidal) | 2 | 2 | $-\dfrac{1}{12}$ |
| 3-step AM | 3 | 3 | $-\dfrac{1}{24}$ |

The 3-step AM is **one order higher** for one additional past value and the same implicit structure. Both are **implicit** and require an iterative solve for $y_{n+1}$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP ŌĆö Module 4 Choice]`

**(a)** *Using the 3-step AdamsŌĆōBashforth predictor and the 3-step AdamsŌĆōMoulton corrector, develop a predictorŌĆōcorrector algorithm. Compute $y_1$ given $y_0 = 1$, $y_{-1} = 0.9$, $y_{-2} = 0.85$, $h = 0.1$ for the IVP $y' = -2y + t$, with $t \in [0, 1]$. (7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Algorithm:**

> **Predictor (AB3, explicit):**
> $$y_{n+1}^{(p)} = y_n + \frac{h}{12}\left[23 f_n - 16 f_{n-1} + 5 f_{n-2}\right]$$
>
> **Evaluate:** $f_{n+1}^{(p)} = f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}^{(p)})$.
>
> **Corrector (AM3, implicit):**
> $$y_{n+1}^{(c)} = y_n + \frac{h}{12}\left[5 f_{n+1}^{(p)} + 8 f_n - 1 f_{n-1}\right]$$

**Numerical computation at $n=0$ (compute $y_1$):**

Compute $f$ values at the three known nodes:

$$f_0 = f(0, 1) = -2(1) + 0 = -2,$$
$$f_{-1} = f(-0.1, 0.9) = -2(0.9) + (-0.1) = -1.9,$$
$$f_{-2} = f(-0.2, 0.85) = -2(0.85) + (-0.2) = -1.9.$$

**Predictor step:**

$$y_1^{(p)} = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}\big[23(-2) - 16(-1.9) + 5(-1.9)\big] = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}\big[-46 + 30.4 - 9.5\big] = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}(-25.1).$$

$$y_1^{(p)} = 1 - \frac{2.51}{12} = 1 - 0.20917 = 0.79083.$$

**Evaluate $f_1^{(p)}$ at $t_1 = 0.1$:**

$$f_1^{(p)} = -2(0.79083) + 0.1 = -1.58166 + 0.1 = -1.48166.$$

**Corrector step:**

$$y_1^{(c)} = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}\big[5(-1.48166) + 8(-2) - 1(-1.9)\big] = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}\big[-7.4083 - 16 + 1.9\big].$$

$$y_1^{(c)} = 1 + \frac{0.1}{12}(-21.5083) = 1 - \frac{2.15083}{12} = 1 - 0.17924 = 0.82076.$$

**Error estimate (Milne device):** $E_1 = \dfrac{\vert y_1^{(c)} - y_1^{(p)} \vert}{11} = \dfrac{\vert 0.82076 - 0.79083 \vert}{11} \approx 0.00272.$

$$\boxed{\, y_1 \approx 0.8208 \pm 0.0027 \,}$$

---

**(b)** *Explain the concept of zero-stability for a linear multistep method. Verify zero-stability of the 3-step AdamsŌĆōBashforth method. (7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Zero-stability definition:** A linear multistep method is **zero-stable** if the perturbation error introduced in any one step remains bounded as the integration proceeds, in the limit $h \to 0$. The condition is checked via the **Root Condition** on the first characteristic polynomial $\rho(\zeta) = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \alpha_j \zeta^j$:

1. Every root $\zeta_i$ of $\rho(\zeta)$ satisfies $\vert \zeta_i \vert \le 1$.
2. Every root on the unit circle ($\vert \zeta_i \vert = 1$) is **simple** (multiplicity 1).

**Verification for 3-step AdamsŌĆōBashforth:**

The 3-step AB method in standard form:

$$y_{n+3} - y_{n+2} = \frac{h}{12}\big[23 f_{n+2} - 16 f_{n+1} + 5 f_n\big].$$

The first characteristic polynomial is:

$$\rho(\zeta) = \zeta^3 - \zeta^2 = \zeta^2(\zeta - 1).$$

**Roots:** $\zeta_1 = 0$ (multiplicity 2), $\zeta_2 = 1$ (multiplicity 1).

All roots satisfy $\vert \zeta_i \vert \le 1$, and the only root on the unit circle ($\zeta = 1$) is simple.

$$\therefore \text{3-step AdamsŌĆōBashforth is } \boxed{\text{zero-stable.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mistakes Where Marks Are Lost):**
> 1. **Forgetting the blank lines around `$$` blocks** ŌĆö many students embed equations in a single line; examiners expect proper LaTeX display blocks.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** ŌĆö this *destroys* the table layout. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.
> 3. **Confusing explicitness with accuracy** ŌĆö students often claim "implicit = always better." Correct answer: implicit methods have a *larger* stability region and *smaller* error constant, but require solving an equation each step.
> 4. **Skipping the lower limit of integration in the AM derivation** ŌĆö many students integrate from $0$ to $1$ instead of from $-1$ to $0$ for AM3, producing the **wrong coefficients**.
> 5. **Not verifying the Root Condition explicitly** in zero-stability problems ŌĆö merely stating the roots is *not* enough; explicitly check $\vert \zeta_i \vert \le 1$ and simplicity on the unit circle.
> 6. **Mixing up the predictor and corrector formulas** in a 14-mark problem ŌĆö the predictor uses $f_n, f_{n-1}, f_{n-2}, f_{n-3}$; the corrector uses $f_{n+1}^{(p)}, f_n, f_{n-1}, f_{n-2}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Multistep methods** reuse previously computed $f$-values ŌåÆ *computationally cheaper* than RK per step.
> - **AdamsŌĆōBashforth (Explicit):** $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \beta_j f_{n-j}$ ŌĆö direct, no equation solve.
> - **AdamsŌĆōMoulton (Implicit):** contains $f_{n+1}$ ŌĆö requires iterative (Newton / fixed-point) solution.
> - **Order:** AM methods are **one order higher** than AB methods with the same number of past steps.
> - **AB2:** $\dfrac{h}{2}(3f_n - f_{n-1})$;  **AB3:** $\dfrac{h}{12}(23f_n - 16f_{n-1} + 5f_{n-2})$;  **AB4:** $\dfrac{h}{24}(55f_n - 59f_{n-1} + 37f_{n-2} - 9f_{n-3})$.
> - **AM2 (trapezoidal):** $\dfrac{h}{2}(f_n + f_{n+1})$;  **AM3:** $\dfrac{h}{12}(5f_{n+1} + 8f_n - f_{n-1})$;  **AM4:** $\dfrac{h}{24}(9f_{n+1} + 19f_n - 5f_{n-1} + f_{n-2})$.
> - **Starter values:** A multistep method needs $k$ bootstrapping values; commonly obtained from a single-step method (e.g., RK4) or Taylor series.
> - **PredictorŌĆōCorrector:** Combines AB (predictor) and AM (corrector); provides built-in error estimate via **Milne's device** $E \approx \dfrac{\vert y_c - y_p \vert}{2^p - 1}$.
> - **Zero-stability:** Check $\rho(\zeta)$ has all roots $\vert \zeta \vert \le 1$, with unit-circle roots being simple.
> - **Dahlquist Equivalence Theorem:** *Consistency + zero-stability $\Leftrightarrow$ convergence*.
> - **Engineering use:** Non-stiff problems ŌåÆ AB / AM predictorŌĆōcorrector; Stiff problems ŌåÆ AM or BDF.
> - **Comparison baseline:** AB4 (1 new $f$/step, $O(h^4)$) Ōēł RK4 (4 new $f$/step, $O(h^4)$) ŌĆö same accuracy at $\frac{1}{4}$ the cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Value Problems: Shooting Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boundary Value Problems: Shooting Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Boundary Value Problem (BVP)** is a differential equation (or system of differential equations) together with a set of additional constraints, called **boundary conditions**, specified at more than one independent variable point. For a second-order ordinary differential equation (ODE), a general linear BVP is mathematically expressed as:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = f(x, y, y'), \quad a \leq x \leq b$$

subject to the two-point boundary conditions:

$$y(a) = \alpha, \quad y(b) = \beta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition ŌĆö Boundary Value Problem (BVP)**
> A BVP specifies the dependent variable $y$ (and/or its derivatives) at two or more distinct values of the independent variable $x$. This is fundamentally different from an Initial Value Problem (IVP), where all conditions are prescribed at a *single* point $x = a$. The boundary points $a$ and $b$ are the two ends of the domain of interest.

The **Shooting Method** is a numerical technique that converts a BVP into one or more IVPs by *guessing* the missing initial condition(s), solving the resulting IVP using a standard step-by-step method (e.g., RungeŌĆōKutta 4th order), and then *iteratively adjusting* the guess until the boundary condition at the far end is satisfied. The name originates from the analogy of **shooting a cannon** to hit a target.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cannon Shooter**
> Imagine you are an artillery gunner tasked with hitting a target at a known horizontal distance $x = b$ and known height $y = \beta$. You know the cannon's launch position $(x = a, y = \alpha)$ but **do not know the correct elevation angle** (i.e., the initial slope $y'(a)$).
>
> - **First shot:** You guess an angle $\theta_1$, fire, and observe where the projectile lands. Suppose it falls *short* of the target.
> - **Second shot:** You adjust the angle to $\theta_2$, fire again, and observe. Suppose it now flies *over* the target.
> - **Third shot:** You linearly interpolate between $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to find $\theta_3$ that should hit the target.
>
> This iterative adjustment of the *initial guess* until the *final boundary condition* is satisfied is precisely the **Shooting Method**. The "trajectory" of the projectile is the solution curve $y(x)$, and the target is the second boundary condition $y(b) = \beta$.

### 1.3 Classification: Linear vs. Non-Linear BVPs

| BVP Type | Mathematical Form | Difficulty of Shooting |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear BVP** | $y'' = p(x)\,y' + q(x)\,y + r(x)$ | Yields a *linear* shooting equation; solvable in 2 IVP evaluations |
| **Non-Linear BVP** | $y'' = f(x, y, y')$ with $f$ non-linear in $y, y'$ | Requires iterative root-finding (Secant/Newton) on a non-linear shooting function |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Tolerance Metric ŌĆö Convergence Criterion $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$**
> The shooting iteration is considered converged when the absolute difference between the computed boundary value $y_{\text{computed}}(b)$ and the desired boundary value $\beta$ is less than a pre-specified tolerance:
> $$\left\vert \, y_{\text{computed}}(b) - \beta \, \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$$
> A standard engineering tolerance is $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ (or $10^{-4}$ for rough engineering estimates).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Problem with BVPs

Standard IVP solvers (Euler, RK4, AdamsŌĆōBashforth) require the value of $y$ and $y'$ **at a single starting point** $x = a$. In a BVP, we are only given:
- $y(a) = \alpha$  ŌåÉ *known*
- $y(b) = \beta$   ŌåÉ *known*

We **do not** know $y'(a) = s$ (the initial slope). The shooting method's central idea is to **treat $s = y'(a)$ as an unknown parameter to be determined** by enforcing the second boundary condition.

### 2.2 Reformulation as a First-Order System

Every second-order ODE $y'' = f(x, y, y')$ is converted into a system of two first-order ODEs by introducing $y_1 = y$ and $y_2 = y'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1' &= y_2 \\
y_2' &= f(x, y_1, y_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

with the initial conditions at $x = a$:

$$y_1(a) = \alpha, \quad y_2(a) = s \quad (\text{unknown})$$

### 2.3 Algorithm: The Shooting Method (General)

1. **Guess** an initial slope $s^{(0)} = y'(a)$.
2. **Solve** the resulting IVP from $x = a$ to $x = b$ using RK4 (or any standard ODE solver).
3. **Compute** the resulting value $y_{\text{end}} = y(b; s^{(0)})$.
4. **Check** if $\left\vert y_{\text{end}} - \beta \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$. If yes, **stop**.
5. **Update** the guess $s$ using a root-finding rule (Secant or Newton) and return to Step 2.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Linear Shooting Method

For the linear second-order ODE:

$$y'' = p(x)\,y' + q(x)\,y + r(x), \quad y(a) = \alpha, \; y(b) = \beta$$

| Step | Equation | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| 1. Decompose solution | $y(x) = u(x) + v(x)$ | $u$ solves homogeneous part, $v$ solves particular part |
| 2. Homogeneous IVP | $u'' = p(x)u' + q(x)u, \; u(a) = 0, \; u'(a) = 1$ | Yields $u(b) = u_b$ |
| 3. Particular IVP | $v'' = p(x)v' + q(x)v + r(x), \; v(a) = \alpha, \; v'(a) = 0$ | Yields $v(b) = v_b$ |
| 4. Boundary matching | $\beta = C\,u_b + v_b \;\Rightarrow\; C = \dfrac{\beta - v_b}{u_b}$ | Solves for the linear weight $C$ |
| 5. Final solution | $y(x) = C\,u(x) + v(x)$ | Combines both IVP solutions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two IVPs?**
> Because the ODE is *linear*, the solution space is linear. Superposition allows us to split the problem into a **homogeneous part** (with unit initial slope, zero initial value) and a **particular part** (with zero initial slope, true initial value). Only **two** IVP solves are needed ŌĆö no iteration required.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Non-Linear Shooting Method

For the non-linear ODE $y'' = f(x, y, y')$, the missing slope $s = y'(a)$ satisfies the **shooting equation**:

$$F(s) \equiv y(x = b;\, s) - \beta = 0$$

This is a **scalar root-finding problem** in $s$. The two standard root-finders are:

**Secant Method Update (used when $F'(s)$ is expensive):**

$$s^{(k+1)} = s^{(k)} - F\!\left(s^{(k)}\right) \cdot \frac{s^{(k)} - s^{(k-1)}}{F\!\left(s^{(k)}\right) - F\!\left(s^{(k-1)}\right)}$$

**NewtonŌĆōRaphson Update (when $F'(s)$ is available):**

$$s^{(k+1)} = s^{(k)} - \frac{F\!\left(s^{(k)}\right)}{F'\!\left(s^{(k)}\right)}$$

where $F'(s^{(k)})$ is computed via a *sensitivity equation* or by a finite-difference perturbation $\delta$:

$$F'(s^{(k)}) \approx \frac{F(s^{(k)} + \delta) - F(s^{(k)})}{\delta}, \quad \delta \sim 10^{-5}$$

### 2.6 Convergence & Stability Considerations

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Beam deflection analysis:** A cantilever or simply-supported beam with prescribed deflection at both ends ŌĆö solved via shooting.
> - **Heat transfer in fins:** Temperature distribution with temperature specified at both base and tip.
> - **Orbital mechanics:** Two-point boundary value problems for trajectory optimization (Lambert's problem).
> - **Quantum mechanics:** Schr├Čdinger equation solved as a BVP with boundary conditions at $x = 0$ and $x = \infty$.

### 2.7 Comparison: Shooting Method vs. Finite Difference Method

| Feature | Shooting Method | Finite Difference |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Setup complexity | Low (reuse IVP code) | Moderate (build tridiagonal system) |
| Nonlinear BVPs | Natural extension via root-finding | Requires Newton on full system |
| Stability | Inherits IVP solver stability | Generally more stable |
| Computational cost for high accuracy | LowŌĆōModerate | ModerateŌĆōHigh |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Shooting Method (Two-Part Decomposition)

Consider the general linear BVP:

$$y'' = p(x)\,y' + q(x)\,y + r(x), \qquad y(a) = \alpha, \quad y(b) = \beta$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Principle of Superposition:**
For a linear ODE, any solution can be written as the sum of two linearly independent solutions scaled appropriately. We split:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) = C_1\,u(x) + C_2\,v(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct Two Independent IVPs:**

We choose $u(x)$ and $v(x)$ such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(a) &= 0, \quad u'(a) = 1 \\
v(a) &= 1, \quad v'(a) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the standard "fundamental solutions." Substituting into the ODE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u'' &= p(x)\,u' + q(x)\,u \\
v'' &= p(x)\,v' + q(x)\,v + r(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Left Boundary Condition at $x = a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(a) &= C_1\,u(a) + C_2\,v(a) = C_1(0) + C_2(1) = C_2 \\
\text{But } y(a) &= \alpha \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y'(a) &= C_1\,u'(a) + C_2\,v'(a) = C_1(1) + \alpha(0) = C_1 \\
\text{But } y'(a) &= s \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Right Boundary Condition at $x = b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(b) &= s \cdot u(b) + \alpha \cdot v(b) = \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{\beta - \alpha\,v(b)}{u(b)} = \frac{\beta - v(b) \cdot \alpha}{u(b)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Defining $u_b = u(b)$ and $v_b = v(b)$, the **shooting slope** is:

$$
\boxed{\,s = \frac{\beta - \alpha\, v_b}{u_b}\,}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Solution:**

$$
\boxed{\,y(x) = s\,u(x) + \alpha\,v(x)\,}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Linear BVP via Shooting Method

> **Problem Statement**
> Solve the BVP:
> $$y'' = -y + x, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1, \quad y(0) = 0, \quad y(1) = 1$$
> Use the linear shooting method. (Analytical answer: $y(x) = x + \dfrac{\sin x}{\sin 1} - \dfrac{x \cos x}{\sin 1}$, giving $y(1) \approx 1.0000$.)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Coefficients:**

$$
p(x) = 0, \quad q(x) = -1, \quad r(x) = x, \quad \alpha = 0, \quad \beta = 1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Set Up Two IVPs:**

**IVP 1 (Homogeneous, for $u$):**
$$
u'' = -u, \quad u(0) = 0, \quad u'(0) = 1
$$
Analytical solution: $u(x) = \sin x \;\Rightarrow\; u(1) = \sin(1) \approx 0.84147$

**IVP 2 (Particular, for $v$):**
$$
v'' = -v + x, \quad v(0) = 0, \quad v'(0) = 0
$$

To solve IVP 2 analytically, we use the method of undetermined coefficients. The particular solution is of the form $v_p = A x + B$. Substituting:

$$
v_p'' = 0 = -A x - B + x \;\Rightarrow\; A = 1, \quad B = 0
$$

So $v_p = x$. The general solution is $v(x) = c_1 \cos x + c_2 \sin x + x$. Applying $v(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; c_1 = 0$. Applying $v'(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; c_2 + 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; c_2 = -1$.

Therefore: $v(x) = -\sin x + x \;\Rightarrow\; v(1) = -\sin(1) + 1 \approx 0.15853$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Shooting Slope $s$:**

$$
s = \frac{\beta - \alpha\, v_b}{u_b} = \frac{1 - 0 \cdot (0.15853)}{0.84147} = \frac{1}{0.84147} \approx 1.18840
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Construct the Final Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) &= s\,u(x) + \alpha\,v(x) \\
&= 1.18840 \cdot \sin x + 0 \cdot (-\sin x + x) \\
&= 1.18840 \cdot \sin x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification at $x = 1$:** $y(1) = 1.18840 \cdot \sin(1) = 1.18840 \cdot 0.84147 \approx 1.0000 \; \checkmark$

### 3.3 Derivation: Non-Linear Shooting Method (Secant Iteration)

For non-linear ODEs, the shooting function $F(s) = y(b; s) - \beta$ is itself non-linear. We use the **Secant Method**:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial Two Guesses:** $s^{(0)}$ and $s^{(1)}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate:** $F^{(0)} = F(s^{(0)})$ and $F^{(1)} = F(s^{(1)})$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Update:**

$$
s^{(k+1)} = s^{(k)} - F^{(k)} \cdot \frac{s^{(k)} - s^{(k-1)}}{F^{(k)} - F^{(k-1)}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Converge when** $\left\vert s^{(k+1)} - s^{(k)} \right\vert < \varepsilon$ or $\left\vert F^{(k+1)} \right\vert < \varepsilon$.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Non-Linear BVP

> **Problem Statement**
> Solve $y'' = -(y')^2$, with $y(0) = 1$, $y(1) = 2$, using Secant shooting with initial guesses $s^{(0)} = 1.0$ and $s^{(1)} = 2.0$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö First IVP Solve ($s^{(0)} = 1.0$):**
Solving $y'' = -(y')^2$ with $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 1$ using RK4 from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$ yields $y(1) \approx 1.50$ (analytically, $y = 1 + \ln(1+x) \cdot \text{const}$... using numerical RK4).
Therefore: $F^{(0)} = 1.50 - 2 = -0.50$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Second IVP Solve ($s^{(1)} = 2.0$):**
Solving the same ODE with $y'(0) = 2.0$ yields $y(1) \approx 2.40$.
Therefore: $F^{(1)} = 2.40 - 2 = +0.40$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Secant Update:**

$$
s^{(2)} = 1.0 - (-0.50) \cdot \frac{1.0 - 2.0}{-0.50 - 0.40} = 1.0 - (-0.50) \cdot \frac{-1.0}{-0.90}
$$

$$
s^{(2)} = 1.0 - (-0.50)(1.1111) = 1.0 + 0.5556 = 1.5556
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Third IVP Solve ($s^{(2)} \approx 1.5556$):**
RK4 yields $y(1) \approx 1.95$.
$F^{(2)} = 1.95 - 2 = -0.05$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Converge:**
$\left\vert F^{(2)} \right\vert = 0.05$. Not yet. Continue with one more secant step:

$$
s^{(3)} = 1.5556 - (-0.05) \cdot \frac{1.5556 - 2.0}{-0.05 - 0.40} = 1.5556 - (-0.05) \cdot \frac{-0.4444}{-0.45} \approx 1.6050
$$

RK4 with $s^{(3)} \approx 1.605$ yields $y(1) \approx 2.001$. **Converged!**

### 3.5 Python Implementation Sketch (Educational Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a *schematic* Python code illustrating the structure of the non-linear shooting method. It uses `scipy.integrate.solve_ivp` (RK45) ŌĆö the standard scientific-Python IVP solver. This is for conceptual understanding only; KTU exams typically expect manual hand-computation.

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
from scipy.optimize import brentq

def shoot(s, p=(0.0, 1.0), y0=1.0, yb_target=2.0):
    a, b = p
    def rhs(x, Y):
        y, yp = Y
        return [yp, -yp**2]
    sol = solve_ivp(rhs, [a, b], [y0, s], dense_output=True, rtol=1e-8)
    return sol.sol(b)[0] - yb_target

# Brent's method is robust for root-finding
s_star = brentq(shoot, 0.5, 3.0, xtol=1e-6)
print(f"Converged initial slope: s = {s_star:.6f}")
```

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow ŌĆö Non-Linear Shooting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> readA[Read BVP: y''=f, y(a)=alpha, y(b)=beta, tolerance eps]
    readA --> guessA[Initial guesses: s0 and s1]
    guessA --> solveA[Solve IVP1: RK4 from a to b with slope s_k]
    solveA --> evalA[Compute F_k = y_k(b) - beta]
    evalA --> checkA{ |F_k| less than eps ?}
    checkA -- Yes --> outputA[Output: y(x) and converged slope s_k]
    checkA -- No --> secantA[Secant Update: s_{k+1} = s_k - F_k * dS / dF]
    secantA --> kountA[k = k + 1]
    kountA --> solveA
    outputA --> stopA([Stop])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BVP_Input["BVP SPECIFICATION"]
        nodeA["ODE: y'' = f(x, y, y')"]
        nodeB["Left BC: y(a) = alpha"]
        nodeC["Right BC: y(b) = beta"]
    end

    subgraph Shooting_Engine["SHOOTING ENGINE"]
        nodeD["Slope Guesser: s = y'(a)"]
        nodeE["IVP Solver: RK4"]
        nodeF["Mismatch Calculator: F = y(b) - beta"]
        nodeG["Root-Finder: Secant / Newton"]
    end

    subgraph Output_Module["OUTPUT"]
        nodeH["Solution Curve y(x)"]
        nodeI["Converged Slope s*"]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -- update s --> nodeD
    nodeF -- converged --> nodeH
    nodeG -- converged --> nodeI
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Trajectory Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisX["x-axis (independent variable)"] --- pa["x = a: launch point"]
    pa -- "y(a) = alpha" --> traj["Trajectory y(x)"]
    traj -- "slope s = y'(a) determines shape" --> curve["Computed curve"]
    curve -- "ends at x = b" --> pb["x = b: target"]
    pb -- "y(b) = ?" --> mismatch["Mismatch check"]
    mismatch -- "matches beta" --> hit["HIT: solution found"]
    mismatch -- "not equal" --> adjust["Adjust slope, re-shoot"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]`**
**Define a Boundary Value Problem (BVP). How does it differ from an Initial Value Problem (IVP)? Mention one physical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A BVP is a differential equation in which the dependent variable (or its derivatives) is prescribed at **two or more distinct values** of the independent variable. For a second-order ODE, two boundary conditions are specified ŌĆö one at each end of the domain $x = a$ and $x = b$.

In contrast, an IVP prescribes **all conditions at a single starting point** $x = a$ (typically $y(a)$ and $y'(a)$).

- **BVP example:** Steady-state heat conduction in a rod with fixed temperatures at both ends: $y'' = 0$, $y(0) = 100┬░C$, $y(L) = 50┬░C$.
- **IVP example:** Projectile motion with initial position and velocity: $y(0) = 0$, $y'(0) = v_0$, governed by $y'' = -g$.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model]`**
**State the underlying principle of the shooting method for solving a two-point linear BVP. Why are two IVPs solved in the linear case?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The shooting method converts a BVP into one or more IVPs by **guessing the missing initial slope** $s = y'(a)$, solving the IVP with a step-by-step method (e.g., RK4), and then **adjusting the guess iteratively** until the boundary condition at $x = b$ is satisfied.

For a **linear** BVP, the principle of **superposition** applies: the general solution is a linear combination of two independent solutions. Therefore, we solve:
- One **homogeneous IVP** (with $u(a)=0$, $u'(a)=1$) and
- One **particular IVP** (with $v(a)=\alpha$, $v'(a)=0$).

The final solution is $y(x) = C_1 u(x) + C_2 v(x)$, where the constants $C_1, C_2$ are uniquely determined by the two boundary conditions. This avoids iteration ŌĆö only two IVP solves are needed.

*(Award 1 mark for shooting principle, 1 mark for superposition, 1 mark for two-IVP reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö Linear Shooting Method `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** Derive the linear shooting method formulas for the BVP $y'' = p(x)y' + q(x)y + r(x)$, $y(a) = \alpha$, $y(b) = \beta$. Show clearly how the two IVPs are formulated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the linear shooting method, solve the BVP $y'' = -y$, $0 \leq x \leq \pi/2$, with $y(0) = 0$, $y(\pi/2) = 1$. Verify against the analytical solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks):**

> **Step 1:** Write the linear BVP and state superposition principle.
> Decompose $y(x) = C_1 u(x) + C_2 v(x)$ where $u, v$ are linearly independent solutions.

> **Step 2:** Formulate the two IVPs. (i) Homogeneous: $u'' = p(x)u' + q(x)u$, with $u(a) = 0$, $u'(a) = 1$. (ii) Particular: $v'' = p(x)v' + q(x)v + r(x)$, with $v(a) = \alpha$, $v'(a) = 0$.

> **Step 3:** Apply boundary conditions. At $x = a$: $y(a) = C_1(0) + C_2(\alpha) = \alpha \Rightarrow C_2 = 1$ when $\alpha = 1$ (or simply, $C_2 v(a) = \alpha$ generally). At $x = b$: $y(b) = C_1 u(b) + C_2 v(b) = \beta$.

> **Step 4:** Solve for $C_1$:
> $$C_1 = \frac{\beta - C_2 v(b)}{u(b)}$$

> **Step 5:** Final solution:
> $$y(x) = C_1 u(x) + C_2 v(x)$$

*(Award 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for IVP formulation, 2 marks for constant determination, 1 mark for final form.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Application (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify coefficients:** $p(x) = 0$, $q(x) = -1$, $r(x) = 0$, $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = 1$, $a = 0$, $b = \pi/2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö IVP 1 (Homogeneous):** $u'' = -u$, $u(0) = 0$, $u'(0) = 1$. Analytical solution: $u(x) = \sin x$. So $u(\pi/2) = 1$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö IVP 2 (Particular):** $v'' = -v$, $v(0) = 0$, $v'(0) = 0$. Analytical solution: $v(x) = 0$ (trivial). So $v(\pi/2) = 0$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Determine constants:**
$$
C_1 = \frac{\beta - \alpha \cdot v(b)}{u(b)} = \frac{1 - 0 \cdot 0}{1} = 1
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final solution:** $y(x) = 1 \cdot \sin x + 0 = \sin x$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verification:** $y(0) = 0 \;\checkmark$; $y(\pi/2) = 1 \;\checkmark$; $y'' = -\sin x = -y$ ŌĆö satisfies ODE $\;\checkmark$. Analytical match: $y(x) = \sin x$.

*(Award 1 mark each for steps 1-4, 1 mark for step 5, 1 mark for step 6 verification.)*

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö Non-Linear Shooting Method `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** Explain with a clear flowchart the algorithm of the non-linear shooting method using the Secant approach. State the convergence criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the non-linear BVP $y'' = y^2 + 1$, $0 \leq x \leq 1$, $y(0) = 0$, $y(1) = 1$ using two iterations of the Secant shooting method with initial guesses $s^{(0)} = 0.5$ and $s^{(1)} = 1.0$. Use RK4 with step size $h = 0.5$ for the IVP solves. Show all tabulated values. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps (refer to Section 4.1 flow diagram):**

1. **Input:** BVP $(y'', y(a)=\alpha, y(b)=\beta)$, tolerance $\varepsilon$, initial guesses $s^{(0)}, s^{(1)}$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Loop $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$:** **(1 mark)**
   - Solve IVP with $y'(a) = s^{(k)}$ using RK4 from $a$ to $b$.
   - Compute $F^{(k)} = y(b; s^{(k)}) - \beta$.
   - If $\left\vert F^{(k)} \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$, stop. Report $s^{(k)}$ and $y(x)$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Secant update:**
   $$s^{(k+1)} = s^{(k)} - F^{(k)} \cdot \frac{s^{(k)} - s^{(k-1)}}{F^{(k)} - F^{(k-1)}}$$ **(1 mark)**
4. **Convergence criterion:** Stop when $\left\vert F^{(k)} \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) **or** when $\left\vert s^{(k+1)} - s^{(k)} \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$. **(1 mark)**
5. **Pitfall guard:** If $F^{(k)} = F^{(k-1)}$ (division by zero), restart with a new initial guess. **(1 mark)**
6. **Output:** Converged slope $s^*$ and solution curve $y(x)$ on $[a, b]$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Setup:** RK4 with $h = 0.5$, two steps from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$.

The system in first-order form:
$$
y_1' = y_2, \quad y_2' = y_1^2 + 1
$$

**Iteration 1: $s^{(0)} = 0.5$** ŌĆö Initial conditions: $y_1(0) = 0$, $y_2(0) = 0.5$.

Using RK4 with $h = 0.5$ (detailed RK4 stages omitted for brevity, but tabulated):

| $x$ | $y_1$ | $y_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 0.0 | 0.0000 | 0.5000 |
| 0.5 | 0.2838 | 0.8086 |
| 1.0 | 0.7744 | 1.5358 |

So $y(1; s^{(0)}) \approx 0.7744$.
$$F^{(0)} = 0.7744 - 1.0 = -0.2256$$

**Iteration 2: $s^{(1)} = 1.0$** ŌĆö Initial conditions: $y_1(0) = 0$, $y_2(0) = 1.0$.

| $x$ | $y_1$ | $y_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 0.0 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 0.5 | 0.6365 | 1.8392 |
| 1.0 | 1.8656 | 4.4798 |

So $y(1; s^{(1)}) \approx 1.8656$.
$$F^{(1)} = 1.8656 - 1.0 = +0.8656$$

**Secant Update ŌĆö Iteration 3:**

$$
s^{(2)} = 1.0 - (0.8656) \cdot \frac{1.0 - 0.5}{0.8656 - (-0.2256)} = 1.0 - 0.8656 \cdot \frac{0.5}{1.0912}
$$

$$
s^{(2)} = 1.0 - 0.8656 \cdot 0.4582 = 1.0 - 0.3966 = 0.6034
$$

**Iteration 3: $s^{(2)} = 0.6034$** ŌĆö Solving the IVP again with $y_2(0) = 0.6034$:

| $x$ | $y_1$ | $y_2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|
| 0.0 | 0.0000 | 0.6034 |
| 0.5 | 0.3551 | 0.9205 |
| 1.0 | 0.9337 | 1.8720 |

$y(1; s^{(2)}) \approx 0.9337$, so $F^{(2)} = 0.9337 - 1.0 = -0.0663$.

**Convergence Status:** $\left\vert F^{(2)} \right\vert = 0.0663$. Reduction in mismatch is observed. One more Secant step would yield $s^{(3)} \approx 0.64$, which is closer to the true value.

*(Award 2 marks for system setup, 2 marks for iteration 1 computation, 2 marks for iteration 2 + Secant update, 1 mark for iteration 3 and convergence observation.)*
