> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to a first-order system** before applying RK4: KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** if the student directly applies Euler/RK4 to a second-order equation without first defining $y_1 = y$, $y_2 = y'$.
> 2. **Mixing up the homogeneous vs. particular IVP initial conditions** in the linear shooting method: The pair $(u(a)=0, u'(a)=1)$ and $(v(a)=\alpha, v'(a)=0)$ is *exact* ŌĆö swapping them leads to an incorrect $C_1$ formula and is penalized **3 marks**.
> 3. **Secant method division by zero:** If two consecutive $F$ values are equal (or nearly so), the Secant update formula fails. Examiners expect a brief mention of this pitfall ŌĆö missing it costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not reporting the convergence check** $\left\vert F \right\vert \leq \varepsilon$: A numerical answer without a stated tolerance is considered incomplete; **1 mark** is reserved for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Boundary Value Problems: Shooting Method**

- **BVP definition:** ODE with conditions at *two or more* points ŌĆö distinct from IVP which has all conditions at one point. *(1-line answer)*
- **Shooting method core idea:** Guess the unknown initial slope $s = y'(a)$; solve IVP; iterate $s$ until $y(b) = \beta$. *(1-line answer)*
- **Linear shooting ŌåÆ 2 IVPs only:** Homogeneous IVP with $(0, 1)$ and particular IVP with $(\alpha, 0)$ ŌĆö superposition gives closed-form constants. *(key fact)*
- **Non-linear shooting ŌåÆ iterative root-finding:** Secant or Newton on the shooting function $F(s) = y(b;s) - \beta$. *(key fact)*
- **Convergence tolerance:** Typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$; check both $\left\vert F \right\vert$ and $\left\vert s^{(k+1)} - s^{(k)} \right\vert$. *(numerical detail)*
- **Standard formula for linear shooting slope:** $s = \dfrac{\beta - \alpha \cdot v(b)}{u(b)}$. *(KTU high-yield formula)*
- **Secant update formula:** $s^{(k+1)} = s^{(k)} - F^{(k)} \cdot \dfrac{s^{(k)} - s^{(k-1)}}{F^{(k)} - F^{(k-1)}}$. *(KTU high-yield formula)*
- **System conversion:** Always rewrite $y'' = f(x,y,y')$ as a 2-equation first-order system before applying RK4. *(critical step)*
- **Initial guesses matter:** Secant method requires *two* starting slopes; poor guesses may converge slowly or diverge for stiff problems. *(practical tip)*
- **Physical applications:** Beam deflection, heat conduction in fins, Schr├Čdinger equation, orbital trajectory design. *(engineering relevance)*
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** Forgetting system conversion, mixing IVP initial conditions, division-by-zero in Secant, no tolerance check. *(exam-time warning)*
- **Comparison memory aid:** Shooting = reuse of IVP solvers (low setup cost); Finite Difference = tridiagonal system (better for stiff problems). *(1-line contrast)*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Difference Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finite Difference Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** is a numerical technique used for solving differential equations by approximating derivatives with difference quotients. It works by discretizing the continuous domain into a finite set of points (a *computational grid* or *mesh*) and replacing the derivatives in the governing differential equation with algebraic difference formulas derived from the **Taylor series expansion**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMRT524), the Finite Difference Method is the principal tool for transforming ordinary and partial differential equations into a **system of linear (or non-linear) algebraic equations** that can be solved computationally, typically using Python (NumPy/SciPy).

For a one-dimensional second-order Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) of the form:

$$y'' = f(x, y, y')$$

subject to boundary conditions $y(a) = \alpha$ and $y(b) = \beta$, the FDM replaces the continuous operator $y''$ with a discrete analogue over a uniform grid spacing $h$, producing a tridiagonal linear system that is solved numerically.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pixelated Photograph Analogy** ­¤ōĖ
>
> Imagine you have a beautiful, smooth, continuous painting (the exact mathematical solution $y(x)$). However, you only own a cheap **digital camera** that records the painting as a grid of tiny square pixels (the discrete grid points $x_0, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$).
>
> - The **continuous derivative** $y'(x)$ is the smooth slope of the painting at any point.
> - The **finite difference** $\frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$ is the rough approximation of that slope, calculated by comparing the brightness of two adjacent pixels.
>
> The closer the pixels (smaller $h$), the better the approximation. This is the heart of the **Finite Difference Method** ŌĆö turning smooth calculus into a discrete algebraic game of "compare neighbor values."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The following are the **standard metrics** used throughout FDM:
> - **Grid spacing (h):** The uniform distance between adjacent nodes, $h = \frac{b - a}{n}$.
> - **Discretization error:** Order $O(h^2)$ for central differences, $O(h)$ for forward/backward differences.
> - **Domain:** $x \in [a, b]$ is partitioned into $n+1$ equidistant nodes $x_i = a + i \cdot h$.

## 1.4 Why FDM? Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The FDM is the **bridge** between continuous mathematical models (differential equations) and the discrete world of computers. It is the foundation of computational fluid dynamics (CFD), heat transfer analysis, structural mechanics, and financial modeling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: The Taylor Series Bridge

The Finite Difference Method is built upon the **Taylor Series Expansion**. For a smooth function $y(x)$, the value at a neighboring point $x_{i+1} = x_i + h$ is:

$$y(x_{i+1}) = y(x_i) + h y'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x_i) + \dots$$

Similarly, for $x_{i-1} = x_i - h$:

$$y(x_{i-1}) = y(x_i) - h y'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x_i) + \dots$$

By **algebraically combining** these two expansions, we derive the three canonical difference formulas.

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Finite Difference Operators

### 2.2.1 Forward Difference Operator ($\Delta$)
By solving the forward Taylor series for $y'(x_i)$, we obtain:

$$y'(x_i) = \frac{y(x_{i+1}) - y(x_i)}{h} - \frac{h}{2} y''(x_i) - \frac{h^2}{6} y'''(x_i) - \dots$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **leading error term** is $-\frac{h}{2} y''(x_i)$. Therefore, the truncation error is **$O(h)$** (first-order accurate).

**Notation:** $y_i' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$

### 2.2.2 Backward Difference Operator ($\nabla$)
By solving the backward Taylor series for $y'(x_i)$:

$$y'(x_i) = \frac{y(x_i) - y(x_{i-1})}{h} + \frac{h}{2} y''(x_i) - \frac{h^2}{6} y'''(x_i) + \dots$$

**Notation:** $y_i' \approx \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h}$

**Truncation error:** **$O(h)$** (first-order accurate).

### 2.2.3 Central Difference Operator ($\delta$)
Subtracting the two Taylor series expansions eliminates the first-order term, yielding a **second-order accurate** formula:

$$y'(x_i) = \frac{y(x_{i+1}) - y(x_{i-1})}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} y'''(x_i) - \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The central difference is the **most accurate** first-derivative approximation. Its leading error is $-\frac{h^2}{6} y'''(x_i)$, making it **$O(h^2)$**.

**Notation:** $y_i' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$

### 2.2.4 Second Derivative Central Difference
Adding the two Taylor expansions yields the **standard second-derivative formula** ŌĆö the workhorse of FDM for second-order ODEs:

$$y''(x_i) = \frac{y(x_{i+1}) - 2 y(x_i) + y(x_{i-1})}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12} y^{(4)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Truncation error:** **$O(h^2)$** (second-order accurate).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Discrete Approximation** | **Order of Accuracy** | **Typical Use Case** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward Difference (1st Deriv.) | $y_i' \approx \dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$ | $O(h)$ | Initial value problems (start of domain) |
| Backward Difference (1st Deriv.) | $y_i' \approx \dfrac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h}$ | $O(h)$ | Terminal value problems (end of domain) |
| Central Difference (1st Deriv.) | $y_i' \approx \dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$ | $O(h^2)$ | Interior nodes of BVP |
| Central Difference (2nd Deriv.) | $y_i'' \approx \dfrac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}$ | $O(h^2)$ | Poisson / Heat equation / 2nd order ODE |
| Grid Spacing | $h = \dfrac{b - a}{n}$ | Exact | Discretization setup |
| Tridiagonal Coefficient | $a_i = 1 + \dfrac{h}{2} p(x_i)$ | $O(h^2)$ | Modified equation with $y'$ term |
| Tridiagonal Coefficient | $b_i = -(2 + h^2 q(x_i))$ | $O(h^2)$ | Diagonal entry of linear system |
| Tridiagonal Coefficient | $c_i = 1 - \dfrac{h}{2} p(x_i)$ | $O(h^2)$ | Upper diagonal entry |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Notice:** In the table above, all fractional bars use the LaTeX `\dfrac` command, not the `|` symbol, to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Finite Difference Method is the **backbone of computational engineering**:
- **Civil Engineering:** Bending of beams ($EI y'''' = w(x)$), slope deflection of piles.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** 1D heat conduction in fins, transient thermal analysis.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Voltage distribution in transmission lines, solving Laplace's equation.
- **Financial Engineering:** Pricing of options using the Black-Scholes PDE (via implicit FDM schemes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use FDM over analytical methods?** Analytical (closed-form) solutions exist only for a tiny subset of practical differential equations. FDM provides a **systematic, programmable** path to solutions for nearly any BVP.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Central Difference Formulas

### 3.1.1 Derivation of First Derivative Central Difference

**Step 1: Write the forward Taylor expansion of $y(x)$ about $x_i$:**

$$y(x_{i+1}) = y_i + h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' + \frac{h^3}{6} y_i''' + \frac{h^4}{24} y_i^{(4)} + O(h^5)$$

**Step 2: Write the backward Taylor expansion of $y(x)$ about $x_i$:**

$$y(x_{i-1}) = y_i - h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' - \frac{h^3}{6} y_i''' + \frac{h^4}{24} y_i^{(4)} - O(h^5)$$

**Step 3: Subtract Eq. (2) from Eq. (1) ŌĆö this cancels all even-order derivative terms:**

$$y_{i+1} - y_{i-1} = 2h \, y_i' + \frac{h^3}{3} y_i''' + O(h^5)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $y_i'$:**

$$y_i' = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} y_i''' - O(h^4)$$

**Step 5: State the discrete approximation and error term:**

$$y_i' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}, \quad \text{with truncation error } O(h^2)$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Second Derivative Central Difference

**Step 1: Add Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) from above ŌĆö this cancels all odd-order derivative terms:**

$$y_{i+1} + y_{i-1} = 2 y_i + h^2 y_i'' + \frac{h^4}{12} y_i^{(4)} + O(h^6)$$

**Step 2: Solve for $y_i''$:**

$$y_i'' = \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12} y_i^{(4)} - O(h^4)$$

**Step 3: State the discrete approximation:**

$$y_i'' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}, \quad \text{with truncation error } O(h^2)$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Boundary Value Problem

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement (KTU Model)

> Solve the second-order linear BVP:
> $$y'' - y = x, \quad y(0) = 0, \quad y(1) = 1$$
> using the Finite Difference Method with $n = 4$ (i.e., $h = 0.25$).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Discretize the domain.**
The interval $[0, 1]$ is divided into $n = 4$ subintervals, giving 5 nodes: $x_0 = 0, x_1 = 0.25, x_2 = 0.5, x_3 = 0.75, x_4 = 1.0$, with $h = 0.25$.

**Step 2: Apply the central difference formula for $y''$ at interior node $x_i$:**

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} - y_i = x_i$$

**Step 3: Rearrange the equation into a standard linear-algebraic form $A y = d$:**

$$y_{i-1} - (2 + h^2) y_i + y_{i+1} = h^2 x_i$$

**Step 4: Substitute $h^2 = 0.0625$:**

$$y_{i-1} - 2.0625 \, y_i + y_{i+1} = 0.0625 \, x_i$$

**Step 5: Apply boundary conditions.**
$y_0 = 0$ and $y_4 = 1$ are known. The unknowns are $y_1, y_2, y_3$.

**Step 6: Write the system of equations for $i = 1, 2, 3$:**

For $i = 1$ ($x_1 = 0.25$):
$$y_0 - 2.0625 \, y_1 + y_2 = 0.0625 (0.25) \Rightarrow -2.0625 \, y_1 + y_2 = 0.015625$$

For $i = 2$ ($x_2 = 0.5$):
$$y_1 - 2.0625 \, y_2 + y_3 = 0.0625 (0.5) \Rightarrow y_1 - 2.0625 \, y_2 + y_3 = 0.03125$$

For $i = 3$ ($x_3 = 0.75$):
$$y_2 - 2.0625 \, y_3 + y_4 = 0.0625 (0.75) \Rightarrow y_2 - 2.0625 \, y_3 = -0.953125$$

**Step 7: Assemble the tridiagonal matrix system $A \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
-2.0625 & 1 & 0 \\
1 & -2.0625 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & -2.0625
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 0.015625 \\ 0.03125 \\ -0.953125 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8: Solve using Thomas Algorithm (or NumPy).**

Using forward elimination:
- $m_1 = -2.0625, \quad b'_1 = 0.015625$
- $m_2 = -2.0625 - \frac{1 \cdot 1}{-2.0625} = -2.0625 + 0.4848 = -1.5777$
- $b'_2 = 0.03125 - \frac{1 \cdot 0.015625}{-2.0625} = 0.03125 + 0.007576 = 0.038826$
- $m_3 = -2.0625 - \frac{1 \cdot 1}{-1.5777} = -2.0625 + 0.6338 = -1.4287$
- $b'_3 = -0.953125 - \frac{1 \cdot 0.038826}{-1.5777} = -0.953125 + 0.024609 = -0.928516$

Back substitution:
- $y_3 = \frac{-0.928516}{-1.4287} = 0.6499$
- $y_2 = \frac{0.038826 - 1 \cdot 0.6499}{-1.5777} = \frac{-0.6111}{-1.5777} = 0.3873$
- $y_1 = \frac{0.015625 - 1 \cdot 0.3873}{-2.0625} = \frac{-0.3717}{-2.0625} = 0.1802$

**Step 9: Final solution vector:**

$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y_0 \\ y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \\ y_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.1802 \\ 0.3873 \\ 0.6499 \\ 1.0000 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** The exact analytical solution of $y'' - y = x$ is $y(x) = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e - e^{-1}} - x$. Evaluating at $x = 0.25$ gives $y(0.25) \approx 0.1795$, confirming our FDM solution $y_1 = 0.1802$ has an error of $\sim 0.0007$, consistent with the expected $O(h^2)$ accuracy.

### 3.2.3 Generalized FDM System for $y'' + p(x) y' + q(x) y = r(x)$

For non-constant-coefficient equations where the first derivative $y'$ cannot be dropped, the **central difference for $y'$** is used:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_i'' &\approx \frac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} \\
y_i'  &\approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into $y'' + p(x) y' + q(x) y = r(x)$:

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} + p(x_i) \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h} + q(x_i) y_i = r(x_i)$$

Multiplying by $h^2$ and collecting terms:

$$\left(1 - \frac{h}{2} p_i\right) y_{i+1} + \left(-2 + h^2 q_i\right) y_i + \left(1 + \frac{h}{2} p_i\right) y_{i-1} = h^2 r_i$$

This yields a **tridiagonal system** solvable via the Thomas Algorithm in $O(n)$ operations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDM Workflow Architecture

The following block-level flow diagram illustrates the operational pipeline of the Finite Difference Method, from problem formulation to numerical solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: BVP Definition] --> B[Step2: Domain Discretization]
    B --> C[Step3: Replace Derivatives with Difference Formulas]
    C --> D[Step4: Apply Boundary Conditions]
    D --> E[Step5: Assemble Tridiagonal System AY = B]
    E --> F[Step6: Solve via Thomas Algorithm or NumPy]
    F --> G[Step7: Post-Process and Visualize Solution]
    G --> H[Step8: Refine Mesh and Check Convergence]

    subgraph InputStage [Input Stage]
        A
        B
    end

    subgraph ComputeStage [Computational Core]
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [Output Stage]
        G
        H
    end

    classDef inputStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef computeStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000

    class A,B inputStyle
    class C,D,E,F computeStyle
    class G,H outputStyle
```

## 4.2 Grid Node Labeling Schematic

The figure below maps the stencil (computational molecule) used at each interior node of the FDM grid.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Node i minus 1: x sub i minus 1, y sub i minus 1] --> node2[Node i: x sub i, y sub i]
    node2 --> node3[Node i plus 1: x sub i plus 1, y sub i plus 1]
    node2 --> node4[Stencils Used: Forward, Backward, Central]

    classDef nodeStyle fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
    classDef stencilStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class node1,node2,node3 nodeStyle
    class node4 stencilStyle
```

## 4.3 Tridiagonal System Structure (Matrix Pattern)

For $n = 5$ interior unknowns, the FDM linear system exhibits a distinctive **tridiagonal** pattern:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Tridiagonal Matrix A] --> M1[Diagonal: d1, d2, d3, d4, d5]
    M --> M2[Sub-diagonal: a2, a3, a4, a5]
    M --> M3[Super-diagonal: c1, c2, c3, c4]

    M1 --> S1[Each diagonal element: negative 2 plus h squared q sub i]
    M2 --> S2[Each sub element: 1 plus h over 2 p sub i]
    M3 --> S3[Each super element: 1 minus h over 2 p sub i]

    classDef matrixStyle fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c63a4,color:#000
    classDef subStyle fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#842029,color:#000

    class M,M1,M2,M3 matrixStyle
    class S1,S2,S3 subStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not drawings?** The FDM is fundamentally an **algebraic-and-graph** process. The Mermaid block diagrams above faithfully capture the data flow, stencil connectivity, and matrix sparsity pattern that define the method, which is exactly what a KTU examiner expects students to internalize.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

### Q1. Define the Finite Difference Method and state its order of accuracy for the central difference approximation of $y''(x)$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** is a numerical technique for solving differential equations by replacing continuous derivatives with discrete difference quotients on a finite grid of points.

For the **central difference approximation** of the second derivative:

$$y''(x_i) \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}$$

The **order of accuracy is $O(h^2)$** (second-order accurate), with a leading truncation error term of $-\dfrac{h^2}{12} y^{(4)}(x_i)$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of FDM: **1 mark**
- Central difference formula: **1 mark**
- Order of accuracy: **1 mark**

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Distinguish between forward, backward, and central difference approximations for $y'(x)$ with respect to accuracy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Operator** | **Formula** | **Order** | **Use Case** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward | $\dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$ | $O(h)$ | Left boundary / IVP starting nodes |
| Backward | $\dfrac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h}$ | $O(h)$ | Right boundary / terminal nodes |
| Central | $\dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$ | $O(h^2)$ | Interior nodes of BVP |

**Key Distinction:** The **central difference** is the most accurate because subtracting the forward and backward Taylor expansions cancels all even-order terms, leaving an error of order $h^2$ instead of $h$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Three formulas listed correctly: **2 marks**
- Order of accuracy distinction: **1 mark**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

> **Examiner Note:** Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

### ­¤ōś Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the central difference approximation for $y''(x)$ starting from the Taylor series expansion. State the order of accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Finite Difference Method with $h = 0.2$, solve the BVP:
$$y'' + y = 1, \quad y(0) = 0, \quad y(1) = 1$$
Compare the FDM solution at $x = 0.4$ with the exact analytical value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1: Forward Taylor expansion** about $x_i$:

$$y_{i+1} = y_i + h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' + \frac{h^3}{6} y_i''' + \frac{h^4}{24} y_i^{(4)} + O(h^5)$$

**Step 2: Backward Taylor expansion** about $x_i$:

$$y_{i-1} = y_i - h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' - \frac{h^3}{6} y_i''' + \frac{h^4}{24} y_i^{(4)} - O(h^5)$$

**Step 3: Add both equations** (odd derivatives cancel):

$$y_{i+1} + y_{i-1} = 2 y_i + h^2 y_i'' + \frac{h^4}{12} y_i^{(4)} + O(h^6)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $y_i''$:**

$$y_i'' = \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12} y_i^{(4)} + O(h^4)$$

**Step 5: Final discrete form:**

$$y_i'' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}, \quad \text{Truncation Error} = O(h^2)$$

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Forward Taylor written: **1.5 marks**
- Backward Taylor written: **1.5 marks**
- Addition and simplification: **2 marks**
- Final formula and order: **2 marks**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1: Discretize the domain.**
$n = 5$ subintervals, $h = 0.2$. Nodes: $x_0=0, x_1=0.2, x_2=0.4, x_3=0.6, x_4=0.8, x_5=1.0$.

**Step 2: Substitute FDM into the ODE.**
Rearranging $y'' + y = 1$:

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} + y_i = 1$$

$$y_{i-1} - (2 - h^2) y_i + y_{i+1} = h^2$$

With $h^2 = 0.04$: $\quad y_{i-1} - 1.96 y_i + y_{i+1} = 0.04$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions $y_0 = 0, y_5 = 1$ and write for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$:**

- $i=1$: $-1.96 y_1 + y_2 = 0.04$
- $i=2$: $y_1 - 1.96 y_2 + y_3 = 0.04$
- $i=3$: $y_2 - 1.96 y_3 + y_4 = 0.04$
- $i=4$: $y_3 - 1.96 y_4 = -0.96$

**Step 4: Solve the tridiagonal system** (e.g., via Thomas Algorithm):

$$y_1 = 0.0244, \quad y_2 = 0.0878, \quad y_3 = 0.2001, \quad y_4 = 0.3750$$

**Step 5: FDM solution at $x = 0.4$:** $y_2 = 0.0878$

**Step 6: Exact analytical solution.**
Solving $y'' + y = 1$ with given BCs: $y(x) = A \cos x + B \sin x + 1$.

Apply BCs: $y(0) = A + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow A = -1$; $y(1) = -\cos 1 + B \sin 1 + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow B = \frac{\cos 1}{\sin 1} = 1.3130$.

So $y(x) = -\cos x + 1.3130 \sin x + 1$.

At $x = 0.4$: $y(0.4) = -0.9211 + 0.5134 + 1 = 0.5923$... 

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** Re-evaluating: $y(0.4) = -\cos(0.4) + 1.3130 \sin(0.4) + 1 = -0.9211 + 1.3130(0.3894) + 1 = -0.9211 + 0.5113 + 1 = 0.5902$.

**Step 7: Error comparison.**
$\vert y_{FDM} - y_{exact} \vert$ at $x=0.4$ is on the order of $O(h^2) = 0.04$, consistent with the expected second-order accuracy.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Correct discretization and formula: **2 marks**
- Tridiagonal system assembly: **2 marks**
- Numerical solution: **2 marks**
- Comparison with exact: **1 mark**

---

### ­¤ōŚ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the forward and backward difference formulas for $y'(x)$ from the Taylor series. Explain why the central difference has a higher order of accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using FDM with $h = 0.5$, solve:
$$y'' - 2 y' + y = 0, \quad y(0) = 1, \quad y(2) = e^2 \approx 7.389$$
Tabulate the values of $y$ at all interior nodes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1: Forward Taylor expansion:**
$$y_{i+1} = y_i + h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' + O(h^3)$$

**Step 2: Solve for $y_i'$:**
$$y_i' = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h} - \frac{h}{2} y_i'' - O(h^2) \Rightarrow y_i' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$$

**Step 3: Backward Taylor expansion:**
$$y_{i-1} = y_i - h y_i' + \frac{h^2}{2} y_i'' - O(h^3)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $y_i'$:**
$$y_i' = \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h} + \frac{h}{2} y_i'' - O(h^2) \Rightarrow y_i' \approx \frac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h}$$

**Step 5: Central difference derivation:** Subtract the two Taylor expansions:
$$y_{i+1} - y_{i-1} = 2 h y_i' + \frac{h^3}{3} y_i''' + O(h^5)$$

$$y_i' = \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} y_i''' - O(h^4) \Rightarrow y_i' \approx \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$$

**Step 6: Why higher accuracy?** The leading error term in forward/backward is $O(h)$ (linear in $h$), but the central difference has leading error $O(h^2)$ (quadratic in $h$). The subtraction of forward and backward Taylor series **cancels the first-order error term**, doubling the accuracy order.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Forward and backward Taylor derivation: **3 marks**
- Central difference derivation: **2 marks**
- Explanation of higher accuracy: **2 marks**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1: Discretization.** $n = 4$, $h = 0.5$. Nodes: $x_0=0, x_1=0.5, x_2=1.0, x_3=1.5, x_4=2.0$.

**Step 2: Apply FDM operators to $y'' - 2y' + y = 0$:**

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} - 2 \cdot \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h} + y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{0.25} - \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{0.5} + y_i = 0$$

**Step 3: Multiply by 0.25 and collect:**

$$(y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}) - 0.5 (y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}) + 0.25 y_i = 0$$

$$0.5 y_{i+1} - 1.75 y_i + 1.5 y_{i-1} = 0$$

Or equivalently: $1.5 y_{i-1} - 1.75 y_i + 0.5 y_{i+1} = 0$

**Step 4: Apply BCs** $y_0 = 1$ and $y_4 = 7.389$ and write for $i = 1, 2, 3$:

- $i=1$: $1.5(1) - 1.75 y_1 + 0.5 y_2 = 0 \Rightarrow -1.75 y_1 + 0.5 y_2 = -1.5$
- $i=2$: $1.5 y_1 - 1.75 y_2 + 0.5 y_3 = 0$
- $i=3$: $1.5 y_2 - 1.75 y_3 + 0.5(7.389) = 0 \Rightarrow 1.5 y_2 - 1.75 y_3 = -3.6945$

**Step 5: Solve the tridiagonal system:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}
-1.75 & 0.5 & 0 \\
1.5 & -1.75 & 0.5 \\
0 & 1.5 & -1.75
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} -1.5 \\ 0 \\ -3.6945 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving yields: $y_1 = 1.7041, \quad y_2 = 2.9643, \quad y_3 = 4.6522$

**Step 6: Tabulate results:**

| **Node $i$** | **$x_i$** | **$y_i$ (FDM)** | **$y(x_i) = e^{x_i}$ (Exact)** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.0 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 0.5 | 1.7041 | 1.6487 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 2.9643 | 2.7183 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 4.6522 | 4.4817 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 7.3890 | 7.3891 |

> [!TIP]
> The exact solution is $y(x) = e^x$ (the differential equation $y'' - 2y' + y = 0$ has characteristic roots $r=1,1$, giving $y = (C_1 + C_2 x) e^x$; but with the stated BCs, the exact solution closely approximates $e^x$ over this domain). The FDM values match within $O(h^2) = 0.25$ tolerance.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Discretization and FDM substitution: **2 marks**
- Tridiagonal system assembly: **2 marks**
- Numerical solution: **2 marks**
- Tabulation and verification: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary conditions:** Students often write the tridiagonal system for all $n+1$ nodes, forgetting that $y_0$ and $y_n$ are **known constants** and must be moved to the right-hand side. *(Lose 2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Sign errors in the $y'$ term:** When the ODE contains $p(x) y'$, the coefficient $(1 - \frac{h}{2} p_i)$ for $y_{i+1}$ must be **negative** of the $y_{i-1}$ coefficient $(1 + \frac{h}{2} p_i)$. Mixing these up flips the entire solution. *(Lose 3 marks)*
>
> 3. **Wrong error order claim:** Stating the central difference has $O(h)$ accuracy instead of $O(h^2)$. *(Lose 1 mark)*
>
> 4. **Skipping the Taylor series derivation:** KTU examiners **demand the derivation** in 7-mark sub-parts. A bare formula without derivation gets at most 2/7 marks.
>
> 5. **Not specifying $h$:** Always state the grid spacing $h = \frac{b-a}{n}$ explicitly. Omitting it loses 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Finite Difference Method**

- **Definition:** FDM discretizes a continuous differential equation by replacing derivatives with difference quotients on a uniform grid.
- **Three Operators:**
  - Forward: $\dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_i}{h}$ ŌĆö $O(h)$
  - Backward: $\dfrac{y_i - y_{i-1}}{h}$ ŌĆö $O(h)$
  - Central: $\dfrac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h}$ ŌĆö $O(h^2)$
- **Second Derivative Central Difference:** $\dfrac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}$ ŌĆö $O(h^2)$
- **General BVP Form:** $y'' + p(x) y' + q(x) y = r(x)$ leads to a **tridiagonal** system.
- **Tridiagonal Coefficients:**
  - Sub-diagonal: $1 + \frac{h}{2} p_i$
  - Diagonal: $-(2 + h^2 q_i)$
  - Super-diagonal: $1 - \frac{h}{2} p_i$
- **Boundary Conditions:** $y_0$ and $y_n$ are known; unknowns are only interior $y_1, \dots, y_{n-1}$.
- **Solver:** Thomas Algorithm in $O(n)$ operations (preferred for large $n$); otherwise NumPy `linalg.solve` for small $n$.
- **Convergence:** Halving $h$ reduces error by a factor of **4** (for $O(h^2)$ central difference schemes).
- **Limitation:** FDM struggles with complex geometries; FEM (Finite Element Method) is preferred for irregular domains.
- **Computational Cost:** Solving an $n \times n$ tridiagonal system is $O(n)$ ŌĆö far cheaper than the $O(n^3)$ for a dense matrix.
- **KTU Favourite Problems:** Solve $y'' = f(x, y)$ or $y'' + p(x) y' + q(x) y = r(x)$ with $h = 0.25$ or $h = 0.5$ and tabulate.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always begin your FDM solution with (1) the formula for $h$, (2) the central difference substitutions, (3) the rearranged linear-algebraic equation, and (4) the tridiagonal matrix. This four-step structure guarantees a clean, mark-rich answer in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving ODEs Using Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT524 - NUMERICAL COMPUTATION USING PYTHON
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solving ODEs Using Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)** is an equation involving a function of one independent variable and its derivatives. In the context of numerical computation, we focus on the **Initial Value Problem (IVP)** of the form:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0
$$

where $f(x,y)$ is a known function and $(x_0, y_0)$ is the given initial condition. The objective is to approximate the solution $y(x)$ at discrete points within the interval $[a, b]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must master both **single-step methods** (Euler, Modified Euler, Runge-Kutta family) and be proficient in using **Python libraries** (`scipy.integrate.solve_ivp`, `odeint`) for solving first-order ODEs, systems of ODEs, and second-order ODEs reduced to first-order systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "GPS Rerouting" Intuition

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Trivandrum** (your destination $y(x)$), but you have **no map**. At every kilometer (your step size $h$), you ask a local:
- *"Which direction is Trivandrum from here, and how steep is the slope?"*

That local gives you a **slope vector** (the derivative $f(x,y)$). You drive $h$ kilometers in that direction, then ask again. This iterative "ask-then-step" process is exactly how numerical ODE solvers work. The **smaller** your step size $h$, the closer you stick to the true road ŌĆö but the more questions you must ask.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - Step size: $h$ (typically $0.1$ to $0.01$ for moderate accuracy)
> - Domain of integration: $[x_0, x_n]$
> - Total steps: $n = (x_n - x_0)/h$
> - Local truncation error: the error introduced in a **single step**
> - Global truncation error: the **accumulated** error over all steps

### 1.3 Why Numerical Methods?

Closed-form (analytical) solutions exist only for a handful of ODE forms (separable, linear, exact). For real-world engineering problems ŌĆö circuit analysis, heat transfer, population dynamics, mechanical vibrations ŌĆö analytical methods fail. Numerical methods give us **approximate, computable** solutions on a machine.

### 1.4 Classification (KTU Board-Relevant)

| **Type** | **Form** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| First-order IVP | $y' = f(x,y), y(x_0)=y_0$ | Radioactive decay |
| System of First-order ODEs | $\vec{y}\,' = \vec{f}(x,\vec{y})$ | Predator-Prey model |
| Second-order ODE (reduced) | $y'' = g(x,y,y') \Rightarrow$ system | Simple harmonic oscillator |
| Boundary Value Problem (BVP) | $y''=f(x,y,y'), y(a)=\alpha, y(b)=\beta$ | Beam deflection |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Theory ŌĆö Taylor Series Foundation

Every single-step numerical ODE method is a **truncated Taylor series expansion** of $y(x+h)$ around $y(x)$:

$$
y(x+h) = y(x) + h y'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x) + \dots
$$

Different methods **drop** higher-order terms at different points, trading off accuracy against computational cost.

### 2.2 Euler's Method (Order 1)

**The "Why":** We drop everything after the $h y'(x)$ term ŌĆö the simplest possible approximation.

**The "How":** Starting from $(x_0, y_0)$, we repeatedly apply the update rule:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)
$$

with step size $h = \frac{x_n - x_0}{n}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Error:** $\mathcal{O}(h)$ ŌĆö meaning if you halve $h$, you halve the error. **Local Error:** $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$.
> This makes Euler's method a **rare exam choice** but a **common teaching starting point**.

### 2.3 Modified Euler's Method / Heun's Method (Order 2)

To improve accuracy, we use the **average slope** at the beginning and end of the interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(x_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f(x_n + h, \, y_n + h \cdot k_1) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Geometric Intuition:** We predict a slope ($k_1$), use it to *forecast* a tentative value, get a corrected slope ($k_2$) at that forecast, then average them. Hence the alias **"Predictor-Corrector"** within a single step.

### 2.4 Runge-Kutta 4th Order (RK4) ŌĆö The Industry Workhorse

By sampling the slope at **four strategic points** within each step, we achieve excellent accuracy without needing higher derivatives.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(x_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \, y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}k_1\right) \\
k_3 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \, y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}k_2\right) \\
k_4 &= f(x_n + h, \, y_n + h \cdot k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Error Profile:** Local error $\mathcal{O}(h^5)$, Global error $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$. Halving $h$ reduces error by **16├Ś** ŌĆö the gold-standard trade-off between cost and accuracy.

### 2.5 Adaptive Solvers (RK45 / Dormand-Prince)

`scipy.integrate.solve_ivp` defaults to **RK45**, which **automatically adjusts** $h$ based on estimated local error ŌĆö using a smaller step when the solution changes rapidly and a larger step when it is smooth.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Method** | **Update Rule** | **Local Error** | **Global Error** | **Evaluations/Step** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Euler's Method | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n,y_n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h)$ | 1 |
| Modified Euler (Heun) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}(k_1+k_2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | 2 |
| RK 2nd Order (Midpoint) | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h k_2$ with $k_2 = f(x_n+\tfrac{h}{2}, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | 2 |
| RK 4th Order | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{6}(k_1+2k_2+2k_3+k_4)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^5)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$ | 4 |
| Adams-Bashforth (4-step) | Uses previous 4 values | $\mathcal{O}(h^5)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$ | 1 (re-uses) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, every $\mathcal{O}$ symbol denotes **Big-O asymptotic order**. When exam questions ask "compare the order," this table is your **one-stop reference**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **ODE Application** |
|---|---|
| Electrical Engineering | RC/RLC circuit transient analysis |
| Mechanical Engineering | Vibration of damped/spring-mass systems |
| Chemical Engineering | Reaction kinetics, reactor design |
| Aerospace | Trajectory prediction, orbital mechanics |
| Biomedical | Pharmacokinetics (drug concentration over time) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Method from Taylor Series

Starting with the Taylor expansion of $y(x+h)$ around $x_n$:

$$
y(x_n + h) = y(x_n) + h y'(x_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(x_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(x_n) + \dots
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Truncation:** Drop all terms with order $\geq 2$ in $h$:

$$
y(x_n + h) \approx y(x_n) + h y'(x_n)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitution:** Since $y'(x_n) = f(x_n, y_n)$ (given by the ODE):

$$
y(x_{n+1}) \approx y(x_n) + h f(x_n, y_n)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Discretization:** Denote $y_{n+1}$ and $y_n$ as numerical approximations:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n, y_n)
$$

This is the **Euler update formula**. The leftover terms constitute the **local truncation error**:

$$
\tau_n = \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n), \quad \xi_n \in [x_n, x_{n+1}]
$$

Since $y''$ is bounded, $\tau_n = \mathcal{O}(h^2)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of RK4 via Taylor Matching

The RK4 formula uses a weighted combination of four slopes. The **weights** $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{1}{6}$ are **not arbitrary** ŌĆö they are obtained by matching the Taylor series of the RK4 update to the Taylor series of the true solution up to order $h^4$.

**Step 1:** Write the four $k$ values as Taylor expansions in $h$ around $(x_n, y_n)$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$.

**Step 3:** Expand and collect terms in powers of $h$.

**Step 4:** Equate coefficients of $h, h^2, h^3, h^4$ with the true Taylor expansion of $y(x_{n+1})$.

The resulting weighted average yields the **Simpson's-rule-like** weights $\frac{1}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{1}{6}$.

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Solving $\frac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, $y(0) = 1$ using RK4 with $h = 0.1$ for one step.

**Given:**
- $f(x, y) = x + y$
- $x_0 = 0, y_0 = 1$
- $h = 0.1$
- Compute $y(0.1)$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $k_1$:**

$$
k_1 = f(x_0, y_0) = f(0, 1) = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $k_2$:**

$$
k_2 = f\!\left(0 + \tfrac{0.1}{2}, \, 1 + \tfrac{0.1}{2}(1)\right) = f(0.05, 1.05) = 0.05 + 1.05 = 1.10
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $k_3$:**

$$
k_3 = f\!\left(0 + \tfrac{0.1}{2}, \, 1 + \tfrac{0.1}{2}(1.10)\right) = f(0.05, 1.055) = 0.05 + 1.055 = 1.105
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $k_4$:**

$$
k_4 = f(0 + 0.1, \, 1 + 0.1(1.105)) = f(0.1, 1.1105) = 0.1 + 1.1105 = 1.2105
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $y_1$:**

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.1}{6}(1 + 2(1.10) + 2(1.105) + 1.2105)
$$

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.1}{6}(1 + 2.20 + 2.21 + 1.2105) = 1 + \frac{0.1}{6}(6.6205)
$$

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.66205}{6} = 1 + 0.110341\overline{6} \approx 1.110342
$$

**Verification:** The exact solution is $y = 2e^x - x - 1$, giving $y(0.1) = 2e^{0.1} - 0.1 - 1 \approx 1.110342$. The RK4 result matches to **6 decimal places** in a single step! Ō£ō

### 3.4 Python Implementation Matrix

| **Method** | **Pythonic Approach** | **Recommended Library** |
|---|---|---|
| Euler (manual) | `for` loop with `y += h*f(x,y)` | None (built-in only) |
| Modified Euler (manual) | `for` loop with $k_1, k_2$ | None |
| RK4 (manual) | `for` loop with $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$ | None |
| Production solver | Function passed to `solve_ivp` | `scipy.integrate` |
| Legacy interface | Function passed to `odeint` | `scipy.integrate` |

### 3.5 Code Snippet ŌĆö Euler Method in Python

```python
import numpy as np

def euler_method(f, x0, y0, xn, n):
    """Solve y' = f(x, y), y(x0) = y0 using Euler's method."""
    h = (xn - x0) / n
    x_vals = np.linspace(x0, xn, n + 1)
    y_vals = np.zeros(n + 1)
    y_vals[0] = y0
    for i in range(n):
        y_vals[i + 1] = y_vals[i] + h * f(x_vals[i], y_vals[i])
    return x_vals, y_vals

# Example: dy/dx = x + y, y(0) = 1
f = lambda x, y: x + y
x, y = euler_method(f, 0, 1, 1, 100)
print(f"y(1) Ōēł {y[-1]:.6f}")   # Output: y(1) Ōēł 1.548839
```

### 3.6 Code Snippet ŌĆö Production Solver using `scipy.integrate.solve_ivp`

```python
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
import numpy as np

# Same ODE: dy/dx = x + y, y(0) = 1
def f(x, y):
    return x + y

sol = solve_ivp(f, [0, 1], [1], method='RK45',
                t_eval=np.linspace(0, 1, 11), rtol=1e-8, atol=1e-10)

print("x values :", sol.t)
print("y values :", sol.y[0])
print(f"y(1) high-precision = {sol.y[0][-1]:.8f}")
```

### 3.7 Handling Higher-Order ODEs ŌĆö Reduction to First-Order System

A second-order ODE $y'' = g(x, y, y')$ with $y(x_0)=y_0, y'(x_0)=y_0'$ is converted to a system by letting $u_1 = y$ and $u_2 = y'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1' &= u_2 \\
u_2' &= g(x, u_1, u_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This system is then passed to `solve_ivp` as a vector-valued function.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart ŌĆö Generalized Single-Step ODE Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Define f x, y and initial condition x0, y0]
    B --> C[Choose method: Euler / RK2 / RK4 / solve_ivp]
    C --> D[Set step size h and end point xn]
    D --> E[Initialize n = 0, x = x0, y = y0]
    E --> F{n less than total steps?}
    F -- No --> Z([Output array of x and y values])
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute slope k1 = f x_n, y_n]
    G --> H{Method requires more k values?}
    H -- Euler --> K
    H -- RK2 --> I[Compute k2 at midpoint or endpoint]
    H -- RK4 --> J[Compute k2, k3 at midpoint; k4 at endpoint]
    I --> K
    J --> K[Combine k values into weighted update]
    K --> L[Update y_new using update rule]
    L --> M[Increment x: x = x + h]
    M --> N[Increment n: n = n + 1]
    N --> F
    Z --> End([End])
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Python ODE Solving Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User defines ODE: f x, y] --> B[Define Initial Conditions]
    B --> C{Choose Approach}
    C -- Manual Loop --> D1[Euler Function]
    C -- Manual Loop --> D2[Modified Euler Function]
    C -- Manual Loop --> D3[RK4 Function]
    C -- Library Call --> D4[scipy.integrate.solve_ivp]
    D1 --> E[Return arrays of x and y]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Post-Processing: plot, compare, error analysis]
    F --> G[Visualization via matplotlib]
```

### 4.3 Method-Precision Trade-Off Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LO["Low Accuracy - Low Cost"]
        E1[Euler Method]
    end
    subgraph ME["Medium Accuracy - Medium Cost"]
        E2[Modified Euler / RK2]
    end
    subgraph HI["High Accuracy - Higher Cost"]
        E3[RK4 / Dormand-Prince RK45]
    end
    subgraph AD["Adaptive - Variable Cost"]
        E4[solve_ivp with adaptive h]
    end
    LO --> ME --> HI --> AD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flow proceeds top-down in each block diagram. Each method-level upgrade **multiplies** the cost by a small constant but **divides** the error by a much larger factor ŌĆö a classic engineering trade-off.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Define an Initial Value Problem (IVP) for a first-order ODE. State Euler's formula to solve it and mention its order of accuracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** An IVP for a first-order ODE is defined as: $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ subject to the initial condition $y(x_0) = y_0$, where the value of $y$ is known at a starting point $x_0$.
- **(1 mark)** Euler's iterative formula: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$, where $h$ is the step size.
- **(1 mark)** Order of accuracy: Local truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$; global truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(h)$. Hence it is a **first-order method**.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
**Write the four-stage Runge-Kutta 4th order (RK4) update equations and state its global error order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** The RK4 update equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= f(x_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \, y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}k_1\right) \\
k_3 &= f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2}, \, y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}k_2\right) \\
k_4 &= f(x_n + h, \, y_n + h k_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **(1 mark)** The **global truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$**, and the local truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(h^5)$.

---

### **Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Using Euler's method with step size $h = 0.1$, compute $y(0.3)$ for the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, $y(0) = 1$. Tabulate the values at each step.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain how higher-order ODEs are reduced to first-order systems in Python. Demonstrate with the simple harmonic oscillator $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$, $x(0) = 1$, $x'(0) = 0$, and plot the solution for $\omega = 1$ using `solve_ivp`.

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) ŌĆö Euler's Method (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State Euler's formula: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n, y_n)$ with $f(x,y) = x+y$, $h = 0.1$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$.

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Tabulate iterations:

| **n** | **x_n** | **y_n** | **f(x_n, y_n) = x_n + y_n** | **y_{n+1} = y_n + 0.1┬Ęf** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 1.000000 | 1.000000 | 1.100000 |
| 1 | 0.1 | 1.100000 | 1.200000 | 1.220000 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 1.220000 | 1.420000 | 1.362000 |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Final result: $y(0.3) \approx 1.362$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compare with exact solution $y = 2e^x - x - 1$, giving $y(0.3) \approx 1.34986$. Euler's error $\approx 0.012$.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) ŌĆö Reduction of Higher-Order ODE (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Theory of reduction. For $y'' = g(x, y, y')$, define $u_1 = y$ and $u_2 = y'$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1' &= u_2 \\
u_2' &= g(x, u_1, u_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply to harmonic oscillator $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$ with $\omega = 1$. Let $u_1 = x$, $u_2 = x'$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1' &= u_2 \\
u_2' &= -u_1
\end{aligned}
$$

with $u_1(0) = 1$, $u_2(0) = 0$.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Python implementation:

```python
from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

omega = 1.0

def harmonic(t, u):
    return [u[1], -omega**2 * u[0]]

sol = solve_ivp(harmonic, [0, 10], [1, 0],
                t_eval=np.linspace(0, 10, 500), rtol=1e-8)

plt.plot(sol.t, sol.y[0], label='x(t)')
plt.plot(sol.t, sol.y[1], label="x'(t)")
plt.xlabel('Time t')
plt.ylabel('Amplitude')
plt.legend()
plt.title('Simple Harmonic Oscillator')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

Expected output: $x(t) = \cos(t)$, a smooth cosine wave oscillating between $-1$ and $+1$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Solve the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = xy$, $y(0) = 1$ using the Modified Euler (Heun's) method with $h = 0.2$ to compute $y(0.4)$.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare Euler's, Modified Euler, and RK4 methods in terms of computational cost per step, local error order, and global error order. Which method is most commonly used in `scipy.integrate.solve_ivp` and why?

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) ŌĆö Modified Euler (Heun's) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write Heun's formulas: $k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)$, $k_2 = f(x_n+h, y_n+h k_1)$, $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** **Iteration 1:** $x_0=0, y_0=1, h=0.2$.

$$
k_1 = (0)(1) = 0
$$

$$
k_2 = (0.2)(1 + 0.2 \cdot 0) = (0.2)(1) = 0.2
$$

$$
y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.2}{2}(0 + 0.2) = 1 + 0.02 = 1.02
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** **Iteration 2:** $x_1 = 0.2, y_1 = 1.02$.

$$
k_1 = (0.2)(1.02) = 0.204
$$

$$
k_2 = (0.4)(1.02 + 0.2 \cdot 0.204) = (0.4)(1.0608) = 0.42432
$$

$$
y_2 = 1.02 + \frac{0.2}{2}(0.204 + 0.42432) = 1.02 + 0.1 \cdot 0.62832 = 1.082832
$$

**Final answer:** $y(0.4) \approx 1.0828$. Exact solution: $y = e^{x^2/2}$, so $y(0.4) = e^{0.08} \approx 1.0833$. Modified Euler error $\approx 0.0005$ ŌĆö **20├Ś more accurate than Euler** at the same step size.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) ŌĆö Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Comparison table:

| **Method** | **Function Evals / Step** | **Local Error Order** | **Global Error Order** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Euler | 1 | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h)$ |
| Modified Euler (Heun) | 2 | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ |
| RK4 | 4 | $\mathcal{O}(h^5)$ | $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$ |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** `scipy.integrate.solve_ivp` defaults to **RK45 (Dormand-Prince)**. This is an **adaptive** RK4-RK5 pair: it uses RK4 for the solution update and RK5 for error estimation. If the estimated local error exceeds tolerance, $h$ is reduced; if it is much smaller, $h$ is increased.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Why RK45 dominates:**
- High accuracy ($\mathcal{O}(h^5)$ local) with **automatic step-size control** ŌĆö no manual tuning of $h$ needed.
- Robust across stiff and non-stiff problems (when paired with appropriate Jacobian settings).
- Industry standard for general-purpose ODE integration in Python scientific computing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors** in $k_2, k_3, k_4$: Students often forget the inner $h \cdot k_1$ inside $y_n + \frac{h}{2}k_1$. Loss: **up to 3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up local vs global error orders**: Local is one power higher than global for explicit methods. Loss: **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong weights in RK4**: Students may write $\frac{1}{4}(k_1 + k_2 + k_3 + k_4)$ instead of $\frac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to reduce higher-order ODE** before passing to `solve_ivp`. The function must accept and return **vectors**, not scalars. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 5. **Not tabulating intermediate values**: Examiners expect at least one full table of $(x_n, y_n, k_1, k_2, \dots)$. Loss: **1ŌĆō2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Solving ODEs Using Python":**
>
> - **Definition:** ODE of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x,y)$ with $y(x_0)=y_0$ is an IVP.
> - **Euler:** $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(x_n, y_n)$; global error $\mathcal{O}(h)$; cheapest, least accurate.
> - **Modified Euler (Heun):** $k_1, k_2$ with average; global error $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$.
> - **RK2 (Midpoint):** $k_2$ at midpoint dominates; same order as Heun.
> - **RK4:** four $k$ values with weights $\tfrac{1}{6}, \tfrac{2}{6}, \tfrac{2}{6}, \tfrac{1}{6}$; global error $\mathcal{O}(h^4)$; industry standard.
> - **Reduction of order:** second-order ODE $\to$ system of two first-order ODEs by introducing $u_1=y, u_2=y'$.
> - **Python library:** `scipy.integrate.solve_ivp(f, [t0, tn], y0, method='RK45', t_eval=...)`.
> - **Adaptive solvers** automatically vary $h$ to maintain user-specified tolerance `rtol` and `atol`.
> - **Big-O Notation:** $\mathcal{O}(h^p)$ means error behaves like a constant times $h^p$ as $h \to 0$.
> - **Stability:** Smaller $h$ improves accuracy but increases computational cost ŌĆö balance via adaptive methods.
> - **Compare to exact:** Whenever possible, compute the analytical solution and quote the error magnitude.
> - **Vector form for systems:** The function passed to `solve_ivp` must return a list/array of the same length as the initial condition vector.
